title: Foreword
summary: I know what you're thinking.
bodyMd:
# Foreword: Why Another Process Improvement Book?

I know what you're thinking.

The last thing the world needs is another book about process improvement. Your bookshelf -- or more likely, a forgotten folder on your company's shared drive -- already has at least one Lean manual, a Six Sigma workbook, maybe a Kaizen guide that someone brought back from a conference in 2017. They're good books. Some of them are great books. And most of them have been collecting dust since the week after the initiative launch meeting.

I've watched this happen more times than I can count.


## The Graveyard of Good Intentions

Here's how it usually goes. Someone in leadership reads an article, attends a seminar, or hires a consultant. They come back fired up. There's a kickoff meeting. There are new terms to learn. Maybe some colored belts get mentioned. People nod along, because the ideas genuinely make sense. Of course we should reduce waste. Of course we should find root causes instead of slapping band-aids on symptoms. Of course we should measure outcomes.

Then reality sets in.

The methodology requires a certification program. The tools require a statistician to operate. The language feels like it belongs in a manufacturing plant, not an accounting department or a pharmacy claims team. The consultant leaves. The champion gets pulled onto another project. The binder goes on the shelf. Six months later, the same problems are still there, and now people are cynical about the next improvement initiative before it even starts.

I have sat in rooms where someone literally said, "Oh great, another process improvement thing." Not sarcastically. Resignedly. They'd seen the movie before and they knew how it ended.

This is not a criticism of Lean, Six Sigma, Kaizen, Total Quality Management, or any of the other methodologies that have transformed industries. They work. The evidence is overwhelming. Toyota didn't become Toyota by accident. GE didn't save billions by guessing. These frameworks represent decades of rigorous thinking about how organizations get better.

The problem isn't the methodologies. The problem is the gap between the methodology and the people who are supposed to use it.


## The Gap

Let me paint you a picture from my own career.

I spent the better part of two decades in healthcare IT and operations, mostly in pharmacy benefits management. I was usually employee number ten or fifteen -- hired into small companies that were growing fast and needed someone to build the infrastructure before the wheels came off. If you've never worked in PBM, here's what you need to know: you're processing millions of pharmacy claims, the systems are often held together with duct tape and institutional knowledge, and when something goes wrong, a real person doesn't get their medication on time. The stakes aren't abstract.

I learned this the hard way. Early in my career, I spent year-end seasons working hundred-hour weeks, loading millions of records into AS400 systems that would choke if you looked at them wrong. The documentation was terrible. The processes were worse. I slept at the office more times than I'd like to admit. I never once celebrated New Year's Eve during those years because I was always elbow-deep in some production system, praying it would hold. Every year, the same fire drill. Every year, the same scramble.

But here's the thing -- every year got a little better. Not because someone handed us a playbook. Because we started paying attention. We started asking why the same things broke every December. We started building automation, writing things down, preparing in October for what used to ambush us in January. Year by year, the hundred-hour weeks became sixty-hour weeks became forty-hour weeks. Not because the work went away. Because we built systems to handle it.

That experience taught me something that became the foundation of everything in this book: the people closest to the work are the ones who can fix it. Not consultants. Not executives in a conference room. The accounting clerk who noticed that payment postings crashed twice a day. The pharmacy tech who kept seeing the same rejection code on member ID mismatches. The IT helpdesk analyst getting the same ticket every Monday. They knew where the problems lived. They just didn't have a structured way to do anything about it.

I tried the standard approaches. I sent people to Lean training. I brought in Six Sigma consultants. And I kept running into the same wall: the people closest to the problems didn't feel like the tools belonged to them. The language was a barrier. The statistical requirements were a barrier. The certification gatekeeping was a barrier. A pharmacy tech isn't going to use a methodology that requires a statistics degree. They need something they can pick up and use.

That's where PIPS came from.


## What PIPS Is

PIPS stands for Process Improvement and Problem Solving. It's a six-step structured methodology for identifying problems, analyzing their root causes, generating solutions, selecting the best one, implementing it, and evaluating the results.

If that sounds simple, good. It's supposed to.

PIPS has its roots in Xerox Corporation's Problem Solving Process, which was developed in the 1980s as part of their quality revolution. The intellectual lineage runs deep. W. Edwards Deming's thinking about variation and systems is in here. Kaoru Ishikawa's cause-and-effect analysis is in here. Alex Osborn's brainstorming principles are in here. Kurt Lewin's force field analysis is in here. William Ouchi's Theory Z approach to consensus decision-making is in here.

But PIPS isn't an academic exercise in paying homage to quality pioneers. It's a practitioner's framework that takes the best ideas from those traditions and packages them in a way that real teams in real organizations can actually use. Every tool, every template, every step has been adapted and tested in environments where people had claims to process, deadlines to meet, and no patience for theory that didn't translate into results.

The key difference -- and this is the thing I care about most -- is accessibility. PIPS is designed so that the same framework works whether you're an individual contributor trying to fix a recurring error in your daily workflow or a senior leader tackling an organization-wide strategic problem. The six steps are the same. The thinking discipline is the same. The tools scale up and down.

You don't need a certification to use PIPS. You don't need a consultant standing over your shoulder. You need a team, a problem, some data, and the willingness to follow a structured process instead of jumping straight to solutions. That's it.


## Who This Book Is For

This book is for the person who has ever sat in a meeting and thought, "We keep talking about this same problem. Why hasn't anything changed?"

It's for the manager who knows their team's error rate is too high but doesn't know how to systematically figure out why. It's for the director who just inherited a department full of broken processes and needs a starting point. It's for the HR leader who wants to build a culture of continuous improvement but isn't sure how to make it stick beyond the first quarter. It's for the IT manager who's tired of fighting the same fires every week.

It's also for the frontline worker who sees waste and inefficiency every day and has ideas about how to fix things but has never been given a structured way to bring those ideas forward. PIPS gives you that structure. More importantly, it gives you credibility. When you walk into a room and say "I've identified a problem, I've gathered data on it, here's what the root cause analysis shows, and here are three potential solutions I've evaluated against these criteria," people listen. That's not a gut feeling. That's a disciplined argument backed by evidence.

You don't need to be a quality professional to read this book. You don't need a statistics background. If you can count, calculate a percentage, and hold a conversation with your colleagues, you have everything you need.


## Why "The Never-Ending Quest"

I want to be upfront about the title, because some people hear "never-ending" and feel tired. I get it. If you're already drowning in work, the idea of one more thing that never ends sounds exhausting.

But here's what I've learned after years of doing this: the "never-ending" part is the whole point.

The organizations that fail at process improvement are the ones that treat it like a project. They launch it, they run it for a while, they declare victory or defeat, and they move on. The organization goes back to how it was. The problems creep back. And then in two years, someone proposes another initiative.

The organizations that succeed are the ones that treat continuous improvement as a permanent operating discipline. Not a project. Not an initiative. A way of thinking. A muscle that the organization builds and maintains, like fitness.

You don't stop going to the gym because you achieved your target weight. You don't stop maintaining your car because it passed inspection this year. And you don't stop improving your processes because you hit your target metric last quarter. There's always a next problem. There's always a higher standard. There's always a better way.

Once you accept that, it's actually freeing. You never have to be perfect. You just have to keep getting better. And now you have a system for doing exactly that.


## What This Book Is Not

I should tell you what you won't find here.

You won't find a comprehensive survey of every quality methodology ever developed. This isn't a textbook, and I'm not a professor. If you want the complete intellectual history of the quality movement, there are excellent books for that. I'll point you to some in the back matter.

You won't find statistical process control charts or regression analysis. PIPS uses data, but it doesn't require advanced statistics. If your problem calls for that kind of analysis, great -- bring in a statistician. But most problems in most organizations don't need a statistician. They need people who are willing to count things, measure things, and be honest about what the numbers are telling them.

You won't find magic. PIPS doesn't work because it's clever. It works because it imposes discipline on a process -- problem solving -- that most organizations do badly. It forces you to slow down before you speed up. It forces you to define the problem before you solve it. It forces you to look at data before you form opinions. It forces you to generate multiple solutions before you pick one. None of that is magic. It's just rigor. And rigor, applied consistently over time, produces results.

You also won't find perfection. I'll share stories throughout this book of PIPS working exactly as designed, and I'll also share stories of it falling flat. I'll tell you about the times teams got stuck, the times leadership didn't follow through, the times we picked the wrong solution and had to go back. Honest failures teach more than curated successes, and I'd rather you trust what I'm telling you than think I'm selling you something.


## How We Got Here

I've spent my career being hired into companies during their growth phase -- usually as one of the first ten or twenty employees -- and then helping build the infrastructure that lets them scale. I've watched companies go from a handful of people to hundreds, from scrappy startups to Inc. 5000 honorees. And I've watched the same pattern every time: what works at ten people breaks at fifty, and what works at fifty breaks at two hundred. Departments form. Silos emerge. Gaps develop. The things that made the company nimble and fast start to calcify into "the way we do things."

PIPS grew out of that experience. I borrowed from every source I could find -- Xerox's Problem Solving Process, Lean, Six Sigma, Kaizen -- adapted what worked, discarded what didn't, and tested everything in environments where failure had real consequences. The framework evolved through use. It got simpler over time, not more complex, because every time I added a step or a tool, I'd watch people in the field and ask myself: "Would an accounting clerk use this? Would a pharmacy tech use this? Would a help desk analyst use this?" If the answer was no, it didn't survive.

The result is a methodology that has been used by teams across departments and disciplines -- IT, operations, clinical, finance, HR, marketing -- to solve problems ranging from "our payment postings crash twice a day" to "our onboarding process takes three times longer than it should" to "we're losing market share and we don't know why."

It works. Not because I say so. Because the data says so. And data over opinions is the first principle of everything you're about to read.

Let's get started.

---

*Marc Albers*
*March 2026*

title: Introduction
summary: Let me tell you about a team I'll never forget.
bodyMd:
# Introduction: A System That Belongs to Your Organization

Let me tell you about a team I'll never forget.

It was a pharmacy claims processing team, and they were in trouble. Their first-pass error-free rate -- the percentage of claims that went through the system correctly the first time, without requiring manual rework -- was sitting at 68%. That means roughly one out of every three claims had something wrong with it. Every error meant someone had to stop what they were doing, pull the claim, figure out what went wrong, fix it, and resubmit it. Multiply that by thousands of claims per day, and you're looking at an enormous amount of wasted time, frustrated staff, and delayed outcomes for the members who were waiting on their medications.

Everyone knew it was a problem. It had been a problem for months. And everyone had a theory about why.

The most popular theory, by far, was "carelessness." People were making mistakes because they weren't paying attention. They were rushing. They didn't care enough. If you've spent any time in operations, you've heard this diagnosis before. It's the default explanation for almost every quality problem, and it's almost always wrong.

But nobody had data. They had opinions. Strong ones.

So we put PIPS to work. We formed a team -- not outside consultants, not quality experts, but the people who actually processed claims every day, along with their supervisor and a representative from the training department. We walked through the six steps.

In Step 1, we got specific about the problem. Not "quality is bad" -- that's too vague to solve. The As-Is statement was precise: "68% of pharmacy claims are processed error-free on first submission." The Desired State was ambitious but realistic: "90% of pharmacy claims processed error-free on first submission within six months." The Gap was clear: 22 percentage points of improvement needed.

In Step 2, we analyzed the data. And this is where it got interesting. Instead of accepting the "carelessness" hypothesis, we looked at the actual errors. We categorized them. We counted them. We built cause-and-effect diagrams. We looked at which types of errors occurred most frequently, at what time of day, by which staff members, and on which types of claims.

What the data revealed was not carelessness. It was two things.

First, incorrect member ID entry. A significant percentage of errors came from member IDs being entered wrong -- transposed digits, wrong suffix, outdated ID numbers. When we dug deeper, we found that the member ID field accepted input without real-time validation, so there was no feedback telling the processor they'd made an error until the claim was rejected downstream. It wasn't that people didn't care. It was that the system gave them no way to catch mistakes in the moment.

Second, insufficient onboarding for new staff. When we broke the error data down by employee tenure, there was a clear pattern: processors in their first 90 days had error rates roughly three times higher than experienced staff. The onboarding program covered the basics of the system but didn't provide enough practice with the edge cases -- the unusual claim types, the member plans with non-standard ID formats, the scenarios where manual overrides were needed. New hires were being put on the production floor before they were ready, and the error rate reflected it.

Neither of those root causes was "carelessness." One was a system design issue. The other was a training issue. Both were fixable.

In Steps 3 and 4, the team generated potential solutions and evaluated them. For the member ID problem, they proposed real-time validation logic and a visual confirmation step. For the onboarding gap, they designed an extended training track with simulated claims and a mentorship period before new hires handled live claims independently. They scored each solution against criteria -- cost, time to implement, degree of control, expected impact -- and selected the ones that scored highest.

In Step 5, they implemented. The IT team built the validation feature. The training department redesigned the onboarding curriculum. Both changes were rolled out over about eight weeks.

In Step 6, they evaluated. Within six months, the first-pass error-free rate went from 68% to 94%.

Let me say that again, because it matters. Ninety-four percent. From a team that everyone assumed was just being careless. The "careless" people were still the same people. The system around them had changed.

That experience crystallized something for me. The problems that organizations struggle with are rarely caused by the things people assume. And the solutions that actually work are rarely the ones that seem obvious. What you need, between the assumption and the solution, is a structured process for getting to the truth. That's what PIPS is.


## What Makes PIPS Different

There's no shortage of problem-solving methodologies in the world. So why does PIPS deserve your attention?

It comes down to three things.

**First, PIPS is a common language, not a specialized one.** Most quality methodologies were developed for specific contexts -- manufacturing floors, engineering teams, statistical analysis labs. They carry the vocabulary of those origins. PIPS was developed for mixed-discipline teams in office environments. The language is plain. The concepts are intuitive. An accounting clerk and a VP of Operations can sit in the same room, use the same framework, and understand each other. That matters more than people think. Half the battle in organizational problem solving is just getting everyone to talk about the same thing in the same way.

**Second, PIPS has a built-in rhythm of expansion and contraction.** At every step, the process follows the same pattern: first you expand -- you generate lots of options, lots of ideas, lots of data. Then you contract -- you narrow down, evaluate, select, decide. This is the heartbeat of PIPS. It's what prevents teams from jumping to the first idea that sounds good (contraction without expansion) and from endlessly debating without ever deciding anything (expansion without contraction). Most problems in group problem solving come from skipping one half of this rhythm. PIPS doesn't let you skip it.

**Third, PIPS is designed to be a permanent operating system, not a one-time project.** This is the big one. PIPS isn't something you "do" and then you're done. It's something your organization *becomes*. When it works -- when it really takes hold -- people stop thinking of it as a special process they use for special problems. It just becomes the way problems get solved. The way decisions get made. The way things get better.


## The Three Things Every Organization Needs

After years of working with teams across departments -- IT, operations, finance, clinical, HR, marketing -- I've concluded that every organization needs exactly three things to get consistently better at solving problems and improving processes.

### A Common Language for Problems

This sounds basic, but it's remarkable how many organizations lack it. In one department, a "problem" is a crisis that requires immediate action. In another, it's a metric that's trending in the wrong direction. In a third, it's a feeling that something isn't right but nobody can articulate exactly what.

PIPS gives you a common vocabulary. A problem is the gap between where you are (the As-Is state) and where you want to be (the Desired State). That's it. It's observable. It's measurable. It's free of assumptions about causes or solutions. And it's the same definition whether you're talking about a payment posting failure, an employee retention issue, or a customer satisfaction decline.

When everyone in the organization uses the same definition of a problem, the same format for problem statements, and the same criteria for deciding which problems are worth tackling, something remarkable happens. Communication gets clearer. Meetings get shorter. People stop talking past each other.

### A Structured Way to Solve Them

The second thing is a repeatable process. Not a different process for every department. Not a process that only trained specialists can use. One process that everyone understands and that works for problems of different sizes and types.

PIPS provides six steps. They are always the same six steps, in the same order, with the same discipline. Whether the problem is small (a recurring data entry error) or large (a fundamental breakdown in a core business process), the steps are the same. The depth of effort changes -- a small problem might take a team through all six steps in a few sessions, while a large one might take months -- but the structure doesn't change.

I'll give you a brief overview of the six steps here, just enough to orient you. The detailed treatment comes in Part II.

**Step 1: Identify and Select the Problem.** This is where you define what you want to change. You develop a clear problem statement -- the As-Is state, the Desired State, and the Gap between them. If there are multiple problem candidates, you use structured tools to evaluate and select the one the team will focus on. The key question: *What do we want to change?*

**Step 2: Analyze the Problem.** This is where you figure out what's really going on. You collect data. You build cause-and-effect diagrams. You use force field analysis to understand the driving and restraining forces. You keep asking "why" until you get to root causes, not symptoms. The key question: *What's preventing us from reaching our desired state?*

**Step 3: Generate Potential Solutions.** This is where creativity lives. You brainstorm. You brainwrite. You generate as many potential solutions as possible without judging them. Quantity over quality at this stage. You want the widest possible set of options before you start narrowing. The key question: *How could we make the change?*

**Step 4: Select and Plan the Solution.** This is where you get rigorous. You evaluate the candidate solutions against defined criteria -- cost, feasibility, time, expected impact, risk. You use weighted scoring to make the decision as objective as possible. Then you build an implementation plan: who does what, by when, with what resources. The key question: *What's the best way to do it?*

**Step 5: Implement the Solution.** This is where you execute the plan. You track progress against milestones. You monitor for unintended consequences. You make adjustments as needed. This step is pure execution -- there's no expansion phase, just disciplined follow-through. The key question: *Are we following the plan?*

**Step 6: Evaluate the Solution.** This is where you close the loop. You compare results to your original Desired State. Did you hit the target? What worked? What didn't? What did you learn? And most importantly: what's the next problem? Because there's always a next problem. The key question: *How well did it work?*

Six steps. Six questions. One cycle that repeats forever.

### A Feedback Loop That Never Stops

The third thing -- and this is the one most organizations miss -- is the feedback loop. Steps 1 through 5 are a project. Step 6 is where the project becomes a system. When you evaluate your results and ask "what's next?", you're not closing a chapter. You're starting the next one.

This is why the PIPS diagram is a circle, not a line. Step 6 feeds back into Step 1. The evaluation of one problem reveals the next problem. The lessons learned from one cycle make the next cycle better. Over time, the organization doesn't just solve individual problems -- it gets better at *the process of solving problems*.

That's the muscle I keep talking about. It's organizational fitness. And like physical fitness, it compounds. A team that has been through three PIPS cycles is dramatically better than a team on its first. They're faster at defining problems. They're more disciplined about gathering data. They're more creative in generating solutions. They're more rigorous in evaluating them. They've built the muscle.


## How to Read This Book

This book is organized into four parts, and you don't have to read them in order. In fact, I'd encourage you not to.

**Part I: The Philosophy** covers why PIPS exists, what makes it different from other methodologies, and the three core principles that underpin everything: Data Over Opinions, Expand Then Contract, and Close the Loop. If you're the kind of person who needs to understand the "why" before the "how," start here. If you're more of a "just show me what to do" person, you can skip ahead and come back to Part I later.

**Part II: The Six Steps** is the engine. Each step gets its own chapter with detailed guidance, tools, templates, worked examples, common pitfalls, and facilitation tips. This is the reference section of the book. You don't need to read it cover to cover. Once you're working a real problem with a real team, turn to the chapter for whatever step you're on and use it as a guide. I've written each chapter to stand on its own, so you can open to Step 4 without having just read Steps 1 through 3.

**Part III: PIPS in the Real World** is about making it work in your specific context. How do you apply PIPS in different departments? How do you scale it for different problem sizes? How do you build a culture that sustains it? How do you facilitate sessions that people actually want to attend? This is the section for after you've been through a cycle or two and you're ready to go bigger.

**Part IV: The Never-Ending Quest** is about the long game. What do you do when PIPS fails? What does a mature PIPS organization look like? How do you keep improving the improvement process itself? Read this when you're ready to think about PIPS not as something your organization does, but as something your organization is.

The appendices contain every tool, template, and quick reference card you'll need. Print them out. Stick them on the wall. Mark them up with a pen. They're not precious. They're tools.


## The Promise

I'm going to make you a promise, and I'm going to be specific about it, because vague promises are worthless.

If you commit to using PIPS -- not as a one-time experiment, not as a check-the-box compliance exercise, but as a genuine operating discipline -- your organization will get measurably better at solving problems and improving processes.

Not overnight. PIPS is not a shortcut. The first time you run a team through the six steps, it will feel slow. People will want to jump ahead. The discipline of defining the problem before solving it will feel tedious. The data collection in Step 2 will feel like busywork. The brainstorming in Step 3 will feel chaotic. The evaluation criteria in Step 4 will feel bureaucratic.

Do it anyway.

By the second cycle, the team will be faster. By the third, they'll start applying the thinking instinctively -- even outside of formal PIPS sessions. By the fourth or fifth, you'll notice something different about the way the organization handles problems. People will stop saying "we've always done it this way." They'll start saying "what does the data tell us?" They'll stop pointing fingers and start drawing fishbone diagrams. They'll stop accepting broken processes as permanent fixtures and start seeing them as opportunities.

That transformation doesn't come from a single initiative. It doesn't come from a training session or a consultant engagement or a new piece of software. It comes from building the muscle. One problem at a time. One cycle at a time. With a framework that everybody in the organization can use, understand, and own.

That's the promise. Not perfection -- progress. Measurable, sustainable progress that compounds over time.

It starts with a single question: *What do we want to change?*


## A Note on the Examples in This Book

Throughout this book, I'll use examples drawn from real experiences in healthcare IT, pharmacy claims processing, and corporate operations -- industries where I spent years building teams from scratch and scaling companies from a handful of employees to hundreds. Names, specifics, and identifying details have been changed, and in some cases I've created composite examples that combine elements from multiple real situations to illustrate a point more clearly. But the patterns are real. Every worked example reflects dynamics that actually played out in real teams working on real problems.

You'll also notice that I occasionally reference simple, everyday problems -- like finding a parking spot or fixing a meeting that runs too long -- alongside the bigger case studies. These small examples are deliberate. They show that PIPS scales all the way down to the frustrations you encounter before your first cup of coffee. Once you see how the six-step thinking applies to something as simple as a parking problem, you'll start seeing opportunities to apply it everywhere.

Where I share failures -- and I will -- they're also real. I've disguised them enough to protect people, but the lessons are intact. I believe you learn more from watching something go wrong than from reading another polished success story. If this book is honest about what can go wrong, you'll be better prepared when it happens to you. And it will happen to you. That's not a warning -- it's just how it goes when you're building something new.


## Before You Turn the Page

One more thing.

PIPS is not my invention. It's an adaptation. Its intellectual roots go back decades, through Xerox's Problem Solving Process, through the quality revolution, through the work of brilliant thinkers who devoted their careers to understanding how organizations get better. I stand on the shoulders of Deming, Ishikawa, Osborn, Lewin, Ouchi, and many others. What I've done is take those ideas, strip away the parts that created barriers to adoption, add the things I learned from watching real teams in real organizations, and package it in a way that works for the modern workplace.

I don't claim this is the only way. I claim it's a way that works, that's accessible to everyone in your organization, and that gets better the more you use it. If you find a better way, use that instead. And then tell me about it, because I'd like to learn from you. That's how continuous improvement works.

The quest never ends. Let's begin.

---

*Proceed to Part I: The Philosophy, or jump to Part II: The Six Steps if you're ready to get to work.*

title: What Makes PIPS Different
summary: There's no shortage of problem-solving methodologies in the world. So why does PIPS deserve your attention?
bodyMd:
There's no shortage of problem-solving methodologies in the world. So why does PIPS deserve your attention?

It comes down to three things.

**First, PIPS is a common language, not a specialized one.** Most quality methodologies were developed for specific contexts -- manufacturing floors, engineering teams, statistical analysis labs. They carry the vocabulary of those origins. PIPS was developed for mixed-discipline teams in office environments. The language is plain. The concepts are intuitive. An accounting clerk and a VP of Operations can sit in the same room, use the same framework, and understand each other. That matters more than people think. Half the battle in organizational problem solving is just getting everyone to talk about the same thing in the same way.

**Second, PIPS has a built-in rhythm of expansion and contraction.** At every step, the process follows the same pattern: first you expand -- you generate lots of options, lots of ideas, lots of data. Then you contract -- you narrow down, evaluate, select, decide. This is the heartbeat of PIPS. It's what prevents teams from jumping to the first idea that sounds good (contraction without expansion) and from endlessly debating without ever deciding anything (expansion without contraction). Most problems in group problem solving come from skipping one half of this rhythm. PIPS doesn't let you skip it.

**Third, PIPS is designed to be a permanent operating system, not a one-time project.** This is the big one. PIPS isn't something you "do" and then you're done. It's something your organization *becomes*. When it works -- when it really takes hold -- people stop thinking of it as a special process they use for special problems. It just becomes the way problems get solved. The way decisions get made. The way things get better.

title: The Three Things Every Organization Needs
summary: After years of working with teams across departments -- IT, operations, finance, clinical, HR, marketing -- I've concluded that every organization needs exactly three things to get consistently better...
bodyMd:
After years of working with teams across departments -- IT, operations, finance, clinical, HR, marketing -- I've concluded that every organization needs exactly three things to get consistently better at solving problems and improving processes.

### A Common Language for Problems

This sounds basic, but it's remarkable how many organizations lack it. In one department, a "problem" is a crisis that requires immediate action. In another, it's a metric that's trending in the wrong direction. In a third, it's a feeling that something isn't right but nobody can articulate exactly what.

PIPS gives you a common vocabulary. A problem is the gap between where you are (the As-Is state) and where you want to be (the Desired State). That's it. It's observable. It's measurable. It's free of assumptions about causes or solutions. And it's the same definition whether you're talking about a payment posting failure, an employee retention issue, or a customer satisfaction decline.

When everyone in the organization uses the same definition of a problem, the same format for problem statements, and the same criteria for deciding which problems are worth tackling, something remarkable happens. Communication gets clearer. Meetings get shorter. People stop talking past each other.

### A Structured Way to Solve Them

The second thing is a repeatable process. Not a different process for every department. Not a process that only trained specialists can use. One process that everyone understands and that works for problems of different sizes and types.

PIPS provides six steps. They are always the same six steps, in the same order, with the same discipline. Whether the problem is small (a recurring data entry error) or large (a fundamental breakdown in a core business process), the steps are the same. The depth of effort changes -- a small problem might take a team through all six steps in a few sessions, while a large one might take months -- but the structure doesn't change.

I'll give you a brief overview of the six steps here, just enough to orient you. The detailed treatment comes in Part II.

**Step 1: Identify and Select the Problem.** This is where you define what you want to change. You develop a clear problem statement -- the As-Is state, the Desired State, and the Gap between them. If there are multiple problem candidates, you use structured tools to evaluate and select the one the team will focus on. The key question: *What do we want to change?*

**Step 2: Analyze the Problem.** This is where you figure out what's really going on. You collect data. You build cause-and-effect diagrams. You use force field analysis to understand the driving and restraining forces. You keep asking "why" until you get to root causes, not symptoms. The key question: *What's preventing us from reaching our desired state?*

**Step 3: Generate Potential Solutions.** This is where creativity lives. You brainstorm. You brainwrite. You generate as many potential solutions as possible without judging them. Quantity over quality at this stage. You want the widest possible set of options before you start narrowing. The key question: *How could we make the change?*

**Step 4: Select and Plan the Solution.** This is where you get rigorous. You evaluate the candidate solutions against defined criteria -- cost, feasibility, time, expected impact, risk. You use weighted scoring to make the decision as objective as possible. Then you build an implementation plan: who does what, by when, with what resources. The key question: *What's the best way to do it?*

**Step 5: Implement the Solution.** This is where you execute the plan. You track progress against milestones. You monitor for unintended consequences. You make adjustments as needed. This step is pure execution -- there's no expansion phase, just disciplined follow-through. The key question: *Are we following the plan?*

**Step 6: Evaluate the Solution.** This is where you close the loop. You compare results to your original Desired State. Did you hit the target? What worked? What didn't? What did you learn? And most importantly: what's the next problem? Because there's always a next problem. The key question: *How well did it work?*

Six steps. Six questions. One cycle that repeats forever.

### A Feedback Loop That Never Stops

The third thing -- and this is the one most organizations miss -- is the feedback loop. Steps 1 through 5 are a project. Step 6 is where the project becomes a system. When you evaluate your results and ask "what's next?", you're not closing a chapter. You're starting the next one.

This is why the PIPS diagram is a circle, not a line. Step 6 feeds back into Step 1. The evaluation of one problem reveals the next problem. The lessons learned from one cycle make the next cycle better. Over time, the organization doesn't just solve individual problems -- it gets better at *the process of solving problems*.

That's the muscle I keep talking about. It's organizational fitness. And like physical fitness, it compounds. A team that has been through three PIPS cycles is dramatically better than a team on its first. They're faster at defining problems. They're more disciplined about gathering data. They're more creative in generating solutions. They're more rigorous in evaluating them. They've built the muscle.

title: The Problem With Problems
summary: I want to start with a confession: I spent the first several years of my career solving the wrong problems.
bodyMd:
# Chapter 1: The Problem with Problems

---

I want to start with a confession: I spent the first several years of my career solving the wrong problems.

Not because I wasn't smart enough. Not because I wasn't working hard enough. I was working *too* hard, actually. That was part of the problem. I was so busy running from one emergency to the next that I never stopped to ask a very basic question: *Why do we keep having the same emergencies?*

If you've spent any time in an organization of more than about ten people, you know exactly what I'm talking about. Every Monday brings a fresh batch of crises. Every quarter-end is the same scramble. Every year, somebody says "we really need to fix that" about the same thing somebody said "we really need to fix that" about last year. And the year before that.

Organizations are, by and large, terrible at solving problems. Not because the people in them are incapable. The people are usually brilliant, dedicated, and genuinely want things to be better. The organizations are terrible at it because they've never built a *system* for getting better at it. They rely on heroics instead of habits. They reward firefighters instead of fire prevention.

This chapter is about understanding why. Because before we can build something better, we need to be honest about what's broken.

---

## The Firefighting Trap

Here's a story you'll recognize.

It's 4:30 PM on a Thursday. You're about to leave for the day when your phone buzzes. There's been a system outage. Claims aren't processing. The client is furious. You cancel your evening plans, pull together a team, work until midnight, find the issue, patch it, get everything running again. The client sends a thank-you email. Your boss copies leadership on a message praising your team's quick response. Someone uses the word "heroic."

You feel great. And you should--you solved a real problem under real pressure.

But here's the question nobody asks: Why did the system go down in the first place? Was it the same type of failure that happened three months ago? Is there a pattern? Could it have been prevented?

Nobody asks because there's already a new fire to fight.

This is the firefighting trap, and it is absolutely everywhere. I've seen it in healthcare IT, in hospital operations, in HR departments, in finance teams, in software companies of every size. The pattern is always the same:

1. A problem erupts.
2. Smart people drop everything to fix it.
3. They fix it (or at least make it stop hurting).
4. Everyone gets praised for the response.
5. Nobody investigates the root cause.
6. The same problem erupts again weeks or months later.
7. Return to Step 1.

The trap is self-reinforcing because of how organizations hand out rewards. Think about who gets recognized at your company. Is it the person who quietly prevented a problem from ever occurring? Or is it the person who swooped in at 11 PM to save the day when the problem exploded?

I already know the answer. We all do.

I once worked with an operations team that had a "hero board" in their break room. Literally a whiteboard where they celebrated people who went above and beyond during crises. It was a genuinely well-intentioned morale booster. But think about what it was actually incentivizing. The message, whether anyone intended it or not, was: *crises are where you get noticed.*

Nobody puts you on the hero board for "quietly improved a process so that the thing that used to break every month hasn't broken in six months." There's no drama in prevention. There's no adrenaline in stability. But stability is where the real value lives.

The firefighting trap costs organizations enormous amounts of money, but the costs are mostly invisible. You don't see the invoice for "time spent on problems that could have been prevented." It doesn't show up as a line item in the budget. But it's there, buried in overtime, in turnover, in missed deadlines, in the slow erosion of morale that comes from working in an environment where nothing ever really gets fixed.

I'm not saying crisis response doesn't matter. Of course it does. When the building is on fire, you need firefighters. But if the building catches fire every month, at some point you need to stop congratulating the firefighters and start asking why the building keeps catching fire.

---

## The Solution-First Disease

If the firefighting trap is the most common problem-solving failure, the solution-first disease is the most dangerous. Because it feels productive. It feels like progress. You're *doing something*. You're being proactive. You're taking action.

You're also, very possibly, wasting a tremendous amount of time and money.

Here's how it works. Someone in a meeting says: "We need a new CRM."

Everyone nods. A committee is formed. Vendors are evaluated. Demos are scheduled. Six months and a quarter million dollars later, you have a shiny new CRM system. And the sales team still spends 40% of their time on data entry instead of selling.

Because "we need a new CRM" was never a problem statement. It was a solution masquerading as a problem. The actual problem--if anyone had bothered to define it--was something like: "Sales representatives spend 40% of their working hours on administrative data entry, reducing customer-facing selling time by approximately 20 hours per rep per week."

That's a problem statement. It describes a current state, it's specific, and most importantly, it doesn't presuppose the solution. Maybe a new CRM is the answer. Maybe it's automation within the existing CRM. Maybe it's hiring admin support. Maybe it's eliminating half the fields that nobody looks at anyway. You don't know until you actually analyze the problem. But the moment someone said "we need a new CRM," all those other possibilities disappeared.

I see this constantly. It's not limited to technology decisions. It shows up everywhere:

**HR:** "We need to increase salaries to reduce turnover."
Really? Have you actually talked to the people who left? Have you looked at the data? I worked with a healthcare organization where everyone--leadership, managers, HR--was convinced that compensation was driving their 35% nursing turnover rate. They were ready to spend millions on across-the-board raises. Then someone actually did exit interviews. Real ones, not the checkbox forms people fill out on their last day. Structured conversations with people who had already left and had no reason to hold back.

The number one reason wasn't pay. It was the first 90 days. New nurses felt abandoned after orientation ended. They didn't know who to ask for help. They felt incompetent and unsupported. The ones who left early cited a lack of mentorship and a chaotic onboarding process. The organization didn't have a compensation problem. It had an onboarding problem. And an onboarding fix costs a fraction of what across-the-board raises cost. (I'll walk through a more detailed version of how PIPS handles this kind of HR challenge in Chapter 10.)

**IT:** "We need to upgrade our ticketing system."
Maybe. Or maybe the current system is fine and the real issue is that 60% of your tickets are repeat issues that could be eliminated with better documentation or upstream fixes. A fancier system for managing tickets you shouldn't be getting in the first place is just a more expensive way to be inefficient.

**Operations:** "We need to hire more staff for quarter-end."
Or maybe the quarter-end crunch happens because work that should have been done throughout the quarter gets deferred until the last two weeks. More staff doesn't fix a workflow problem. It just means more people participating in the same broken process.

The solution-first disease is seductive because solutions are concrete. "Buy this software." "Hire these people." "Change this policy." They're action items. They feel decisive. Problems, by contrast, are messy. They require investigation. They require admitting that you don't fully understand what's going on. In most organizational cultures, saying "I think we need to study this more before we act" is career suicide. The person who says "let's just do X" gets praised for being action-oriented.

But action without understanding is just motion. Motion isn't progress.

---

## The Opinion Economy

Every organization runs on some mix of data and opinions. In most organizations, the mix is about 90% opinions and 10% data, and most of that 10% is cherry-picked to support someone's pre-existing opinion.

I call this the opinion economy, and it's the default operating mode of almost every meeting you've ever sat in.

Here's what it looks like. A decision needs to be made. People sit around a table. Everyone shares their perspective, which is usually based on their personal experience, their gut feeling, or something they half-remember from a conference three years ago. The person with the strongest personality or the highest title wins. The decision gets made. Everyone moves on.

Sometimes the loud person or the senior person is right. Experience and intuition are real things and they have real value. But "sometimes being right" isn't a system. It's a coin flip with better-than-average odds.

There's a quote attributed to Grace Hopper, the pioneering computer scientist and Navy rear admiral, that I come back to constantly in this work:

> "One accurate measurement is worth a thousand expert opinions."

One accurate measurement. Not a dashboard with 400 metrics. Not a six-month data science project. One accurate measurement of the thing that actually matters.

I was working with a team that managed patient scheduling for a large clinic network. They were trying to figure out why patient no-show rates were climbing. The meeting went exactly how you'd expect. The office managers said patients were irresponsible. The physicians said the reminder system was inadequate. The IT team said the scheduling software was outdated. Finance said they needed stricter cancellation policies.

Everyone had a theory. Nobody had data.

So we got the data. We pulled the actual no-show records for the past twelve months and broke them down by day of week, time of day, provider, clinic location, and appointment type. The pattern was immediate and obvious: no-show rates were dramatically higher for appointments booked more than three weeks in advance. The longer the lead time between booking and appointment, the more likely the patient was to no-show. The effect was so strong that it dwarfed every other variable.

The solution wasn't a better reminder system or stricter policies or new software. It was reducing the average scheduling window. That's it. A process change that cost essentially nothing.

But here's the thing: nobody in that room would have proposed that solution based on opinions alone. It was invisible to intuition. Only the data made it visible.

I want to be clear about something because I think this gets misunderstood. Saying "data over opinions" does not mean that experience and intuition are worthless. Far from it. Experience is incredibly valuable for telling you *where to look*. The office managers' intuition that something was off with patient behavior was correct. They just didn't have the right explanation for it. Intuition is your compass. Data is your map. You need both. But when the compass and the map disagree, trust the map.

The opinion economy persists because gathering data takes effort, and effort takes time, and time is the thing everyone says they don't have. It's faster to go with your gut. It's faster to defer to the boss. And sometimes fast is what you need. But for the problems that actually matter--the ones that keep coming back, the ones that cost real money, the ones that affect real people--"fast" isn't fast if it leads to the wrong answer.

---

## The "Solved It" Illusion

This one might be the most painful because it strikes at the moment you feel best.

You identified a problem. You analyzed it. You implemented a solution. It worked. People are happy. You move on to the next thing.

Six months later, someone mentions that the problem is back. Not dramatically--it crept back in slowly, like water finding cracks. The old habits returned. The workaround became the norm again. The new process got "adapted" until it was functionally identical to the old process.

What happened?

Nobody checked.

That's it. Nobody went back to verify that the solution was still working. Nobody measured the results after the initial implementation glow faded. Nobody closed the loop.

This is astonishingly common. I would estimate that fewer than 10% of the improvement initiatives I've seen in my career included a genuine follow-up evaluation more than 90 days after implementation. Most of them had some kind of "check-in" in the first couple of weeks--usually just asking people "how's the new process going?" which invariably produces "fine" whether it's fine or not. But a real evaluation? With data? Comparing current state to the metrics you measured before you started? Almost nobody does this.

And so the illusion persists. The project tracker says "complete." The improvement is listed in the annual report. But the actual problem is right back where it started.

I ran into this with a client that had implemented a new process for managing IT change requests. The old process was chaos--changes would get made without proper review, things would break, fingers would get pointed. The new process added structure: review boards, documentation requirements, approval workflows. It worked beautifully for about four months.

Then I visited them a year later and the change request process was, to put it diplomatically, no longer being followed as designed. The review board meetings had become "optional." The documentation was being backfilled after changes were already made. The approval workflow had an unofficial fast-track that bypassed most of the controls.

When I asked what happened, the answer was instructive: "Oh, we completed that project. It was a big success." They genuinely believed this. Because at the time they launched it, it was a success. They just never went back to check if it was *still* a success.

The solved-it illusion is a natural consequence of how we structure work. Projects have start dates and end dates. Improvements are treated as projects. Therefore improvements have end dates. But a process doesn't end. The environment doesn't stop changing. The people doing the work don't stop adapting (and adapting isn't always improving). If you're not continuously monitoring, you're not improving. You're just hoping.

---

## The Amnesia Problem

Maybe the most expensive failure mode of all is organizational amnesia: the pattern where companies solve the same problems over and over because they never captured and shared what they learned.

Every organization has tribal knowledge--things that experienced people know but that aren't written down anywhere. When those people leave (and they always eventually leave), the knowledge walks out the door with them. The next person to encounter the problem starts from scratch.

I've seen this play out in ways that are almost comical, if they weren't so expensive.

A healthcare IT company I worked with spent three months troubleshooting a data migration issue for a client implementation. The team documented their findings in an email thread. Two years later, a different team hit the exact same issue with a different client. They spent two and a half months troubleshooting it. Nobody thought to search for the previous team's work because it was buried in someone's email. Even if they had searched, the original team lead had left the company, and her email was deactivated.

Three months of expensive expert labor, essentially duplicated, because there was no system for capturing what was learned.

This happens at every scale. It happens with the IT helpdesk that solves the same printer configuration issue thirty times because no one writes a knowledge base article. It happens with the HR team that reinvents the interview process every time they hire for a new role because no one standardized what worked last time. It happens with the operations team that scrambles through the same quarter-end closing process every three months because the checklist is in someone's head instead of in a shared document.

The amnesia problem isn't a people problem. It's a systems problem. People don't deliberately withhold knowledge (usually). They just don't have a structure that makes capturing and sharing it a natural part of the work. Without that structure, knowledge becomes personal, scattered, and ephemeral.

This is one of the things that motivated me to build PIPS into a system rather than just a method. A method tells you how to solve a problem. A system tells you how to solve a problem *and* how to remember that you solved it *and* how to share what you learned *and* how to check later whether the solution is still working. The method is the engine. The system is the whole car.

---

## The Same Play, Different Departments

One of the things that struck me early in my career is how universal these patterns are. The specific symptoms look different from department to department, but the underlying dysfunction is identical.

**HR** has been trying to fix turnover for three years. Every year, leadership asks why turnover is high. Every year, HR says it's compensation. Every year, they do a market analysis and adjust a few salary bands. Every year, turnover stays roughly the same. Nobody has done a rigorous analysis of *why* people actually leave because everyone already "knows" the answer. The opinion economy in action.

**IT** gets 200 helpdesk tickets a month. About 40% of them are the same 15 issues. Every ticket gets resolved individually. Nobody steps back to ask: what if we eliminated these 15 issues at the source? Because resolving tickets is the job. The job is measured by tickets closed, not by tickets prevented. The firefighting trap in action.

**Operations** runs the same fire drill every quarter-end. The last two weeks of every quarter are all-hands-on-deck chaos. Reports that should take hours take days. Data that should be clean requires manual correction. Everyone works late. Then the quarter closes, everyone exhales, and nobody does a lessons-learned review because they're too exhausted and the next quarter is already starting. The solved-it illusion in action--except in this case, nobody even pretends it was solved. It's just accepted as "how things are."

**Finance** implemented a new expense reporting process eighteen months ago. It was great for about four months. Now people are back to submitting expenses late, in the wrong format, with missing receipts. Finance blames the employees for not following the process. Nobody has asked whether the process is actually usable or whether it was designed around what Finance needed rather than how employees actually work. The amnesia problem meets the solved-it illusion.

**Product Development** keeps shipping features that customers don't use. They have a backlog a mile long, mostly populated by feature requests from the loudest customers and the most persuasive internal stakeholders. They don't measure feature adoption after launch. They don't go back and ask: did this feature actually solve the problem it was supposed to solve? The opinion economy meets the solved-it illusion.

I could keep going, but you see the pattern. Every department, every function, every industry. The failure modes are the same because the underlying cause is the same: there is no system.

People are doing their best within the systems they're given. Give them a better system and they produce better results. I've seen it happen too many times to doubt it.

---

## So What Do We Do About It?

None of this is meant to be depressing. It's meant to be clarifying. You can't fix what you don't understand, and you can't understand what you won't look at honestly.

The good news is that every one of these patterns is fixable. Not with a magic wand or a consultant or a reorg. With a system. A simple, practical system that anyone in your organization can learn and use.

That's what the rest of this book is about. In the next chapter, I'll tell you exactly what PIPS is -- and just as importantly, what it isn't. I don't want PIPS to become another binder on a shelf. I want it to become how your organization thinks.

---

## What You Can Do Monday Morning

1. **Pick one recurring problem.** Not the biggest one. Just one that keeps coming back. Write it down as a simple statement: "We keep having [this problem] because [we think this is why]." Don't try to solve it yet. Just name it.

2. **Count the cost.** For that one recurring problem, try to estimate how much time your team spends on it each month. Hours of labor, rework, delays, customer impact. You don't need perfect numbers--a rough estimate is enough to make the invisible visible.

3. **Ask "when did we last check?"** Pick any process improvement or fix your team implemented in the past year. Ask yourself: have we gone back to verify it's still working? If the answer is no, you've just found your first closed-loop opportunity.

4. **Watch for solution-first language in your next meeting.** Listen for statements like "We need to..." followed by a solution. When you hear one, gently ask: "What's the problem we're trying to solve?" You don't need to explain PIPS. Just ask the question. You'll be amazed at how often the room goes quiet.

5. **Start a "lessons learned" file.** It can be a shared document, a wiki page, a spreadsheet--the format doesn't matter. The next time your team solves a problem, write down what the problem was, what you tried, what worked, and what you'd do differently. Future you will thank present you.

title: The Firefighting Trap
summary: Here's a story you'll recognize.
bodyMd:
Here's a story you'll recognize.

It's 4:30 PM on a Thursday. You're about to leave for the day when your phone buzzes. There's been a system outage. Claims aren't processing. The client is furious. You cancel your evening plans, pull together a team, work until midnight, find the issue, patch it, get everything running again. The client sends a thank-you email. Your boss copies leadership on a message praising your team's quick response. Someone uses the word "heroic."

You feel great. And you should--you solved a real problem under real pressure.

But here's the question nobody asks: Why did the system go down in the first place? Was it the same type of failure that happened three months ago? Is there a pattern? Could it have been prevented?

Nobody asks because there's already a new fire to fight.

This is the firefighting trap, and it is absolutely everywhere. I've seen it in healthcare IT, in hospital operations, in HR departments, in finance teams, in software companies of every size. The pattern is always the same:

1. A problem erupts.
2. Smart people drop everything to fix it.
3. They fix it (or at least make it stop hurting).
4. Everyone gets praised for the response.
5. Nobody investigates the root cause.
6. The same problem erupts again weeks or months later.
7. Return to Step 1.

The trap is self-reinforcing because of how organizations hand out rewards. Think about who gets recognized at your company. Is it the person who quietly prevented a problem from ever occurring? Or is it the person who swooped in at 11 PM to save the day when the problem exploded?

I already know the answer. We all do.

I once worked with an operations team that had a "hero board" in their break room. Literally a whiteboard where they celebrated people who went above and beyond during crises. It was a genuinely well-intentioned morale booster. But think about what it was actually incentivizing. The message, whether anyone intended it or not, was: *crises are where you get noticed.*

Nobody puts you on the hero board for "quietly improved a process so that the thing that used to break every month hasn't broken in six months." There's no drama in prevention. There's no adrenaline in stability. But stability is where the real value lives.

The firefighting trap costs organizations enormous amounts of money, but the costs are mostly invisible. You don't see the invoice for "time spent on problems that could have been prevented." It doesn't show up as a line item in the budget. But it's there, buried in overtime, in turnover, in missed deadlines, in the slow erosion of morale that comes from working in an environment where nothing ever really gets fixed.

I'm not saying crisis response doesn't matter. Of course it does. When the building is on fire, you need firefighters. But if the building catches fire every month, at some point you need to stop congratulating the firefighters and start asking why the building keeps catching fire.

---

title: The Solution-First Disease
summary: If the firefighting trap is the most common problem-solving failure, the solution-first disease is the most dangerous. Because it feels productive. It feels like progress. You're *doing something*. Yo...
bodyMd:
If the firefighting trap is the most common problem-solving failure, the solution-first disease is the most dangerous. Because it feels productive. It feels like progress. You're *doing something*. You're being proactive. You're taking action.

You're also, very possibly, wasting a tremendous amount of time and money.

Here's how it works. Someone in a meeting says: "We need a new CRM."

Everyone nods. A committee is formed. Vendors are evaluated. Demos are scheduled. Six months and a quarter million dollars later, you have a shiny new CRM system. And the sales team still spends 40% of their time on data entry instead of selling.

Because "we need a new CRM" was never a problem statement. It was a solution masquerading as a problem. The actual problem--if anyone had bothered to define it--was something like: "Sales representatives spend 40% of their working hours on administrative data entry, reducing customer-facing selling time by approximately 20 hours per rep per week."

That's a problem statement. It describes a current state, it's specific, and most importantly, it doesn't presuppose the solution. Maybe a new CRM is the answer. Maybe it's automation within the existing CRM. Maybe it's hiring admin support. Maybe it's eliminating half the fields that nobody looks at anyway. You don't know until you actually analyze the problem. But the moment someone said "we need a new CRM," all those other possibilities disappeared.

I see this constantly. It's not limited to technology decisions. It shows up everywhere:

**HR:** "We need to increase salaries to reduce turnover."
Really? Have you actually talked to the people who left? Have you looked at the data? I worked with a healthcare organization where everyone--leadership, managers, HR--was convinced that compensation was driving their 35% nursing turnover rate. They were ready to spend millions on across-the-board raises. Then someone actually did exit interviews. Real ones, not the checkbox forms people fill out on their last day. Structured conversations with people who had already left and had no reason to hold back.

The number one reason wasn't pay. It was the first 90 days. New nurses felt abandoned after orientation ended. They didn't know who to ask for help. They felt incompetent and unsupported. The ones who left early cited a lack of mentorship and a chaotic onboarding process. The organization didn't have a compensation problem. It had an onboarding problem. And an onboarding fix costs a fraction of what across-the-board raises cost. (I'll walk through a more detailed version of how PIPS handles this kind of HR challenge in Chapter 10.)

**IT:** "We need to upgrade our ticketing system."
Maybe. Or maybe the current system is fine and the real issue is that 60% of your tickets are repeat issues that could be eliminated with better documentation or upstream fixes. A fancier system for managing tickets you shouldn't be getting in the first place is just a more expensive way to be inefficient.

**Operations:** "We need to hire more staff for quarter-end."
Or maybe the quarter-end crunch happens because work that should have been done throughout the quarter gets deferred until the last two weeks. More staff doesn't fix a workflow problem. It just means more people participating in the same broken process.

The solution-first disease is seductive because solutions are concrete. "Buy this software." "Hire these people." "Change this policy." They're action items. They feel decisive. Problems, by contrast, are messy. They require investigation. They require admitting that you don't fully understand what's going on. In most organizational cultures, saying "I think we need to study this more before we act" is career suicide. The person who says "let's just do X" gets praised for being action-oriented.

But action without understanding is just motion. Motion isn't progress.

---

title: The Opinion Economy
summary: Every organization runs on some mix of data and opinions. In most organizations, the mix is about 90% opinions and 10% data, and most of that 10% is cherry-picked to support someone's pre-existing opi...
bodyMd:
Every organization runs on some mix of data and opinions. In most organizations, the mix is about 90% opinions and 10% data, and most of that 10% is cherry-picked to support someone's pre-existing opinion.

I call this the opinion economy, and it's the default operating mode of almost every meeting you've ever sat in.

Here's what it looks like. A decision needs to be made. People sit around a table. Everyone shares their perspective, which is usually based on their personal experience, their gut feeling, or something they half-remember from a conference three years ago. The person with the strongest personality or the highest title wins. The decision gets made. Everyone moves on.

Sometimes the loud person or the senior person is right. Experience and intuition are real things and they have real value. But "sometimes being right" isn't a system. It's a coin flip with better-than-average odds.

There's a quote attributed to Grace Hopper, the pioneering computer scientist and Navy rear admiral, that I come back to constantly in this work:

> "One accurate measurement is worth a thousand expert opinions."

One accurate measurement. Not a dashboard with 400 metrics. Not a six-month data science project. One accurate measurement of the thing that actually matters.

I was working with a team that managed patient scheduling for a large clinic network. They were trying to figure out why patient no-show rates were climbing. The meeting went exactly how you'd expect. The office managers said patients were irresponsible. The physicians said the reminder system was inadequate. The IT team said the scheduling software was outdated. Finance said they needed stricter cancellation policies.

Everyone had a theory. Nobody had data.

So we got the data. We pulled the actual no-show records for the past twelve months and broke them down by day of week, time of day, provider, clinic location, and appointment type. The pattern was immediate and obvious: no-show rates were dramatically higher for appointments booked more than three weeks in advance. The longer the lead time between booking and appointment, the more likely the patient was to no-show. The effect was so strong that it dwarfed every other variable.

The solution wasn't a better reminder system or stricter policies or new software. It was reducing the average scheduling window. That's it. A process change that cost essentially nothing.

But here's the thing: nobody in that room would have proposed that solution based on opinions alone. It was invisible to intuition. Only the data made it visible.

I want to be clear about something because I think this gets misunderstood. Saying "data over opinions" does not mean that experience and intuition are worthless. Far from it. Experience is incredibly valuable for telling you *where to look*. The office managers' intuition that something was off with patient behavior was correct. They just didn't have the right explanation for it. Intuition is your compass. Data is your map. You need both. But when the compass and the map disagree, trust the map.

The opinion economy persists because gathering data takes effort, and effort takes time, and time is the thing everyone says they don't have. It's faster to go with your gut. It's faster to defer to the boss. And sometimes fast is what you need. But for the problems that actually matter--the ones that keep coming back, the ones that cost real money, the ones that affect real people--"fast" isn't fast if it leads to the wrong answer.

---

title: The "Solved It" Illusion
summary: This one might be the most painful because it strikes at the moment you feel best.
bodyMd:

You identified a problem. You analyzed it. You implemented a solution. It worked. People are happy. You move on to the next thing.

What happened?

Nobody checked.

That's it. Nobody went back to verify that the solution was still working. Nobody measured the results after the initial implementation glow faded. Nobody closed the loop.

This is astonishingly common. I would estimate that fewer than 10% of the improvement initiatives I've seen in my career included a genuine follow-up evaluation more than 90 days after implementation. And so the illusion persists. The project tracker says "complete." The improvement is listed in the annual report. But the actual problem is right back where it started.

But a process doesn't end. The environment doesn't stop changing. The people doing the work don't stop adapting (and adapting isn't always improving). If you're not continuously monitoring, you're not improving. You're just hoping.

---
