title: What PIPS Is
summary: Before I explain what PIPS is, I need to tell you how I found it. Or rather, how it found me.
bodyMd:
# Chapter 2: What PIPS Is (and What It Isn't)

---

Before I explain what PIPS is, I need to tell you how I found it. Or rather, how it found me.

Early in my career in healthcare IT, I was managing implementations--the kind where you're deploying complex systems into environments where failure means actual patient care gets disrupted. Every implementation was a variation on the same theme: things went wrong, smart people scrambled, and at the end we either declared victory or quietly moved on. Occasionally someone would mention "process improvement" and we'd all nod solemnly, the way you nod when someone says "we should really exercise more."

Then I encountered the methodology that would eventually become PIPS. It didn't arrive with fanfare. It showed up in a training session I was half-reluctant to attend, rooted in Xerox's Problem Solving Process--a structured approach developed in the 1980s that gave people a common language and a repeatable method for tackling problems of any size. Something clicked. Not because the ideas were revolutionary (they weren't), but because they were *practical*. This wasn't theory. This was a system that regular people--not statisticians, not consultants, not Six Sigma black belts--could actually use.

Over the following years, I adapted it. I tested it in real environments with real teams facing real pressure. I borrowed from Lean. I incorporated elements of Kaizen. I simplified where things were overcomplicated and added structure where things were too loose. The methodology evolved through use, through failure, through the kind of messy real-world application that turns a nice idea into something that actually works.

What emerged is PIPS: Process Improvement and Problem Solving. And this chapter is about setting your expectations correctly--because a methodology adopted with the wrong expectations becomes another entry in the graveyard of "things we tried that one time."

---

## The Name Tells You What It Is

PIPS stands for Process Improvement and Problem Solving. Both halves of that name matter, and they're not the same thing.

**Problem Solving** is reactive. Something is broken. Something hurts. Something isn't working the way it should. You need to figure out what's wrong and fix it. This is where most people's minds go when they hear "process improvement"--they think about fixing things that are broken.

**Process Improvement** is proactive. Nothing is necessarily broken. Things are working. But could they work *better*? Could they be faster, less wasteful, more reliable, more satisfying for the people doing them? This is the harder discipline because there's no burning platform. Nobody's screaming. There's no crisis to create urgency. You're choosing to make something good into something better, and that requires a different kind of motivation.

PIPS handles both. The same six steps, the same principles, the same tools. Whether you're responding to a crisis or pursuing an opportunity, the method is the same. That's by design. You shouldn't need two different systems for "fixing what's broken" and "improving what works." The thinking is the same. The rigor is the same. The discipline is the same.

I've seen organizations that are great at problem solving but terrible at process improvement. They fight every fire with admirable speed but never invest in fire prevention. And I've seen organizations (fewer of them, admittedly) that are so focused on long-term optimization that they can't respond to a crisis effectively. PIPS is designed to handle the full spectrum.

---

## What PIPS Is

Here's what you're getting.

### A Structured Six-Step Methodology

PIPS is a six-step process. Each step has a clear purpose, defined inputs and outputs, and specific tools you can use. The steps are:

```
 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                                                          │
 │   1. IDENTIFY          "What do we want to change?"      │
 │        ↓                                                 │
 │   2. ANALYZE           "What's preventing us?"           │
 │        ↓                                                 │
 │   3. GENERATE          "How could we make the change?"   │
 │        ↓                                                 │
 │   4. SELECT & PLAN     "What's the best way to do it?"   │
 │        ↓                                                 │
 │   5. IMPLEMENT         "Are we following the plan?"      │
 │        ↓                                                 │
 │   6. EVALUATE          "How well did it work?"           │
 │        │                                                 │
 │        └──────────── back to Step 1 ──────────────┘      │
 │                                                          │
 └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

That's it. Six steps. The arrow from Step 6 back to Step 1 is the most important arrow in the diagram, and we'll talk about why in the next chapter when we discuss the principles.

Each step will get its own chapter later in this book, with detailed guidance, tools, templates, and examples. For now, I just want you to see the shape of the thing. Notice how each step answers a plain-language question. There's no jargon. There's no specialized vocabulary you need to learn before you can start. If you can ask "what do we want to change?" you can begin Step 1.

### A Common Language

This might be the most underrated benefit of PIPS, and it's the one that takes the longest to appreciate.

When your organization adopts PIPS, you're not just adopting a method. You're adopting a *vocabulary*. When someone says "we're in Step 2," everyone knows what that means. When someone says "we haven't done the expansion phase yet," everyone knows they're saying "we haven't brainstormed enough options before narrowing down." When someone says "we need to close the loop on this," everyone knows they're talking about going back to evaluate whether a previous solution is still working.

Common language eliminates an enormous amount of confusion and misalignment. I've sat in meetings where half the room thought they were discussing the problem and the other half thought they were discussing the solution. Nobody realized they were having two different conversations. With PIPS, you can literally say: "Hold on--are we in Step 1 or Step 3 right now?" and everyone recalibrates instantly.

This sounds trivial. It isn't. Communication breakdowns are at the root of most organizational dysfunction, and a shared framework for thinking and talking about problems is one of the most powerful tools you can give a team.

### A Cultural Operating System

This is where PIPS goes beyond methodology into something bigger.

A methodology tells you how to do something. A cultural operating system tells you how to *think* about things. When PIPS takes root in an organization--really takes root, not just gets adopted on paper--it changes how people approach problems in their daily work, not just in formal improvement projects.

Someone encounters a frustration. Instead of complaining about it or working around it, they think: "That's a problem I could identify and define." A team is debating a decision. Instead of arguing from opinions, someone says: "What does the data tell us?" A project gets implemented and instead of moving on, someone asks: "How are we going to evaluate this in 90 days?"

These aren't formal PIPS projects. They're habits. They're reflexes. They're what happens when a methodology becomes part of how people think. That's the cultural operating system, and it's the real prize.

The word that captures it best is *ownership*. People stop thinking of processes as someone else's responsibility and start thinking "I own this." Not because a manager assigned it to them. Because the system gives them a structured way to actually do something about the problems they see every day. When people feel ownership over their work and the processes around it, they don't just execute tasks -- they question whether the task should exist in the first place. That shift, from executor to owner, is where PIPS stops being a methodology and starts being a culture.

The formal projects are important, but the cultural shift is where the transformative value lives.

### A Toolkit

PIPS comes with tools. Specific, practical tools for each step. Cause-and-effect diagrams. Affinity diagrams. Criteria matrices. Force field analysis. Stakeholder maps. Pareto charts. These aren't exotic--most of them have been around for decades. What PIPS does is organize them. It tells you which tool to use when, and more importantly, *why* you're using it.

I've met plenty of people who know what a fishbone diagram is but have never used one effectively because they didn't know where it fits in the problem-solving process. They've used it in Step 1 when it belongs in Step 2. Or they've done a brainstorm in Step 4 when it belongs in Step 3. The tools aren't magic. Their power comes from using them at the right time, in the right sequence, for the right purpose.

### A Way of Thinking

Ultimately, PIPS is a thinking discipline. It helps you resist the tendencies that make problem solving hard: jumping to solutions, relying on opinions, declaring victory too early, and not going back to check your work.

Here's the thing most people don't realize: you already do this. When you're deciding what to have for dinner, your brain runs through a version of this process. What do I want? What's in the fridge? What are my options? Which one sounds best? Make it. Was it good? You don't think of that as a methodology. It's just how thinking works.

PIPS takes that same natural process and gives it structure for problems that are too big or too complex to work through in your head. The difference between deciding what's for dinner and figuring out why your error rate tripled is scale, not logic. And that's what makes PIPS accessible -- it's not asking you to think in some foreign way. It's asking you to apply the thinking you already do, with more rigor and more discipline, to harder problems.

Most organizations don't need a bucket to bail water out of a leaking boat. They need the bucket, a way to plug the leak, a plan to make sure the leak doesn't happen again, and a way to verify it's actually fixed. That's what PIPS is. The full toolkit, not just the emergency response.

---

## What PIPS Is Not

Setting clear expectations about what PIPS *isn't* will save you frustration later. I've seen each of these misconceptions derail an adoption, and I'd rather address them now.

### It Is Not a Certification Program

There are no PIPS belts. There is no PIPS certification exam. There is no governing body that will audit your PIPS compliance and award you a plaque for your wall.

I say this not because certifications are bad (they have their place), but because the certification mindset creates a dangerous dynamic: it turns a way of thinking into a credential. Once it's a credential, it becomes something a few people "have" and most people don't. It becomes gatekept. It becomes a specialization instead of a universal skill.

PIPS is meant to be owned by everyone. The VP and the front-line worker. The 30-year veteran and the new hire. The moment you create a class of "PIPS-certified people" you've created a class of "everyone else," and "everyone else" is where the problems actually live.

### It Is Not a Consulting Engagement

I'm not going to show up at your office for a week, generate a beautiful PowerPoint deck with my findings, hand it to you, and leave. That model of improvement--outside expert parachutes in, diagnoses problems, prescribes solutions, disappears--has a roughly zero percent long-term success rate. I've seen the binders. They're on shelves. Gathering dust.

PIPS only works if your people learn it, practice it, and own it. I can teach it. I can facilitate it. I can coach you through it. But I can't do it *for* you, and frankly, you wouldn't want me to. The whole point is building your organization's own capability. If PIPS only works when I'm in the room, I've failed.

### It Is Not a Replacement for Expertise

PIPS will not teach a nurse how to read an EKG. It will not teach a developer how to write code. It will not teach an accountant how to do a reconciliation. PIPS is a *meta-skill*--it's a way of applying your existing expertise more effectively to problems and improvements.

The best PIPS sessions I've ever facilitated involved people who were deep experts in their domains but had never been given a structured way to channel that expertise toward problem solving. The expertise was always there. PIPS just gave it a direction and a method.

### It Is Not a Silver Bullet

If someone tells you that PIPS will solve all your problems, they're selling you something. PIPS will not fix a toxic leadership culture. It will not compensate for a fundamentally broken business model. It will not overcome a lack of resources or a lack of will.

What PIPS will do is give you a reliable, repeatable way to identify and address the problems that *are* within your control. Over time, the cumulative effect of consistently solving the right problems in the right way is transformative. But "over time" is the key phrase. This isn't a quick fix. It's a discipline, and disciplines take practice.

### It Is Not Something Only "Process People" Do

This is the misconception that bothers me the most. In too many organizations, "process improvement" is a department. It's a team of specialists who run improvement projects while everyone else does "the real work." The implicit message is: improving how we work is someone else's job.

No. Improving how you work is *your* job. It's everyone's job. PIPS is designed for the HR manager who's frustrated that onboarding takes six weeks. For the IT lead who's tired of the same tickets every month. For the finance director who dreads quarter-end. For the nurse who knows there's a better way to handle medication reconciliation. For the developer who spends half their day in meetings that produce no decisions.

If you do work, you can improve work. PIPS is the tool that helps you do it systematically.

---

## Where PIPS Comes From

Every methodology has a lineage, and I think it's important to be transparent about PIPS's roots rather than pretending it emerged fully formed from my brilliant mind. It didn't.

The primary ancestor is **Xerox's Problem Solving Process (PSP)**, developed in the 1980s as part of Xerox's "Leadership Through Quality" initiative. PSP was remarkable for its time--it gave every employee in the company, from factory floor to executive suite, a common method and a common language for solving problems. The core insight of PSP--that problem solving is a skill that can be taught, practiced, and systematized--is the foundation of everything in this book.

PIPS also draws from **Lean thinking**, particularly the relentless focus on waste elimination. The idea that any activity which doesn't add value for the customer is waste--and that you should systematically identify and remove waste--is baked into how PIPS approaches process improvement.

From **Six Sigma**, PIPS borrows the insistence on data-driven decision making. The principle of "Data Over Opinions" (which we'll explore in depth in Chapter 3) is essentially the Six Sigma philosophy stripped of the statistical complexity that makes Six Sigma intimidating to non-specialists.

From **Kaizen**, PIPS takes the concept of continuous, incremental improvement. Not dramatic transformation projects (though PIPS can handle those too), but the steady, disciplined habit of making things a little better every day.

What PIPS does differently is *package* these ideas in a way that's accessible. You don't need a statistics background to use PIPS. You don't need to learn Japanese manufacturing terminology. You don't need a green belt or a black belt. You need a willingness to think clearly, gather data, and follow a structured process. If you can do those things, you can do PIPS.

---

## PIPS Scales

One of the things I love about PIPS is its scalability. The same methodology works for problems of wildly different sizes.

**Small:** "I can never find a parking spot when I arrive at the office." This might sound trivial, but it's a real problem that affects someone's daily experience. PIPS handles it. You identify the problem (parking is consistently full by 8:30 AM). You analyze it (who's parking there? when do they arrive? are there unused spots in the far lot?). You generate solutions (reserved sections, staggered start times, shuttle from overflow lot). You select and plan. You implement. You evaluate. The whole thing might take a single conversation.

**Medium:** "Our new employee onboarding process takes six weeks and new hires report feeling lost for the first three months." This is a meaningful operational problem that affects productivity, retention, and morale. PIPS gives you the structure to tackle it methodically rather than just throwing a "buddy system" at it and hoping for the best.

**Large:** "Our claims processing error rate is 4.7%, which is costing us approximately $2 million annually in rework and client penalties." This is a high-stakes problem that will require a cross-functional team, significant data analysis, and careful implementation. PIPS handles this too--the same six steps, just with more depth at each one.

The method doesn't change based on the size of the problem. The *investment* changes--how long you spend at each step, how many people are involved, how much data you gather. But the thinking is the same. And that's important because it means you can practice on small problems and build the skills you'll need for big ones.

I actually recommend starting small. Find a parking spot problem. A meeting efficiency problem. A supply ordering problem. Something with low stakes and a manageable scope. Use it to learn the method. Make your mistakes on problems where the consequences of mistakes are minimal. Then, when the big problem arrives--and it will--you'll have the skills and the confidence to tackle it.

---

## The Relationship to Your Existing Work

One more thing before we move on. PIPS does not require you to blow up your existing processes and start from scratch. It's not a replacement for how you do your work. It's a system for *improving* how you do your work.

If you already use Lean, PIPS will complement it. If you do Agile sprints, PIPS fits alongside them. If you have a quality management system, PIPS will strengthen it. The six steps can be applied within whatever framework you're already using. They're not competitive with those frameworks--they're *compatible* with them.

I mention this because one of the biggest barriers to adopting any new methodology is the fear that it means abandoning everything you've already built. It doesn't. Think of PIPS as a new tool in your toolbox, not a replacement for the toolbox itself.

---

## What You Can Do Monday Morning

1. **Define PIPS for your team in one sentence.** Try this: "PIPS is a six-step method for solving problems and improving processes, using data instead of opinions, and checking whether our fixes actually worked." Adapt it to your own words. If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it yet, and that's fine--keep reading.

2. **Identify one problem and one improvement opportunity.** A problem is something that's broken or hurting. An improvement opportunity is something that works but could work better. Write both down. Notice how they feel different even though the method for addressing them will be the same.

3. **Draw the six-step diagram.** Put it on a whiteboard, a sticky note, the back of a napkin. Identify, Analyze, Generate, Select & Plan, Implement, Evaluate. The arrow from Evaluate back to Identify. You don't need to understand all the steps yet. Just learn the shape of the process. When a shape is familiar, it's easier to fill in the details.

4. **Have a "What methodology are we using?" conversation.** Ask your team: "When we encounter a problem, what's our process for solving it?" Listen to the answers. If the answers are vague, inconsistent, or amount to "we just figure it out," you've just made the case for PIPS without having to sell anything.

title: The Name Tells You What It Is
summary: PIPS stands for Process Improvement and Problem Solving. Both halves of that name matter, and they're not the same thing.
bodyMd:
PIPS stands for Process Improvement and Problem Solving. Both halves of that name matter, and they're not the same thing.

**Problem Solving** is reactive. Something is broken. Something hurts. Something isn't working the way it should. You need to figure out what's wrong and fix it. This is where most people's minds go when they hear "process improvement"--they think about fixing things that are broken.

**Process Improvement** is proactive. Nothing is necessarily broken. Things are working. But could they work *better*? Could they be faster, less wasteful, more reliable, more satisfying for the people doing them? This is the harder discipline because there's no burning platform. Nobody's screaming. There's no crisis to create urgency. You're choosing to make something good into something better, and that requires a different kind of motivation.

PIPS handles both. The same six steps, the same principles, the same tools. Whether you're responding to a crisis or pursuing an opportunity, the method is the same. That's by design. You shouldn't need two different systems for "fixing what's broken" and "improving what works." The thinking is the same. The rigor is the same. The discipline is the same.

I've seen organizations that are great at problem solving but terrible at process improvement. They fight every fire with admirable speed but never invest in fire prevention. And I've seen organizations (fewer of them, admittedly) that are so focused on long-term optimization that they can't respond to a crisis effectively. PIPS is designed to handle the full spectrum.

---

summary: Here's what you're getting.

title: Three Principles
summary: Every methodology needs a foundation. Not a mission statement--those get printed on posters and ignored. I mean a foundation. Something load-bearing. Something that holds the weight of every decision ...
bodyMd:
# Chapter 3: The Three Principles

---

Every methodology needs a foundation. Not a mission statement--those get printed on posters and ignored. I mean a foundation. Something load-bearing. Something that holds the weight of every decision you'll make when you're in the middle of a messy, ambiguous, frustrating problem and you're not sure what to do next.

PIPS has three principles. They are simple enough to fit on an index card and deep enough to spend a career mastering. They govern every step of the process, and when you're stuck--truly stuck, not sure what to do--coming back to these three principles will almost always show you the way forward.

Here they are:

1. **Data Over Opinions**
2. **Expand Then Contract**
3. **Close the Loop**

Let's take them one at a time.

---

## Principle 1: Data Over Opinions

This is the most important principle in PIPS. If you internalize nothing else from this book, internalize this: every assertion must be grounded in verifiable data.

I know that sounds extreme. It's meant to. Because the default mode of every organization I've ever worked with is the opposite--decisions are made based on experience, intuition, seniority, volume, and political capital. Data is sometimes consulted, often selectively, and frequently overridden by someone who "just knows."

Voltaire wrote something over 250 years ago that I think about regularly:

> "Opinion has caused more trouble on this little earth than plagues or earthquakes."

He was talking about religious and political conflicts, but the principle applies just as well to the conference room. Opinions masquerading as facts cause more wasted effort in organizations than almost anything else. They send teams chasing the wrong problems. They fund the wrong solutions. They create a false sense of understanding that prevents anyone from doing the actual work of finding out what's really going on.

### A Story About Hold Times

Let me tell you about a customer service team I worked with.

They ran a call center for a healthcare technology company. Customer satisfaction scores had been declining for two quarters. When I asked the team what was driving the decline, the answer was immediate and unanimous: hold times. Customers were waiting too long to get through, getting frustrated, and giving low scores.

This made intuitive sense. Everyone hates being on hold. The team had already started working on solutions: hiring more agents, adjusting shift schedules, exploring callback technology. They were in full solution mode.

But I had this nagging principle in my head--data over opinions--so I asked a simple question: "Can I see the data on hold times?"

They pulled it up. Average hold time was 47 seconds. Industry benchmark for their sector was about 60 seconds. Their hold times were *better than average*.

The room went quiet.

So we dug deeper. We pulled the actual customer satisfaction surveys and read the verbatim comments. We correlated satisfaction scores with various call metrics: hold time, call duration, transfer rate, first-call resolution. The pattern was clear and it had nothing to do with hold times.

The driver was **first-call resolution rate**. When customers got their issue resolved on the first call, satisfaction scores were consistently high regardless of how long they waited on hold. When customers had to call back--either because the agent couldn't resolve the issue or because they were transferred to someone who then couldn't resolve it--satisfaction cratered. Customers didn't mind waiting 47 seconds if the person who answered actually fixed their problem. They minded enormously when they had to call back twice.

If the team had followed their opinions, they would have spent six months and significant budget on reducing hold times that were already good. The actual problem--first-call resolution--would have gone unaddressed. Satisfaction scores would have continued to decline, and everyone would have been baffled because they "fixed" hold times and nothing got better.

This is what opinions do. They send you confidently in the wrong direction.

### Where Intuition Fits

Let me be clear: experience and intuition are not worthless. They're incredibly valuable.

But here's the distinction: **intuition tells you where to look. Data tells you what you found.**

The customer service team's intuition that something was wrong with the customer experience was correct. They were right that customers were unhappy. They were right that the problem was related to the phone interactions. Their instincts were good. What they got wrong was the *explanation*--they attributed the dissatisfaction to hold times when the actual cause was first-call resolution.

This is the typical pattern. Experienced people are excellent at detecting that something is off. They've seen enough patterns to know when something doesn't feel right. That instinct is valuable. Use it. Let it guide your investigation. But don't let it replace your investigation.

Think of it this way: if you're lost in the woods, your gut feeling about which direction is north is worth paying attention to. It's a starting point. But if you have a compass, and the compass disagrees with your gut, you should follow the compass. Intuition is your gut feeling. Data is your compass.

### The Measurement Principle

There's a corollary to "Data Over Opinions" that's worth stating explicitly:

**If you cannot measure it, you cannot know if you have solved it.**

This might sound like a call for rigorous metrics programs and expensive analytics platforms. It's not. Remember: one accurate measurement is worth a thousand expert opinions. You don't need a dashboard with 47 KPIs. You need one or two measures that tell you whether the thing you're trying to change is actually changing.

Before you start working on any problem, you need to answer two questions:

1. **What does the current state look like, in numbers?** (How bad is it? How often does it happen? How much does it cost?)
2. **How will we know if we've improved it?** (What number needs to change? By how much? Over what time period?)

If you can't answer these questions, you're not ready to start solving the problem. Not because the problem isn't real, but because you'll have no way of knowing whether your solution worked. And if you don't know whether it worked, you'll fall right into the "solved it" illusion from Chapter 1.

This doesn't have to be sophisticated. I've seen highly effective improvement projects where the measurement was a simple tally on a whiteboard. "How many times this week did we have to manually correct a claims file?" Tick marks. That's data. It's not fancy, but it's verifiable, and it gives you a baseline to compare against after you implement a change.

### When You Don't Have Data

Sometimes you genuinely don't have data. The thing you need to measure isn't being tracked. There's no system capturing it. Nobody has ever counted it before.

Good. Now you know your first task: start measuring.

This is itself a valuable outcome. One of the most common realizations teams have when they adopt PIPS is: "We don't actually know how we're performing on this thing we consider important." That realization is the beginning of improvement. You can't manage what you don't measure, and you can't measure what you haven't defined.

Start simple. Count things. Time things. Write things down. You don't need a perfect data collection system to begin. You need a baseline, and a baseline can be as simple as: "This week, we're going to count how many times X happens."

The data doesn't have to be perfect. It has to be *better than zero*. Because zero data means you're operating on opinions, and opinions--as we've established--send you confidently in the wrong direction with alarming regularity.

### The Cultural Challenge

I won't pretend that "Data Over Opinions" is easy to implement culturally. It can be threatening. When you insist on data, you're implicitly saying that experience and seniority don't give someone the right to declare the answer. That can feel disrespectful, especially to people who've been in their roles for decades and are used to being the authority.

The way I've navigated this is by framing data not as a challenge to expertise but as a *complement* to it. "You've been here 20 years and you know this area better than anyone. Help me understand what we should measure to confirm what you're seeing." This enlists the expert as a partner in the data-gathering process rather than positioning them as an adversary who needs to be fact-checked.

Most of the time, the data confirms what experienced people already suspect. When it does, the data gives their intuition credibility and weight. "I've always thought X was the problem" becomes "I suspected X was the problem, and the data confirms it." That's a much stronger position than intuition alone.

And when the data contradicts what people expected? That's where the real learning happens. That's where you discover the first-call resolution problem hiding behind the hold time assumption. Those moments are uncomfortable, but they're also the moments where organizations make genuine breakthroughs.

---

## Principle 2: Expand Then Contract (Diverge Then Converge)

If Data Over Opinions is about *what* you base decisions on, Expand Then Contract is about *how* you make them. It's the rhythm of the PIPS process, and it operates at nearly every step.

The principle is straightforward: at each step, first cast a wide net (expansion), then filter down (contraction). First generate many options, then select from among them.

This rhythm prevents two symmetrical failure modes:

**Tunnel vision** happens when you don't expand enough. You consider one or two options, pick the one that seems best, and move forward. The problem is that the best option might have been the third or fourth one you never thought of. Tunnel vision feels efficient--"we made a quick decision!"--but efficiency is meaningless if you picked the wrong answer.

**Analysis paralysis** happens when you expand without ever contracting. You brainstorm endlessly. You gather more and more options. You explore every possibility. But you never narrow down, never commit, never choose. The project stalls in a morass of "but have we considered..." and nothing gets implemented.

Expand Then Contract prevents both failures by making each phase explicit and time-bounded. You expand. You stop expanding. You contract. You stop contracting. Then you move to the next step.

### How It Works in Practice

Let's walk through how this rhythm plays out across the six steps.

**Step 1: Identify & Select the Problem.**
- *Expand:* Brainstorm all the problems, issues, and improvement opportunities you're aware of. Get them all on the table. Every frustration, every inefficiency, every complaint. Don't evaluate yet. Just collect.
- *Contract:* Now filter. Which problems are most impactful? Most urgent? Most feasible to address? Use criteria to narrow your list to one clearly defined problem to work on.

**Step 2: Analyze the Problem.**
- *Expand:* Generate all possible causes. Use cause-and-effect diagrams. Ask "why?" five times. Talk to the people closest to the work. Cast the net wide--you're looking for every factor that might be contributing.
- *Contract:* Now verify. Which of these potential causes are actually supported by data? Which can you confirm? Which are speculation? Narrow to the root causes you can demonstrate are real.

**Step 3: Generate Potential Solutions.**
- *Expand:* Brainstorm every possible solution you can think of. Wild ideas, conservative ideas, expensive ideas, free ideas. No criticism. No "we tried that before." No "that'll never work." Just ideas.
- *Contract:* Apply criteria. Cost, feasibility, timeline, risk, alignment with organizational goals. Filter the list to viable candidates.

**Step 4: Select & Plan the Solution.**
- *Expand:* For each viable candidate, think through implementation options. What are the different ways you could execute this? What are the contingencies? What might go wrong?
- *Contract:* Select your solution. Build the plan. Assign responsibilities. Set timelines. You're committing now.

**Steps 5 and 6 are different.** Implementation and Evaluation have no expansion phase. They are pure execution and pure assessment. Once you've committed to a plan, you execute it. Once you've implemented, you evaluate results. The time for generating options is over. Now you're doing and measuring.

This is an important structural point that people often miss. The expansion/contraction rhythm is not universal across all six steps. It applies to Steps 1 through 4. Steps 5 and 6 are about discipline and follow-through, not about generating more options. If you find yourself brainstorming during implementation, something has gone wrong--either your plan wasn't good enough (go back to Step 4) or you're avoiding the discomfort of committing to a course of action.

### The Brainstorming Rules

The expansion phase is only effective if you protect it from premature judgment. This is where brainstorming rules come in, and they're non-negotiable:

**During expansion:**
- No criticism. None. Not even body language. Not even a sigh. If someone rolls their eyes at an idea, the expansion phase is contaminated. People will stop offering unusual ideas because they don't want to be the person who gets the eye roll.
- Quantity over quality. You want volume. You want the obvious ideas *and* the weird ones. Some of the most effective solutions I've ever seen started as ideas that seemed absurd during brainstorming.
- Build on others' ideas. "Yes, and..." not "No, but..."
- Write everything down. If it's not written, it doesn't exist. Visual capture (whiteboard, sticky notes, shared document) keeps the ideas alive and accessible.

**During contraction:**
- Apply explicit criteria. Not "I like this one better." Not "this feels right." Criteria. What matters for this decision? Cost? Speed of implementation? Risk? Customer impact? Define the criteria *before* you start evaluating options.
- Be transparent about trade-offs. Every option has pros and cons. Make them visible.
- Vote or rank if needed, but make the decision-making process clear. Everyone should understand *how* the decision was made, even if they don't agree with the outcome.

The shift from expansion to contraction should be explicit. Literally say it out loud: "We're done brainstorming. Now we're going to evaluate." This sounds overly formal, and it is, and it works. The formality is the point--it signals to everyone that the rules of engagement have changed.

### A Story About 36 Ideas

I facilitated a PIPS session with an IT team that was struggling with their ticket tracking process. Tickets were falling through the cracks. Customers were following up on issues that the team thought had been resolved. Response times were inconsistent.

During the expansion phase of Step 3, we brainstormed solutions. The team was skeptical at first--they'd been talking about this problem for months and felt like they'd already considered all the options. But I held them to the brainstorming rules. No criticism. Write everything down. Build on each other.

They generated 36 potential solutions.

Thirty-six. A team that thought they'd "already considered everything" came up with 36 ideas when freed from the constraint of evaluating each one as it was proposed. Some were predictable (upgrade the ticketing software, hire more staff). Some were creative (implement a "ticket buddy" system where two agents share responsibility for each ticket). Some were wild (eliminate tickets entirely and move to a walk-up support model).

Then we moved to contraction. We defined criteria: cost under $10K, implementable within 60 days, measurable impact on ticket resolution rate, no additional headcount required. The 36 ideas became 12 that met all criteria. We scored those 12 against impact and effort. Six scored high on impact and low-to-medium on effort. Of those six, the team selected three to implement in phases.

The three they selected were not the three that anyone would have picked on day one. One of them--a daily 15-minute "ticket triage" standup--had emerged late in the brainstorming session, built on top of two other ideas. Without the expansion phase, it never would have surfaced. It turned out to be the single most effective intervention. Ticket follow-up requests dropped by 60% in the first month.

Expand, then contract. The rhythm works because it separates the creative act from the evaluative act. Your brain is not good at doing both simultaneously. Give it permission to create freely, then switch modes and evaluate rigorously. You'll get better outcomes than you would from trying to generate and judge at the same time.

---

## Principle 3: Close the Loop

The third principle is what makes PIPS a *living system* rather than a one-time project.

The concept comes from Karl Albrecht, who in his 1980 book *Brain Power* distinguished between two types of problem solving:

**Open-loop problem solving:** You identify a problem, implement a solution, and move on. You assume the solution worked. You don't go back to check. You don't measure the outcome. You don't verify that the problem is actually resolved. This is how the overwhelming majority of problem solving works in the overwhelming majority of organizations.

**Closed-loop problem solving:** You identify a problem, implement a solution, measure the results, compare them to your baseline, learn from what you find, and feed that learning back into the system. If the solution worked, you document why and share the knowledge. If it didn't work, you figure out why and try something different. The loop closes. Step 6 feeds directly back into Step 1.

The difference between these two approaches is the difference between an organization that *solves problems* and an organization that *gets better at solving problems*. The first is an event. The second is a capability.

### Why Open-Loop Feels Normal

Open-loop problem solving persists because it maps perfectly to how organizations structure work. Work is organized into projects. Projects have start dates and end dates. You kick off the project, you do the work, you deliver the result, you close the project. Done. Move on to the next one.

This structure makes sense for many types of work. If you're building a bridge, the project ends when the bridge is built. But process improvement isn't building a bridge. Processes are living things. They exist in dynamic environments. The people executing them change. The inputs change. The context changes. A solution that works perfectly in January might be irrelevant by July because the conditions that made it effective have shifted.

Open-loop problem solving treats improvement like bridge-building. Implement the solution, declare the bridge built, move on. Closed-loop problem solving recognizes that the bridge is standing in a river that's constantly changing course, and you need to keep checking that it still connects the two banks.

### What Closing the Loop Actually Looks Like

In practical terms, closing the loop means building evaluation and follow-up into your improvement process as non-optional steps. Not "nice to have." Not "if we have time." Non-optional.

Here's what that looks like:

**At implementation (Step 5):** You define what you're going to measure, when you're going to measure it, and who is responsible for measuring it. You set a specific date for the evaluation. Not "sometime in a few months." A date. On the calendar. With someone's name next to it.

**At the evaluation date (Step 6):** You actually do it. You pull the data. You compare current state to baseline. You answer the question: "Did the solution work?" And you answer it with data, not with feelings.

Three outcomes are possible:

1. **It worked.** The data shows measurable improvement. Document what you did, why it worked, and share it. Add it to your organizational knowledge base. Celebrate. Then ask: "Is there more improvement to be had?" If yes, you're back at Step 1 with a new, refined problem statement.

2. **It partially worked.** Some improvement, but not as much as expected, or improvement in some areas but not others. This is the most common outcome, and it's valuable. Analyze why the improvement was partial. What assumptions were wrong? What factors did you not account for? Use what you've learned to refine the solution and try again.

3. **It didn't work.** No improvement, or the problem got worse. This is disappointing but not a failure. It's data. You now know that this approach doesn't solve this problem, which eliminates a hypothesis and narrows your search. Go back to your analysis (Step 2) and look at what you missed.

In all three cases, the loop closes. Learning happens. The organization is smarter than it was before. Even "failure" produces value when the loop is closed, because you've learned something you didn't know before.

### A Story About the Loop

I mentioned in Chapter 1 the IT change management process that fell apart over the course of a year. Let me tell you about a team that got it right.

A different organization, similar problem. Their software deployment process was unreliable--deployments frequently caused incidents, rollbacks were common, and the team spent an enormous amount of time on post-deployment firefighting. They used PIPS to address it.

They went through Steps 1 through 5 diligently. They identified the problem (deployment failure rate of 23%). They analyzed root causes (insufficient testing, unclear deployment procedures, lack of rollback plans). They generated solutions, selected a combination of automated testing gates, deployment checklists, and mandatory rollback procedures, and implemented them.

Here's where it gets interesting. They scheduled evaluation checkpoints at 30 days, 90 days, and 180 days. Not optional. On the calendar. With assigned owners.

At 30 days, deployment failure rate was down to 11%. Good progress, but not where they wanted to be. They dug into the remaining failures and found that most were related to database migration scripts--an area their automated testing didn't cover well. They refined the solution.

At 90 days, failure rate was at 5%. Much better. But they noticed something unexpected: deployment *velocity* had decreased. Teams were deploying less frequently because the new process added steps. Some of the steps, on closer examination, were redundant. They streamlined.

At 180 days, failure rate was at 3% and deployment frequency had actually *increased* beyond the pre-improvement baseline. Teams were deploying more often with fewer failures. The net result was dramatically more reliable software delivery at higher throughput.

None of this would have happened without the evaluation checkpoints. The 30-day refinement caught the database gap. The 90-day check caught the velocity problem. If they had implemented their initial solution and declared victory, they would have had an 11% failure rate and slower deployments--better than before, but far short of what they achieved through iteration.

The loop. Close it. Every time.

### The Third-Time Rule

The simplest version of closing the loop is a rule I've lived by throughout my career: if you've been asked to do something for a third time, build a way so that person can do it themselves. First time, help them. Second time, help them again and note the pattern. Third time? That's not a request anymore. That's a process gap. Close the loop -- build the self-service tool, write the documentation, automate the workflow, whatever it takes to make that third request the last one.

That single rule, applied consistently, eliminated more recurring work than any formal initiative I've ever run. It's Close the Loop at its most practical: don't just solve the problem. Solve the *category* of problem.

### The Never-Ending Quest

The title of this book is "The Never-Ending Quest," and the closed loop is why.

The quest never ends because there is always more to learn. Every solution creates new conditions. Every improvement reveals the next improvement. Every problem you solve brings a new problem into focus--not because things are getting worse, but because you can now see things that were previously invisible.

I know "the quest never ends" can sound exhausting. But think about it personally -- do you want to be the same person in five years? Same skills, same understanding? Of course not. You want to grow. The quest to improve yourself never ends, and you don't experience that as a burden.

Organizations work the same way. A living organization is constantly learning and adapting. A dead one has "figured it out" and stopped changing.

There's a line I keep coming back to, and I want you to sit with it:

> "Circumstances change, situations change, people change, and your preferences and values change. You must adapt your solutions to the changing times."

The solution you implemented last year was right for last year's conditions. Are conditions the same today? Maybe. Maybe not. You won't know unless you check. And checking is closing the loop.

### Why This Principle Changes Everything

Data Over Opinions governs what you base decisions on. Expand Then Contract governs how you generate and evaluate options. Close the Loop adds the time dimension -- it takes a one-time fix and turns it into an ongoing capability.

Without Close the Loop, PIPS is a good problem-solving method. With it, PIPS becomes a learning system. And learning systems are the only kind that improve over time.

Every principle matters. But if I had to pick the one that separates organizations that dabble in improvement from organizations that are genuinely excellent at it, it's this one. The best organizations I've worked with are not the ones with the smartest problem solvers. They're the ones that relentlessly close the loop. They check. They verify. They learn. They adapt. And they never, ever assume that something they fixed stays fixed.

---

## The Three Principles Together

The three principles are not independent. They reinforce each other.

**Data Over Opinions** fuels **Close the Loop** because you can't evaluate whether something worked unless you have data. If you didn't measure the baseline before you started, you have nothing to compare against. If you're relying on opinions to judge the outcome ("yeah, it feels better"), you'll fall into the solved-it illusion every time.

**Expand Then Contract** prevents the tunnel vision that undermines **Data Over Opinions.** If you only look at one data source, or only consider one possible cause, your data analysis is incomplete even if it's technically rigorous. Expanding your search for causes and solutions before narrowing ensures that your data-driven decisions are based on a comprehensive picture, not a narrow one.

**Close the Loop** makes **Expand Then Contract** sustainable. Each time you close the loop, you learn something that informs the next cycle. The solutions you didn't select in Step 4 might become relevant when conditions change. The root causes you identified but didn't address in Step 2 might become priorities in the next cycle. The organizational knowledge accumulates.

Together, the three principles make the system rigorous (Data Over Opinions), creative (Expand Then Contract), and adaptive (Close the Loop). Drop any one and the system breaks. Rigor without creativity is brittle. Creativity without rigor is chaos. Both without adaptiveness is a system that can't learn from itself.

When you're in the middle of a PIPS project and you're not sure what to do, ask yourself three questions:

1. What does the data tell me? (Data Over Opinions)
2. Have I considered enough options? (Expand Then Contract)
3. How will I know if this worked? (Close the Loop)

If you can answer all three, you're on solid ground. If you can't answer one, that's where your attention should go.

---

## What You Can Do Monday Morning

1. **Ask for data in your next meeting.** When someone makes an assertion--"customers are unhappy," "the process is too slow," "we don't have enough resources"--ask a simple question: "How do we know?" Not confrontationally. Curiously. "How do we know?" opens the door to data without attacking anyone's credibility. You might find out that the assertion is well-supported, which is great. Or you might find out that it's an assumption nobody has verified, which is even more useful.

2. **Practice expansion and contraction deliberately.** The next time you need to make a decision as a team, explicitly split the conversation into two phases. Say: "For the next 15 minutes, let's generate as many options as we can without evaluating any of them. Then we'll spend 15 minutes narrowing down." Set a timer. Enforce the rule. See what happens. Most teams are shocked at how many more options emerge when evaluation is temporarily suspended.

3. **Schedule one follow-up.** Pick any change, improvement, or fix your team has implemented in the past six months. Put a 30-minute meeting on the calendar, three weeks from now, to review whether it's still working. In that meeting, look at data (even informal data). Ask the people doing the work whether the improvement has held. Compare current state to pre-change state if you can. You've just closed a loop. It will feel like the most productive 30 minutes of your month.

4. **Write down the three principles somewhere you'll see them.** A sticky note on your monitor. A note in your phone. A card in your wallet. "Data Over Opinions. Expand Then Contract. Close the Loop." When you find yourself in a conversation where something feels off--where the team is going in circles, or the decision doesn't feel right, or the discussion is generating more heat than light--glance at those three principles. One of them will almost certainly tell you what's missing.

5. **Teach someone else.** Find one person on your team and explain the three principles in your own words. You don't need to call it PIPS. You don't need to reference this book. Just share the ideas. "I've been thinking about how we solve problems, and there are three concepts I think could help us." Teaching is the fastest way to internalize a concept, and it starts building the common language that makes PIPS powerful at the organizational level.

title: Principle 1: Data Over Opinions
summary: This is the most important principle in PIPS. If you internalize nothing else from this book, internalize this: every assertion must be grounded in verifiable data.

It's not fancy, but it's verifiable, and it gives you a baseline to compare against after you implement a change.

### When You Don't Have Data

Sometimes you genuinely don't have data. The thing you need to measure isn't being tracked. There's no system capturing it. Nobody has ever counted it before.

Good. Now you know your first task: start measuring.

This is itself a valuable outcome. One of the most common realizations teams have when they adopt PIPS is: "We don't actually know how we're performing on this thing we consider important." That realization is the beginning of improvement. You can't manage what you don't measure, and you can't measure what you haven't defined.

Start simple. Count things. Time things. Write things down. You don't need a perfect data collection system to begin. You need a baseline, and a baseline can be as simple as: "This week, we're going to count how many times X happens."

The data doesn't have to be perfect. It has to be *better than zero*. Because zero data means you're operating on opinions, and opinions--as we've established--send you confidently in the wrong direction with alarming regularity.

### The Cultural Challenge

I won't pretend that "Data Over Opinions" is easy to implement culturally. It can be threatening. When you insist on data, you're implicitly saying that experience and seniority don't give someone the right to declare the answer. That can feel disrespectful, especially to people who've been in their roles for decades and are used to being the authority.

The way I've navigated this is by framing data not as a challenge to expertise but as a *complement* to it. "You've been here 20 years and you know this area better than anyone. Help me understand what we should measure to confirm what you're seeing." This enlists the expert as a partner in the data-gathering process rather than positioning them as an adversary who needs to be fact-checked.

Most of the time, the data confirms what experienced people already suspect. When it does, the data gives their intuition credibility and weight. "I've always thought X was the problem" becomes "I suspected X was the problem, and the data confirms it." That's a much stronger position than intuition alone.

And when the data contradicts what people expected? That's where the real learning happens. That's where you discover the first-call resolution problem hiding behind the hold time assumption. Those moments are uncomfortable, but they're also the moments where organizations make genuine breakthroughs.

---

title: Principle 2: Expand Then Contract (Diverge Then Converge)
summary: If Data Over Opinions is about *what* you base decisions on, Expand Then Contract is about *how* you make them. It's the rhythm of the PIPS process, and it operates at nearly every step.
bodyMd:
If Data Over Opinions is about *what* you base decisions on, Expand Then Contract is about *how* you make them. It's the rhythm of the PIPS process, and it operates at nearly every step.

The principle is straightforward: at each step, first cast a wide net (expansion), then filter down (contraction). First generate many options, then select from among them.

This rhythm prevents two symmetrical failure modes:

**Tunnel vision** happens when you don't expand enough. You consider one or two options, pick the one that seems best, and move forward. The problem is that the best option might have been the third or fourth one you never thought of. Tunnel vision feels efficient--"we made a quick decision!"--but efficiency is meaningless if you picked the wrong answer.

**Analysis paralysis** happens when you expand without ever contracting. You brainstorm endlessly. You gather more and more options. You explore every possibility. But you never narrow down, never commit, never choose. The project stalls in a morass of "but have we considered..." and nothing gets implemented.

Expand Then Contract prevents both failures by making each phase explicit and time-bounded. You expand. You stop expanding. You contract. You stop contracting. Then you move to the next step.

### How It Works in Practice

Let's walk through how this rhythm plays out across the six steps.

**Step 1: Identify & Select the Problem.**
- *Expand:* Brainstorm all the problems, issues, and improvement opportunities you're aware of. Get them all on the table. Every frustration, every inefficiency, every complaint. Don't evaluate yet. Just collect.
- *Contract:* Now filter. Which problems are most impactful? Most urgent? Most feasible to address? Use criteria to narrow your list to one clearly defined problem to work on.

**Step 2: Analyze the Problem.**
- *Expand:* Generate all possible causes. Use cause-and-effect diagrams. Ask "why?" five times. Talk to the people closest to the work. Cast the net wide--you're looking for every factor that might be contributing.
- *Contract:* Now verify. Which of these potential causes are actually supported by data? Which can you confirm? Which are speculation? Narrow to the root causes you can demonstrate are real.

**Step 3: Generate Potential Solutions.**
- *Expand:* Brainstorm every possible solution you can think of. Wild ideas, conservative ideas, expensive ideas, free ideas. No criticism. No "we tried that before." No "that'll never work." Just ideas.
- *Contract:* Apply criteria. Cost, feasibility, timeline, risk, alignment with organizational goals. Filter the list to viable candidates.

**Step 4: Select & Plan the Solution.**
- *Expand:* For each viable candidate, think through implementation options. What are the different ways you could execute this? What are the contingencies? What might go wrong?
- *Contract:* Select your solution. Build the plan. Assign responsibilities. Set timelines. You're committing now.

**Steps 5 and 6 are different.** Implementation and Evaluation have no expansion phase. They are pure execution and pure assessment. Once you've committed to a plan, you execute it. Once you've implemented, you evaluate results. The time for generating options is over. Now you're doing and measuring.

This is an important structural point that people often miss. The expansion/contraction rhythm is not universal across all six steps. It applies to Steps 1 through 4. Steps 5 and 6 are about discipline and follow-through, not about generating more options. If you find yourself brainstorming during implementation, something has gone wrong--either your plan wasn't good enough (go back to Step 4) or you're avoiding the discomfort of committing to a course of action.

### The Brainstorming Rules

The expansion phase is only effective if you protect it from premature judgment. This is where brainstorming rules come in, and they're non-negotiable:

**During expansion:**
- No criticism. None. Not even body language. Not even a sigh. If someone rolls their eyes at an idea, the expansion phase is contaminated. People will stop offering unusual ideas because they don't want to be the person who gets the eye roll.
- Quantity over quality. You want volume. You want the obvious ideas *and* the weird ones. Some of the most effective solutions I've ever seen started as ideas that seemed absurd during brainstorming.
- Build on others' ideas. "Yes, and..." not "No, but..."
- Write everything down. If it's not written, it doesn't exist. Visual capture (whiteboard, sticky notes, shared document) keeps the ideas alive and accessible.

**During contraction:**
- Apply explicit criteria. Not "I like this one better." Not "this feels right." Criteria. What matters for this decision? Cost? Speed of implementation? Risk? Customer impact? Define the criteria *before* you start evaluating options.
- Be transparent about trade-offs. Every option has pros and cons. Make them visible.
- Vote or rank if needed, but make the decision-making process clear. Everyone should understand *how* the decision was made, even if they don't agree with the outcome.

The shift from expansion to contraction should be explicit. Literally say it out loud: "We're done brainstorming. Now we're going to evaluate." This sounds overly formal, and it is, and it works. The formality is the point--it signals to everyone that the rules of engagement have changed.

### A Story About 36 Ideas

I facilitated a PIPS session with an IT team that was struggling with their ticket tracking process. Tickets were falling through the cracks. Customers were following up on issues that the team thought had been resolved. Response times were inconsistent.

During the expansion phase of Step 3, we brainstormed solutions. The team was skeptical at first--they'd been talking about this problem for months and felt like they'd already considered all the options. But I held them to the brainstorming rules. No criticism. Write everything down. Build on each other.

They generated 36 potential solutions.

Thirty-six. A team that thought they'd "already considered everything" came up with 36 ideas when freed from the constraint of evaluating each one as it was proposed. Some were predictable (upgrade the ticketing software, hire more staff). Some were creative (implement a "ticket buddy" system where two agents share responsibility for each ticket). Some were wild (eliminate tickets entirely and move to a walk-up support model).

Then we moved to contraction. We defined criteria: cost under $10K, implementable within 60 days, measurable impact on ticket resolution rate, no additional headcount required. The 36 ideas became 12 that met all criteria. We scored those 12 against impact and effort. Six scored high on impact and low-to-medium on effort. Of those six, the team selected three to implement in phases.

The three they selected were not the three that anyone would have picked on day one. One of them--a daily 15-minute "ticket triage" standup--had emerged late in the brainstorming session, built on top of two other ideas. Without the expansion phase, it never would have surfaced. It turned out to be the single most effective intervention. Ticket follow-up requests dropped by 60% in the first month.

Expand, then contract. The rhythm works because it separates the creative act from the evaluative act. Your brain is not good at doing both simultaneously. Give it permission to create freely, then switch modes and evaluate rigorously. You'll get better outcomes than you would from trying to generate and judge at the same time.

---

title: Principle 3: Close the Loop
summary: The third principle is what makes PIPS a *living system* rather than a one-time project.
bodyMd:
The third principle is what makes PIPS a *living system* rather than a one-time project.

The concept comes from Karl Albrecht, who in his 1980 book *Brain Power* distinguished between two types of problem solving:

**Open-loop problem solving:** You identify a problem, implement a solution, and move on. You assume the solution worked. You don't go back to check. You don't measure the outcome. You don't verify that the problem is actually resolved. This is how the overwhelming majority of problem solving works in the overwhelming majority of organizations.

**Closed-loop problem solving:** You identify a problem, implement a solution, measure the results, compare them to your baseline, learn from what you find, and feed that learning back into the system. If the solution worked, you document why and share the knowledge. If it didn't work, you figure out why and try something different. The loop closes. Step 6 feeds directly back into Step 1.

The difference between these two approaches is the difference between an organization that *solves problems* and an organization that *gets better at solving problems*. The first is an event. The second is a capability.

### Why Open-Loop Feels Normal

Open-loop problem solving persists because it maps perfectly to how organizations structure work. Work is organized into projects. Projects have start dates and end dates. You kick off the project, you do the work, you deliver the result, you close the project. Done. Move on to the next one.

This structure makes sense for many types of work. If you're building a bridge, the project ends when the bridge is built. But process improvement isn't building a bridge. Processes are living things. They exist in dynamic environments. The people executing them change. The inputs change. The context changes. A solution that works perfectly in January might be irrelevant by July because the conditions that made it effective have shifted.

Open-loop problem solving treats improvement like bridge-building. Implement the solution, declare the bridge built, move on. Closed-loop problem solving recognizes that the bridge is standing in a river that's constantly changing course, and you need to keep checking that it still connects the two banks.

### What Closing the Loop Actually Looks Like

In practical terms, closing the loop means building evaluation and follow-up into your improvement process as non-optional steps. Not "nice to have." Not "if we have time." Non-optional.

Here's what that looks like:

**At implementation (Step 5):** You define what you're going to measure, when you're going to measure it, and who is responsible for measuring it. You set a specific date for the evaluation. Not "sometime in a few months." A date. On the calendar. With someone's name next to it.

**At the evaluation date (Step 6):** You actually do it. You pull the data. You compare current state to baseline. You answer the question: "Did the solution work?" And you answer it with data, not with feelings.

Three outcomes are possible:

1. **It worked.** The data shows measurable improvement. Document what you did, why it worked, and share it. Add it to your organizational knowledge base. Celebrate. Then ask: "Is there more improvement to be had?" If yes, you're back at Step 1 with a new, refined problem statement.

2. **It partially worked.** Some improvement, but not as much as expected, or improvement in some areas but not others. This is the most common outcome, and it's valuable. Analyze why the improvement was partial. What assumptions were wrong? What factors did you not account for? Use what you've learned to refine the solution and try again.

3. **It didn't work.** No improvement, or the problem got worse. This is disappointing but not a failure. It's data. You now know that this approach doesn't solve this problem, which eliminates a hypothesis and narrows your search. Go back to your analysis (Step 2) and look at what you missed.

In all three cases, the loop closes. Learning happens. The organization is smarter than it was before. Even "failure" produces value when the loop is closed, because you've learned something you didn't know before.

### A Story About the Loop

I mentioned in Chapter 1 the IT change management process that fell apart over the course of a year. Let me tell you about a team that got it right.

A different organization, similar problem. Their software deployment process was unreliable--deployments frequently caused incidents, rollbacks were common, and the team spent an enormous amount of time on post-deployment firefighting. They used PIPS to address it.

They went through Steps 1 through 5 diligently. They identified the problem (deployment failure rate of 23%). They analyzed root causes (insufficient testing, unclear deployment procedures, lack of rollback plans). They generated solutions, selected a combination of automated testing gates, deployment checklists, and mandatory rollback procedures, and implemented them.

Here's where it gets interesting. They scheduled evaluation checkpoints at 30 days, 90 days, and 180 days. Not optional. On the calendar. With assigned owners.

At 30 days, deployment failure rate was down to 11%. Good progress, but not where they wanted to be. They dug into the remaining failures and found that most were related to database migration scripts--an area their automated testing didn't cover well. They refined the solution.

At 90 days, failure rate was at 5%. Much better. But they noticed something unexpected: deployment *velocity* had decreased. Teams were deploying less frequently because the new process added steps. Some of the steps, on closer examination, were redundant. They streamlined.

At 180 days, failure rate was at 3% and deployment frequency had actually *increased* beyond the pre-improvement baseline. Teams were deploying more often with fewer failures. The net result was dramatically more reliable software delivery at higher throughput.

None of this would have happened without the evaluation checkpoints. The 30-day refinement caught the database gap. The 90-day check caught the velocity problem. If they had implemented their initial solution and declared victory, they would have had an 11% failure rate and slower deployments--better than before, but far short of what they achieved through iteration.

The loop. Close it. Every time.

### The Third-Time Rule

The simplest version of closing the loop is a rule I've lived by throughout my career: if you've been asked to do something for a third time, build a way so that person can do it themselves. First time, help them. Second time, help them again and note the pattern. Third time? That's not a request anymore. That's a process gap. Close the loop -- build the self-service tool, write the documentation, automate the workflow, whatever it takes to make that third request the last one.

That single rule, applied consistently, eliminated more recurring work than any formal initiative I've ever run. It's Close the Loop at its most practical: don't just solve the problem. Solve the *category* of problem.

### The Never-Ending Quest

The title of this book is "The Never-Ending Quest," and the closed loop is why.

The quest never ends because there is always more to learn. Every solution creates new conditions. Every improvement reveals the next improvement. Every problem you solve brings a new problem into focus--not because things are getting worse, but because you can now see things that were previously invisible.

I know "the quest never ends" can sound exhausting. But think about it personally -- do you want to be the same person in five years? Same skills, same understanding? Of course not. You want to grow. The quest to improve yourself never ends, and you don't experience that as a burden.

Organizations work the same way. A living organization is constantly learning and adapting. A dead one has "figured it out" and stopped changing.

There's a line I keep coming back to, and I want you to sit with it:

> "Circumstances change, situations change, people change, and your preferences and values change. You must adapt your solutions to the changing times."

The solution you implemented last year was right for last year's conditions. Are conditions the same today? Maybe. Maybe not. You won't know unless you check. And checking is closing the loop.

### Why This Principle Changes Everything

Data Over Opinions governs what you base decisions on. Expand Then Contract governs how you generate and evaluate options. Close the Loop adds the time dimension -- it takes a one-time fix and turns it into an ongoing capability.

Without Close the Loop, PIPS is a good problem-solving method. With it, PIPS becomes a learning system. And learning systems are the only kind that improve over time.

Every principle matters. But if I had to pick the one that separates organizations that dabble in improvement from organizations that are genuinely excellent at it, it's this one. The best organizations I've worked with are not the ones with the smartest problem solvers. They're the ones that relentlessly close the loop. They check. They verify. They learn. They adapt. And they never, ever assume that something they fixed stays fixed.

---

title: The Three Principles Together
summary: The three principles are not independent. They reinforce each other.
bodyMd:
The three principles are not independent. They reinforce each other.

**Data Over Opinions** fuels **Close the Loop** because you can't evaluate whether something worked unless you have data. If you didn't measure the baseline before you started, you have nothing to compare against. If you're relying on opinions to judge the outcome ("yeah, it feels better"), you'll fall into the solved-it illusion every time.

**Expand Then Contract** prevents the tunnel vision that undermines **Data Over Opinions.** If you only look at one data source, or only consider one possible cause, your data analysis is incomplete even if it's technically rigorous. Expanding your search for causes and solutions before narrowing ensures that your data-driven decisions are based on a comprehensive picture, not a narrow one.

**Close the Loop** makes **Expand Then Contract** sustainable. Each time you close the loop, you learn something that informs the next cycle. The solutions you didn't select in Step 4 might become relevant when conditions change. The root causes you identified but didn't address in Step 2 might become priorities in the next cycle. The organizational knowledge accumulates.

Together, the three principles make the system rigorous (Data Over Opinions), creative (Expand Then Contract), and adaptive (Close the Loop). Drop any one and the system breaks. Rigor without creativity is brittle. Creativity without rigor is chaos. Both without adaptiveness is a system that can't learn from itself.

When you're in the middle of a PIPS project and you're not sure what to do, ask yourself three questions:

1. What does the data tell me? (Data Over Opinions)
2. Have I considered enough options? (Expand Then Contract)
3. How will I know if this worked? (Close the Loop)

If you can answer all three, you're on solid ground. If you can't answer one, that's where your attention should go.

---

title: What You Can Do Monday Morning
summary: 1. **Ask for data in your next meeting.** When someone makes an assertion--"customers are unhappy," "the process is too slow," "we don't have enough resources"--ask a simple question: "How do we know?...
bodyMd:
1. **Ask for data in your next meeting.** When someone makes an assertion--"customers are unhappy," "the process is too slow," "we don't have enough resources"--ask a simple question: "How do we know?" Not confrontationally. Curiously. "How do we know?" opens the door to data without attacking anyone's credibility. You might find out that the assertion is well-supported, which is great. Or you might find out that it's an assumption nobody has verified, which is even more useful.

2. **Practice expansion and contraction deliberately.** The next time you need to make a decision as a team, explicitly split the conversation into two phases. Say: "For the next 15 minutes, let's generate as many options as we can without evaluating any of them. Then we'll spend 15 minutes narrowing down." Set a timer. Enforce the rule. See what happens. Most teams are shocked at how many more options emerge when evaluation is temporarily suspended.

3. **Schedule one follow-up.** Pick any change, improvement, or fix your team has implemented in the past six months. Put a 30-minute meeting on the calendar, three weeks from now, to review whether it's still working. In that meeting, look at data (even informal data). Ask the people doing the work whether the improvement has held. Compare current state to pre-change state if you can. You've just closed a loop. It will feel like the most productive 30 minutes of your month.

4. **Write down the three principles somewhere you'll see them.** A sticky note on your monitor. A note in your phone. A card in your wallet. "Data Over Opinions. Expand Then Contract. Close the Loop." When you find yourself in a conversation where something feels off--where the team is going in circles, or the decision doesn't feel right, or the discussion is generating more heat than light--glance at those three principles. One of them will almost certainly tell you what's missing.

5. **Teach someone else.** Find one person on your team and explain the three principles in your own words. You don't need to call it PIPS. You don't need to reference this book. Just share the ideas. "I've been thinking about how we solve problems, and there are three concepts I think could help us." Teaching is the fastest way to internalize a concept, and it starts building the common language that makes PIPS powerful at the organizational level.

title: Step 1: Identify
summary: There's a quote I come back to every time I kick off a PIPS engagement. Charles Kettering, the prolific inventor and head of research at General Motors for nearly three decades, said it simply:
bodyMd:
# Chapter 4: Step 1 - Identify & Select the Problem

## "What do we want to change?"

---

There's a quote I come back to every time I kick off a PIPS engagement. Charles Kettering, the prolific inventor and head of research at General Motors for nearly three decades, said it simply:

> "A problem well stated is a problem half solved."

Half solved. Just by stating the problem correctly. That sounds too good to be true, doesn't it? But after years of facilitating PIPS teams across industries, I can tell you Kettering was being conservative. A problem well stated is more than half the battle. It determines whether the entire effort that follows will produce lasting results or waste everyone's time.

And here's the uncomfortable truth: most teams are terrible at stating problems.

Not because they're unintelligent. Not because they lack experience. But because the human brain is wired to skip ahead. We see something broken, and we immediately start imagining fixes. We feel pain, and we reach for relief. It's instinct. And in daily life, it serves us well. But in organizational problem-solving, that instinct will sabotage you every single time.
