- Less spontaneous than freewheeling
- People may tune out between turns
- Harder to build on ideas in real time

**Facilitation tip:** After each round, create a brief "build" window. "Before we start the next round, does anyone want to build on something they just heard?" This preserves the structured equality of round-robin while allowing some of the spontaneous energy of freewheeling.

### Method 3: Slip Method (Written/Anonymous)

Each team member writes their ideas on slips of paper or sticky notes, one idea per slip. After a defined time period (usually 5 to 10 minutes), all slips are collected and displayed on a wall or board. Ideas are read aloud and grouped by theme.

**When to use it:**
- The topic is sensitive or politically charged
- The team has significant power imbalances
- You want the most honest, unfiltered ideas possible
- Individual thinking before group discussion will produce better results

**Advantages:**
- Complete anonymity removes social pressure
- Eliminates anchoring (early ideas influencing later ones)
- Introverts can contribute as effectively as extroverts
- Parallel generation: everyone thinks simultaneously, multiplying the time efficiency

**Risks:**
- No real-time building on ideas
- Less energy and dynamism than verbal methods
- Handwriting can be hard to read (use markers, print clearly)
- Requires more physical setup (supplies, wall space)

**Facilitation tip:** After displaying all the slips, run a brief verbal session to build on the written ideas. "Now that you've seen everyone's ideas, what new thoughts come to mind?" This hybrid approach captures the honesty of written brainstorming and the generative energy of verbal brainstorming.

### Choosing Your Method

Here's my decision framework:

| Situation | Recommended Method |
|---|---|
| High-trust, experienced team | Freewheeling |
| Mixed seniority levels | Round-Robin |
| Sensitive or controversial topic | Slip Method |
| New team, first brainstorm together | Round-Robin (builds confidence) |
| Team tends to socialize and go off-track | Slip Method (focused, quiet) |
| Remote or hybrid team | Slip Method (digital sticky notes) |
| Follow-up session after initial brainstorm | Freewheeling (building on existing ideas) |

In practice, I often combine methods within a single session. Start with the slip method for five minutes to prime the pump, then switch to freewheeling to build energy, then finish with a round-robin to make sure nothing was left unsaid.

---

title: Brainwriting: When Writing Beats Talking
summary: Sometimes the best brainstorming doesn't involve speaking at all. Brainwriting is a family of techniques where ideas are generated in writing and shared through structured exchange. It's not a replace...
bodyMd:
Sometimes the best brainstorming doesn't involve speaking at all. Brainwriting is a family of techniques where ideas are generated in writing and shared through structured exchange. It's not a replacement for brainstorming. It's a complement that works better in specific situations.

I use brainwriting when any of these four conditions are present:

1. **More thoughtful ideas are needed.** Writing forces people to slow down and develop their thoughts more fully than the rapid-fire pace of verbal brainstorming allows.
2. **Sessions are dominated by loud voices.** Even with the best facilitation, some people will always talk more than others. Brainwriting eliminates the loudness advantage entirely.
3. **The group socializes too much.** Some teams are so socially comfortable with each other that brainstorming sessions devolve into conversations, jokes, and tangents. Brainwriting imposes productive silence.
4. **The topic is controversial or sensitive.** When ideas might trigger defensive reactions, the written format provides a buffer that allows more honest and daring contributions.

### Approach 1: 6-3-5 Table Exchange

This is the most structured brainwriting method. Six people sit around a table. Each person has a worksheet with three columns. In the first round, each person writes three ideas in the top row (one per column). After five minutes, everyone passes their sheet to the person on their left. In the next round, each person reads the three ideas on the sheet they received and writes three new ideas in the next row, inspired by (but not duplicating) what they've read. Pass again after five minutes. Continue until all six rows are filled.

The math: 6 people x 6 rounds x 3 ideas per round = 108 ideas in 30 minutes. That's an extraordinary volume. And because each round is triggered by reading someone else's ideas, the building-on-ideas principle is baked into the structure.

**Practical notes:**
- You don't need exactly six people. Adjust the numbers as needed. Four people writing two ideas per round in four-minute intervals works fine.
- The five-minute timer is important. It creates just enough pressure to keep people writing without rushing.
- Some rounds will produce only one or two ideas instead of three. That's fine. Quality doesn't have to be uniform across every cell.

### Approach 2: Card Passing

A simpler variant. Each person writes one idea on an index card and passes it to the right. The next person reads the card, writes a new idea inspired by it on a fresh card, and passes both cards to the right. Continue for a set number of rounds.

This approach is less structured than 6-3-5 but more accessible. It works well with larger groups where a full table exchange would be unwieldy.

### Approach 3: Gallery Method

Each person writes their ideas on a large sheet of paper (flip chart size) posted on the wall. After an initial generation period of 10 to 15 minutes, everyone gets up and walks around the room reading everyone else's ideas. They add new ideas, comments, or builds to any sheet they're inspired by, using a different colored marker.

The Gallery Method has a physical, kinesthetic quality that changes the energy in the room. Getting people out of their chairs and moving around resets attention and stimulates different thinking. I've found it particularly effective after lunch, when energy is low, or during long sessions when the team needs a format change.

---

title: When Brainstorming Stalls
summary: Every brainstorming session hits a wall. The initial burst of ideas slows down. People start staring at the whiteboard. Someone checks their phone. This is normal, and it's not a sign to stop. It's a ...
bodyMd:
Every brainstorming session hits a wall. The initial burst of ideas slows down. People start staring at the whiteboard. Someone checks their phone. This is normal, and it's not a sign to stop. It's a sign that the obvious ideas have been exhausted, and the team needs a prompt to think differently.

Alex Osborn developed a set of nine "idea-spurring questions" for exactly this moment. I keep them on a card in my facilitation kit, and I pull them out whenever a session loses momentum.

### Osborn's 9 Idea-Spurring Questions

1. **Put to other uses?** Can the existing process, tool, or resource be used for something it wasn't designed for? What else could serve this purpose?

2. **Adapt?** What else is like this? What parallel could I draw? Has someone in another industry solved a similar problem?

3. **Modify?** What if we changed one aspect? Different sequence? Different timing? Different format?

4. **Magnify?** What if we did more of something? More often? Larger scale? More detail? More time?

5. **Minify?** What if we did less? Smaller? Fewer steps? Less time? Simpler? What can we eliminate entirely?

6. **Substitute?** What can we use instead? Different person? Different process? Different technology? Different supplier?

7. **Rearrange?** What if we changed the order? Swapped roles? Reorganized the layout? Changed the schedule?

8. **Reverse?** What if we did the opposite? What if we turned it inside out? What if the customer did it instead of us? What if we started at the end and worked backward?

9. **Combine?** What if we merged two ideas? Two processes? Two roles? What if we bundled or integrated?

I don't use all nine in every session. I pick the ones that feel most relevant to the problem at hand and throw them out one at a time when the energy dips. "We've got a good list. Now let me challenge you: What if we reversed the process? What would it look like if the last step happened first?" That kind of prompt can unlock a whole new line of thinking.

---

title: Interviewing and Surveying
summary: Brainstorming and brainwriting generate ideas from the team. But the team doesn't have all the answers. Sometimes the best solution ideas come from outside the room.
bodyMd:
Brainstorming and brainwriting generate ideas from the team. But the team doesn't have all the answers. Sometimes the best solution ideas come from outside the room.

### Interviewing

Interviews are structured conversations with individuals who have knowledge, experience, or perspective relevant to your problem. They're particularly valuable when:

- Subject matter experts exist outside your team
- Frontline workers experience the problem differently than managers
- Customers or stakeholders have insights your team lacks
- Similar problems have been solved in other departments or organizations

I recommend semi-structured interviews: prepare five to seven open-ended questions in advance, but allow the conversation to follow interesting threads. The goal is to surface ideas you wouldn't have generated internally.

**Key questions to include:**
- "If you could change one thing about [the process/system/workflow], what would it be?"
- "What workarounds do you currently use?"
- "Have you seen this done better somewhere else? What was different?"
- "What would you try if you had no constraints?"

That last question is especially powerful. It gives people permission to dream, and dreams often contain the seeds of practical solutions.

Record the ideas generated during interviews and bring them back to the team. They become additional inputs for the solution pool, alongside the brainstormed and brainwritten ideas.

### Surveying

Surveys cast a wider net than interviews. They're useful when you want input from a larger population than you can interview individually. Use surveys when:

- The problem affects many people across the organization
- You want to quantify the frequency of certain ideas or preferences
- Anonymous input is important for honesty
- You need to demonstrate broad input for stakeholder buy-in

Design your survey with a mix of closed-ended questions (for quantifiable data) and open-ended questions (for idea generation). Keep it short. Ten questions maximum. Every question beyond ten reduces your response rate.

The open-ended questions are where the gold is for Step 3. "What ideas do you have for reducing [the problem]?" will generate responses you'd never get in a room of eight people. When Meridian surveyed their 22-person AR team plus 15 practice liaisons, they received ideas from people who would never speak up in a meeting but had been quietly thinking about solutions for months.

---

title: Running Case Study: Meridian Health Systems
summary: The Meridian team enters Step 3 with clear focus. Their verified root causes are:
bodyMd:
The Meridian team enters Step 3 with clear focus. Their verified root causes are:

1. **Coding errors** (42% of denials), amplified by new coder inexperience (nearly double the error rate in first 90 days)
2. **Authorization gaps** (28% of denials), driven by manual tracking and no automated alerts

I design a two-part generation session. Part one addresses coding errors. Part two addresses authorization gaps. For each part, the prompt is simple: "How might we reduce [this root cause]?"

### Part 1: Coding Error Solutions

We start with the slip method to get individual thinking on paper. Five minutes, one idea per sticky note, as many notes as each person can produce. Then we shift to freewheeling to build on what we see.

The ideas start flowing. Some are practical and immediately actionable. Some are ambitious. A few are deliberately wild, which is exactly what I want.

Here's the full list, as it appeared on our wall:

1. Structured 90-day onboarding program for new coders
2. Buddy system pairing new coders with experienced ones
3. Pre-submission claim scrubber tool (software that flags errors before submission)
4. Real-time coding feedback system (errors flagged as they're entered)
5. Standardized coding workflow (single documented process for all coders)
6. Coding cheat sheets for the top 20 most common error types
7. Weekly coding accuracy reports by individual (not punitive, for self-awareness)
8. Monthly "coding challenge" to gamify accuracy improvement
9. Cross-training: have coders shadow billers and vice versa for one week
10. Dedicated coding quality lead role (someone who reviews before submission)
11. Upgrade coding software to the version that flags 19 of 23 common errors
12. Daily 15-minute "coding huddle" to share tips and recent denial patterns
13. Decision tree reference guides for complex coding scenarios
14. Peer review: have a second coder verify claims over $5,000

Then things get interesting.

15. Outsource all coding to AI (this gets a laugh)
16. Stop accepting patients with complex insurance plans (this gets a bigger laugh)

But I don't let the laughs die. "Hold on," I say. "What's the kernel in those ideas? What if we didn't outsource all coding to AI, but used AI to assist coders with complex cases? And what about the insurance complexity angle? Is there something there?"

The team picks up the thread:

17. AI-assisted coding suggestions for complex claim types
18. Pre-authorization specialist role for high-complexity payers
19. Payer complexity tiers, with extra review required for Tier 1 (most complex) payers
20. Automated second check for any claim exceeding a dollar threshold or complexity score

That wild idea about outsourcing to AI sparked a practical conversation about AI-assisted coding. The joke about refusing complex patients led to the genuinely useful concept of a pre-authorization specialist. This is "easier to tame than to invent" in action.

### Part 2: Authorization Gap Solutions

For this round, I use round-robin to ensure the practice liaison and IT representative, who've been quieter, get equal airtime. Their perspective on authorization workflows is more relevant than anyone else's in the room.

21. Automated authorization tracking dashboard (replacing the spreadsheet)
22. Automated alerts when authorizations are within 30 days of expiration
23. Real-time eligibility verification at time of patient scheduling
24. Dedicated authorization follow-up specialist role
25. Weekly authorization status reports sent to practice offices
26. Integration between payer portals and Meridian's billing system for auto-updates
27. Standardized authorization request template to reduce back-and-forth
28. Monthly payer rule update digest compiled and distributed to all coders

### The Final Count

Twenty-eight solutions in total. The team generated them in under two hours. That's not counting the variations and combinations that emerged in the building-on-ideas discussions. The real number of distinct ideas considered was closer to 40, but we consolidated the closely related ones.

Some of these solutions are small and cheap: coding cheat sheets, daily huddles, standardized workflows. Some are medium investments: buddy system, quality lead role, software upgrade. And some are significant: AI-assisted coding, automated authorization dashboard, payer portal integration.

All of them are now candidates for evaluation. But not today. Today, we admire the wall full of possibilities. Today, every idea is equally valid. Tomorrow, in Step 4, we'll bring out the criteria and start making hard choices.

But I'll tell you this: the solution that Meridian eventually implements, the one that drops their denial rate from 38% to 19% in five months, was born from one of those "wild" ideas. The team never would have gotten there if someone had said "that'll never work" at the moment it was first spoken.

---

title: The Tools of Step 3
summary: **Freewheeling**: Open, spontaneous idea generation. Best for high-trust, experienced teams. Fast and energetic but risks dominance by loud voices.
bodyMd:
### Brainstorming (Three Methods)

**Freewheeling**: Open, spontaneous idea generation. Best for high-trust, experienced teams. Fast and energetic but risks dominance by loud voices.

**Round-Robin**: Structured turn-taking. Best for mixed-seniority groups or teams new to brainstorming. Ensures equal participation but can feel slower.

**Slip Method**: Written, anonymous idea generation. Best for sensitive topics or teams with power imbalances. Produces honest ideas but lacks real-time building energy.

### Brainwriting (Three Approaches)

**6-3-5 Table Exchange**: Highly structured written generation where sheets rotate around a table. Produces high volume (potentially 100+ ideas in 30 minutes). Best for groups of 4 to 8.

**Card Passing**: Simpler written exchange using index cards. Good for larger groups. Each card carries one idea that inspires the next.

**Gallery Method**: Ideas written on wall-mounted sheets, with team members walking and adding to each other's ideas. Kinesthetic and visual. Good for breaking up long sessions.

### Interviewing

Semi-structured conversations with subject matter experts, frontline workers, customers, or external contacts. Five to seven prepared questions with freedom to follow threads. Brings outside perspective into the team's solution pool.

### Surveying

Broad-reach data collection through questionnaires. Mix of closed-ended (quantifiable) and open-ended (generative) questions. Ten questions maximum. Anonymous option increases honesty. Useful when the affected population is larger than the team.

---

title: What You Can Do Monday Morning
summary: 1. **Run a 10-minute brainstorm on any current challenge using the slip method.** Give your team sticky notes and a prompt. Five minutes of silent writing, then post them all on a wall and read them t...
bodyMd:
1. **Run a 10-minute brainstorm on any current challenge using the slip method.** Give your team sticky notes and a prompt. Five minutes of silent writing, then post them all on a wall and read them together. No discussion of feasibility. Just observe the range of ideas. You'll be surprised at what surfaces when people write anonymously.

2. **Print Osborn's 9 idea-spurring questions and keep them visible.** The next time a brainstorming session stalls (and it will), pull one out and ask it. "What if we reversed the process?" or "What can we substitute?" can restart the flow immediately.

3. **Catch yourself (and others) evaluating during idea generation.** For one week, pay attention to how often you or your colleagues say "Yeah, but," "That won't work," "We tried that," or "That's too expensive" when ideas are being shared. Just notice it. Awareness is the first step toward breaking the habit.

4. **Interview one person outside your team about a problem you're working on.** Ask them: "What would you try if you had no constraints?" Their answer won't be your solution, but it might contain the seed of one. The best ideas often come from people who see the problem from a different angle.

5. **Embrace a wild idea.** The next time someone in a meeting suggests something that seems absurd or impractical, instead of dismissing it, ask: "What's the kernel of truth in that?" or "What would a practical version of that look like?" Practice taming ideas instead of killing them. You might be surprised at what you find.

title: Step 4: Select & Plan
summary: *"What's the best way to do it?"*
bodyMd:
# Chapter 7: Step 4 -- Select & Plan the Solution

*"What's the best way to do it?"*

---

If Step 3 was about opening the floodgates, Step 4 is about building the dam. You've brainstormed. You've brainwritten. You've hitchhiked on each other's ideas and generated a list of potential solutions that probably feels overwhelming. Good. That means Step 3 worked.

Now comes the most tool-intensive step in the entire PIPS process. This is where the rubber meets the road -- where a creative list of possibilities turns into a concrete plan with names, dates, roles, and contingencies attached. Step 4 has two major sub-phases, and both matter equally: **Selection** (choosing what to do) and **Planning** (figuring out how to do it). Skip either one and you're setting yourself up for trouble.

Let me walk you through how this works.


## Two Phases, One Step

I want to be explicit about why selection and planning live in the same step. In my experience, organizations that separate these into two events -- "let's pick a solution this week, and plan it next month" -- lose momentum. The energy and context from the selection discussion is still fresh when you move into planning. The people who argued for a particular solution are sitting right there and can explain the implementation nuances. The concerns that came up during evaluation become the foundation of your contingency plans.

Do them together. One step, two phases.

**Phase 1 -- Selection:** Reduce the list. Evaluate. Score. Reach consensus.

**Phase 2 -- Planning:** Build the implementation plan. Assign roles. Define contingencies. Prepare for management presentation if needed.

Both phases follow the expand-then-contract rhythm we've been using, though by this point, you're deep into convergence. In the selection phase, you're expanding by generating lots of criteria and exploring lots of evaluation angles. Then you're contracting by scoring, ranking, and converging on a single solution (or a small set of complementary solutions). In the planning phase, you're expanding by thinking through all the possible implementation actions, obstacles, and contingencies. Then you're contracting by committing to a specific plan with specific dates and specific people.


## The Selection Process

Here's the step-by-step process for getting from "a big list of ideas" to "a solution we all believe in." I'll give you the overview first, then we'll dig into each piece.

### Step 1: Reduce the List with a Broad Filter

Start by eliminating ideas that clearly don't deserve further discussion. The filter is simple: *"Does this idea deserve further consideration?"* You're not asking whether it's the best idea. You're asking whether it should stay on the table at all.

Common filters include:
- **Feasibility:** Can we actually do this with the resources we have?
- **Time:** Can we implement this within a reasonable timeframe?
- **Control:** Do we have the authority to make this change, or does it require someone else's approval that we're unlikely to get?
- **Cost:** Is this within the range of what the organization would spend on this problem?

Don't agonize over borderline cases at this stage. If there's any real debate about whether an idea should stay, keep it. You'll have more rigorous tools for the next round. This first pass is about clearing the obvious non-starters so you can focus your energy on the real contenders.

### Step 2: Pros and Cons

For each remaining idea, do a quick round of pros and cons. Here's a rule I've found invaluable: **the "one breath" rule.** Each person states a pro or a con in one breath. Not a speech. Not a three-minute argument. One breath.

This keeps the discussion focused and prevents any single voice from dominating. You go around the room: "What's a pro?" One breath. "What's a con?" One breath. Next idea.

Write them down. Use a balance sheet format -- pros on the left, cons on the right. This isn't the same as a force field analysis, by the way. A balance sheet is a quick tool for documenting the arguments for and against a particular choice. It helps the group move toward a decision. Force field analysis is an analytical tool that examines the driving and restraining forces around a situation. Both are useful, but they serve different purposes at different moments.

### Step 3: Combine Like Ideas

This is the step people skip, and it costs them. Look at your remaining list and ask: "Are any of these really the same idea expressed differently?" Or better yet: "Could any of these be combined into a more powerful solution?"

Think of it like a grocery list. If someone wrote "apples" and someone else wrote "fruit," you don't need both items. If one person suggested "weekly training sessions" and another suggested "a mentoring program," those might be different -- or they might be two components of a single onboarding improvement initiative.

Number the first idea on your list. Compare the second idea to it. If they're similar, group them together under the same number. If they're different, give the second idea a new number. Continue through the list. You'll often find that twenty ideas reduce to eight or ten distinct concepts.

### Step 4: Get to Six or Fewer

Here's a practical truth: most groups can meaningfully evaluate about six options. More than that and the discussion becomes unwieldy, the scoring takes too long, and people disengage. If your combined list still has more than six ideas, apply another round of filtering. This time, use tighter criteria -- not just "does it deserve consideration" but "is this realistic given our constraints?"

If you can't get below six through filtering alone, use weighted voting (which I'll explain shortly) to identify the top six.

### Step 5: Apply Criteria Rating

Now you're ready for the most structured tool in the selection process. This is where you move from discussion to disciplined evaluation.

### Step 6: Reach Consensus

And here's where you turn that evaluation into a commitment. Not a vote. A consensus.

Let me take you through the critical tools for Steps 5 and 6 in detail.


## Scoring Systems: Making the Invisible Visible

The whole point of scoring is to make your decision-making process transparent. When a team picks a solution based on gut feeling, or because the loudest person in the room advocated for it, or because it was the first idea that came up, they've made a decision -- but they can't explain it, defend it, or learn from it. Scoring systems force the team to articulate *why* one solution is better than another, using criteria that everyone agreed to in advance.

I want to walk through four different scoring approaches. They're all legitimate. Which one you use depends on the situation, the team, and the nature of the decision.

### The Criteria Rating Form

This is the workhorse tool of Step 4. If you learn one scoring method, make it this one.

**How it works:**

1. **Select your criteria.** What matters for this decision? Common criteria include: Cost to implement, Time to implement, Expected impact on the problem, Sustainability over time, Resource requirements, Degree of control (can we do this ourselves?), Appropriateness (does this actually address the root cause?), and Acceptability (will the people affected support this change?).

2. **Assign weights.** Not all criteria are equally important. A weight of 1.0 means standard importance. Use 0.5 for criteria that matter but aren't critical. Use 1.5 or 2.0 for criteria that are make-or-break. The rule of thumb is to keep weights between 0.5 and 2.0 -- anything beyond that range tends to overwhelm the other criteria mathematically.

3. **Rate each solution against each criterion on a 1-5 scale.** 1 means poor, 5 means excellent. Important nuance: for criteria where lower is better (like cost or risk), reverse the scale so that 5 means "low cost" or "low risk." This keeps the math consistent -- higher scores always mean better.

4. **Multiply each rating by the criterion's weight.**

5. **Sum the weighted scores for each solution.**

6. **Discuss the results.**

That last step is the one that matters most. Let me say it plainly: **the Criteria Rating Worksheet guides your discussion. It does not make your decision.** If the math says Solution B wins by two points, but the team has a strong feeling that Solution A is the right answer, that's a conversation worth having. Maybe there's a criterion you didn't include. Maybe the weights need adjusting. Maybe someone has information that changes a rating. The worksheet is a mirror -- it reflects the team's thinking back to them in a structured format. It's not a calculator that spits out an answer.

**Worked example:**

Suppose you're evaluating three solutions and you've agreed on four criteria:

| Criterion | Weight | Solution A | Weighted A | Solution B | Weighted B | Solution C | Weighted C |
|-----------|--------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|
| Cost (5=low) | 1.5 | 4 | 6.0 | 2 | 3.0 | 3 | 4.5 |
| Time to Implement | 1.0 | 3 | 3.0 | 4 | 4.0 | 2 | 2.0 |
| Expected Impact | 2.0 | 3 | 6.0 | 5 | 10.0 | 4 | 8.0 |
| Sustainability | 1.0 | 4 | 4.0 | 3 | 3.0 | 5 | 5.0 |
| **Total** | | | **19.0** | | **20.0** | | **19.5** |

Solutions B and C are nearly tied, with A close behind. This doesn't mean B wins automatically. It means B scored highest, and now you have a data-driven starting point for discussion. Why did B score so high on impact? Is that rating justified? Is B's low cost rating a dealbreaker? That's the conversation.

### Weighted Voting

This is faster and less formal than criteria rating. Good for situations where you need a quick read on group preferences.

**How it works:** Each team member gets a number of votes equal to **1.5 times the number of options.** If you have six solutions on the board, each person gets nine votes. They can distribute those votes however they want -- put all nine on one option, spread them evenly, or any combination in between.

**Four procedural tips that make this work:**

1. **Spread your votes.** Encourage people not to dump all votes on one option. The point is to capture degrees of preference, not just first choice.
2. **Decide before recording.** Everyone should decide their vote allocation before anyone records. This prevents anchoring.
3. **Show fingers.** Have everyone hold up their vote count simultaneously for each option. This prevents people from changing their votes based on what they see others doing.
4. **Record by option, not by person.** Write the total votes for Solution A, then Solution B, etc. Don't write "Marc gave 5 votes to A." The results should be collective, not individual.

Key insight: Weighted voting **does not make decisions. It merely gives information.** It tells you where the group's energy is. A solution that gets zero votes from eight people is probably not worth pursuing. A solution that gets at least some votes from everyone might be a unifying choice even if it didn't get the most total votes.

### Paired Comparisons

This method forces the group to make head-to-head choices between every pair of options. It's particularly useful when alternatives seem roughly equal and the group is stuck.

**How it works:** Take every possible pair of solutions and ask: "If we could only do A or B, which would we choose?" Record the winner of each comparison. The solution that wins the most head-to-head matchups is the group's preference.

The number of comparisons grows quickly. For 4 options, you have 6 comparisons. For 6 options, you have 15. For 8 options, you have 28. This is one reason to get your list to six or fewer before applying this tool.

The power of paired comparisons is that it forces the group to choose even when alternatives seem equal. "I like both" isn't an option. You have to pick one. This reveals hidden preferences and often breaks ties that weighted voting couldn't resolve.

### Point-Scoring System

This is a variation on criteria rating where instead of using a fixed 1-5 scale, you allocate an arbitrary number of points (100 or 1000) among your criteria based on their importance, then distribute points within each criterion across your options.

**Worked example with a 1000-point budget:**

Your criteria and their point allocations (totaling 1000):
- Expected Impact: 300 points
- Cost: 200 points
- Implementation Speed: 150 points
- Sustainability: 200 points
- Team Acceptability: 150 points

For each criterion, distribute its points across the solutions based on how well each solution meets that criterion. If Solution A has much higher expected impact than B or C, it might get 150 of the 300 impact points while B gets 100 and C gets 50.

Sum the points for each solution. The one with the most total points is the mathematical favorite -- and, like every other scoring method, it's a starting point for discussion, not a final verdict.

Some teams find the point-allocation approach more intuitive than the weight-times-rating math of criteria rating. Use whichever resonates with your group.


## Consensus: Not What You Think It Means

Let me clear up a misconception that I've seen trip up more teams than any other single issue in the PIPS process.

Consensus does not mean unanimity. It does not mean everyone's first choice wins. It does not mean we discuss until every person is enthusiastically on board. That's a recipe for paralysis, and it gives veto power to the most stubborn person in the room.

The definition I use comes from William Ouchi's *Theory Z* (1981), his influential work on how American organizations could adopt Japanese management principles, and it's the most practical definition of consensus I've ever encountered. Consensus is reached when **each member of the group can honestly say three things:**

1. **"I believe that you understand my point of view."**
2. **"I believe that I understand your point of view."**
3. **"Whether or not I prefer this decision, I will support it because it was reached openly and fairly."**

Read that third statement again. "Whether or not I prefer this decision." That's the key. You don't have to love the decision. You don't have to think it's the best possible choice. You have to believe the process was fair, that your perspective was heard and understood, and that you're willing to support the outcome.

This is why consensus is more about **listening** than about **talking.** If I'm trying to convince you that my solution is better, I'm talking. If I'm trying to understand why you favor a different solution so that I can honestly say "I believe that I understand your point of view," I'm listening. PIPS consensus is built on listening.

### The Three Verification Questions

As a facilitator, you need a practical way to check whether the group has actually reached consensus or whether people are just tired and agreeing to end the meeting. At every decision point -- not just the final solution selection, but every significant decision in Steps 1 through 6 -- ask these three questions:

1. **"Does everyone accept this decision?"**
2. **"Is there any opposition to this decision?"**
3. **"Can everyone support the decision?"**

These aren't redundant. Question 1 tests for passive agreement. Question 2 explicitly invites dissent -- it's permission to object. Question 3 tests for active commitment. Someone might accept a decision (question 1) and not actively oppose it (question 2) but still not be willing to support it (question 3). That matters. If someone can't say yes to all three, the consensus isn't real, and you need more discussion.

I've seen teams where one person stayed silent through the entire evaluation, and the facilitator said "Great, we have consensus" and moved on. That person went back to their desk and quietly undermined the solution for six months. Not maliciously -- they genuinely believed it was the wrong approach. But nobody asked, and they didn't feel comfortable volunteering their opposition. The three verification questions prevent this. They create explicit, structured space for dissent.

### Test for Reasonableness

Before you invest time in detailed planning, pause. Step back. Ask the group: "Does this pass the common-sense test?"

You've just been through a rigorous evaluation process with scoring worksheets and criteria weights and mathematical totals. It's possible to get so deep into the mechanics that you lose sight of practical reality. The solution with the highest score might be technically sound but culturally impossible. It might require a technology the organization has already tried and abandoned. It might depend on a vendor relationship that's deteriorating.

This is a quick gut-check, not a formal process. Two minutes, not two hours. "Looking at this with fresh eyes, does this make sense as the thing we're going to commit our time and resources to?" If the answer is yes, move to planning. If something feels off, name it and discuss it.


## Cost-Benefit Analysis and Balance Sheets

For solutions that involve significant cost, you'll need a more formal analysis than the criteria rating provides. A cost-benefit analysis doesn't have to be an elaborate financial model. At its core, it's a structured comparison of what the solution will cost versus what it will return.

**Costs** include: direct financial expenditure, staff time, opportunity cost (what else could those resources be doing?), disruption during implementation, and training time.

**Benefits** include: direct financial savings, time savings, error reduction, quality improvement, staff satisfaction, and customer satisfaction.

Not everything needs to be dollarized. Some benefits are hard to quantify -- like "reduced frustration" or "better team morale." Include them anyway. A balance sheet with some qualitative entries is more honest and useful than one that pretends everything can be expressed in currency.

The balance sheet is the pros-and-cons format applied specifically to the cost-benefit question. It's a decision support tool, not a decision-making tool. The team still decides. The balance sheet makes the tradeoffs visible.


## Phase 2: Planning the Implementation

You've selected your solution. The group has reached genuine consensus. Now you need a plan that will actually work in the real world.

I use a six-item checklist for implementation planning. Every plan needs to address all six. Skip one and you'll find out about it later, usually at the worst possible time.

### The 6-Item Implementation Planning Checklist

#### 1. Sequential Steps

Divide the solution into sequential, easily manageable steps. Each step becomes a reference point for tracking progress. If you can't break it into steps, the solution is either too simple to need a plan (just do it) or too vague to implement (go back and define it better).

Think about dependencies. What has to happen before something else can start? What can happen in parallel? This is where Gantt charts and PERT charts earn their keep -- they make the sequence and dependencies visible.

#### 2. Clear Roles

Every task needs a name next to it. Not a department. Not "the team." A specific person who is accountable for that specific deliverable. I'm a fan of the RACI matrix for this: for each task, define who is **Responsible** (doing the work), who is **Accountable** (making sure it gets done), who needs to be **Consulted** (their input is needed before action), and who needs to be **Informed** (they need to know what happened after the fact).

The accountability column is the important one. There should be exactly one name in the Accountable column for each task. If there are two names, nobody's actually accountable.

#### 3. Commitment Strategy

This is where you think explicitly about who needs to support this solution and how you'll get -- and keep -- their support.

There's a distinction worth understanding here: **passive commitment** versus **active commitment.** Passive commitment means "I won't stand in the way." Active commitment means "I'll help make this happen." You need to know which type you need from each stakeholder, because the strategies are different.

For passive commitment, information is usually enough. Keep people in the loop. Let them know what's changing and when. For active commitment, you need involvement. Ask for their input. Give them a role. Make them part of the solution, not a bystander watching it happen.

#### 4. Control System

How will you know if the plan is on track? Define the monitoring mechanism before you start implementing. What metrics will you watch? How often will you check them? Who reviews the data? What constitutes "off track" enough to trigger a response?

Keep it simple. A weekly status meeting where the team reviews three or four key metrics is often enough. The control system doesn't have to be sophisticated. It has to be consistent.

#### 5. Data Collection

You need data for two reasons: to monitor implementation (Is the plan on track?) and to evaluate results (Did the solution work?). Define both data collection processes now, during planning, not after implementation has started.

Think about what data you'll need in Step 6 to determine whether the problem has been solved. Then work backward: what collection mechanisms need to be in place from day one to capture that data? If you wait until Step 6 to think about data, you'll find that the data you need was never collected.

#### 6. Contingency Plans

Things will go wrong. The question isn't whether -- it's what you'll do when they do. Contingency planning is about thinking through the "what ifs" before they happen, so you're not making critical decisions under pressure.

Here are seven questions to guide your contingency planning. Notice that each question is framed for both problems AND opportunities -- because contingencies aren't just about bad things happening. Sometimes good things happen that you need to be ready for.

1. **What specific problems (or opportunities) may occur?**
2. **How probable are they?**
3. **How much impact would they have if they occurred?**
4. **What can be done to prevent these potential problems from occurring (or to allow these opportunities to occur)?**
5. **How will you know that the problem (or opportunity) has occurred?**
6. **What contingent action will most decrease (or increase) the detrimental (or beneficial) effects if the problem (or opportunity) occurs?**
7. **How will you deal with unanticipated problems (or opportunities)?**

That seventh question is the one most people forget. You can't plan for everything. But you can plan for the fact that unplanned things will happen. At minimum, define a process: "If something unexpected occurs, we call an emergency team meeting within 24 hours to assess and respond." That's a contingency plan for the unplannable.

The dual framing -- problems and opportunities -- matters more than you might think. I've seen teams that planned beautifully for failure but had no plan for success. What if Phase 1 finishes two weeks early? Can you pull Phase 2 forward? Do you have the resources staged? If the solution works better than expected and demand increases, can you scale? These are good problems to have, but they're still problems if you're not ready for them.


## Getting Management Approval

Not every solution needs management approval. If your team has the authority and the resources to implement the solution, do it. But there are three situations where you'll need to go up the chain:

1. **The manager has reserved decision-making authority** for this type of change.
2. **The solution affects other departments** beyond your team's scope.
3. **The cost requires budget approval** that exceeds your authority.

If you find yourself in any of these situations, you need to prepare a management presentation. And I've watched enough of these go sideways to offer some hard-won advice.

### Eight Principles for Management Presentations

1. **Understand your audience.** What does this manager care about? What metrics do they watch? What concerns keep them up at night? Frame your solution in terms of their priorities, not yours.

2. **Keep it simple, clear, and to the point.** Cover five things: problem background, critical facts, your solution, what you need from them, and a summary. That's it. Resist the urge to walk them through every fishbone diagram and criteria rating worksheet from the last three months. They don't need the process. They need the result.

3. **Use visual aids and examples.** A chart showing the before-and-after numbers. A simple Gantt chart showing the timeline. A one-page cost-benefit summary. Visuals communicate faster and more memorably than words.

4. **Personalize it.** Connect the problem to things the audience has expressed concern about. If the VP has been talking about customer satisfaction, show how your solution impacts customer satisfaction.

5. **Be prepared.** Divide the presentation among team members. Each person owns a segment. This demonstrates that the whole team is invested, not just one champion.

6. **Practice.** Run through it at least once. Get feedback from someone outside the team. Anticipate questions, especially the hard ones.

7. **Use your time well.** If you have 30 minutes, use 20 for the presentation and leave 10 for questions. Don't go over time. Going over time signals that you can't manage a plan.

8. **Plan the logistics.** Give advance notice. Send an agenda. Book the room. Test the projector. Arrange seating. These details matter because they signal professionalism and respect for the audience's time.

### Presentation Structure

Follow this flow and you'll cover what matters:

- **Introduction:** Get their attention. Tell them what you're going to tell them.
- **Problem Statement:** What is the problem and why does it matter?
- **Impact:** What is this problem costing the organization?
- **Solution:** What are you proposing?
- **Benefits:** What will the organization gain?
- **Request:** What specifically do you need from them? (Approval? Budget? Resources? Timeline?)
- **Summary:** Reinforce the key points.

Anticipate objections and prepare responses. If the most likely pushback is "this costs too much," have the cost-benefit analysis ready. If it's "we tried something like this before," be ready to explain what's different this time.


## Meridian Health Systems: Selecting and Planning

Let's return to Meridian Health Systems. The accounts receivable team has been working through the PIPS process to address their 38% claim denial rate, with a target of reducing it to 15%.

In Step 3, the team generated 28 potential solutions through brainstorming and brainwriting. It was a productive session -- ideas ranged from quick fixes ("update the payer reference guide weekly") to major system overhauls ("replace the entire claims management platform"). Now they need to turn that list into action.

### The Selection Process

**Round 1: Broad Filter.** The team applied the feasibility-cost-control filter and eliminated 12 ideas immediately. "Replace the claims management platform" was a $2 million project that required C-suite approval and a two-year timeline -- that's not something the AR team can drive through PIPS. Gone. "Hire ten additional coders" exceeded their budget authority. Gone. "Mandate that all payers use a standard authorization format" -- nice idea, zero control. Gone.

That left 16 solutions.

**Round 2: Pros and Cons.** Using the one-breath rule, the team worked through pros and cons for each remaining idea. This round eliminated another 4 ideas where the cons clearly outweighed the pros, and revealed several overlapping concepts.

**Round 3: Combine Like Ideas.** The team grouped the remaining 12 ideas and found that many were variations on the same themes. "Pair new coders with experienced coders" and "create a structured mentoring program for new hires" were combined into "Enhanced onboarding with buddy system." "Weekly payer rule updates" and "automated payer rule alerts" were combined into "Payer communication tracking system." After combining, the list was down to 8 distinct solutions.

**Round 4: Weighted Voting.** With 8 options and 7 team members, each person got 12 votes (8 x 1.5 = 12). The voting identified clear tiers: three solutions had significantly more votes than the rest, two were in the middle, and three received very few votes.

**Round 5: Criteria Rating.** The team agreed on five evaluation criteria and assigned weights:

| Criterion | Weight | Rationale |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Expected Impact on Denial Rate | 2.0 | This is the whole reason we're here |
| Cost to Implement | 1.5 | Budget is limited |
| Time to Implement | 1.0 | We need progress within 6 months |
| Sustainability | 1.5 | We need lasting change, not a temporary fix |
| Resource Requirements | 1.0 | We can't pull people off their core work indefinitely |

Here's how the top five solutions scored:

| Solution | Impact (x2.0) | Cost (x1.5) | Time (x1.0) | Sustain (x1.5) | Resources (x1.0) | Total |
|----------|--------------|-------------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|-------|
| Enhanced onboarding w/ buddy system | 4 (8.0) | 4 (6.0) | 4 (4.0) | 5 (7.5) | 3 (3.0) | **28.5** |
| Pre-submission claim scrubber tool | 5 (10.0) | 2 (3.0) | 3 (3.0) | 5 (7.5) | 3 (3.0) | **26.5** |
| Authorization tracking dashboard | 4 (8.0) | 3 (4.5) | 3 (3.0) | 4 (6.0) | 4 (4.0) | **25.5** |
| Weekly payer rule communication | 3 (6.0) | 5 (7.5) | 5 (5.0) | 3 (4.5) | 5 (5.0) | **28.0** |
| Coding accuracy gamification program | 3 (6.0) | 4 (6.0) | 4 (4.0) | 2 (3.0) | 4 (4.0) | **23.0** |

The scores told an interesting story. The buddy system scored highest overall. The weekly payer communication scored nearly as high, largely because it was cheap and easy. The scrubber tool scored highest on impact -- the criterion with the heaviest weight -- but its cost pulled it down.

### The Discussion That Mattered

The team didn't just take the top three by score. They had a real conversation. Sarah, the coding manager, argued that the scrubber tool's high impact score justified its cost. "If we're serious about getting to 15%, we need the thing that moves the needle the most," she said. James, from IT, pointed out that the dashboard and the scrubber tool shared some infrastructure -- building one would make the other cheaper.

They also noticed that the weekly payer communication, while scoring well, was essentially a workaround rather than a fix. It would help people cope with the current system, but it wouldn't change the system itself. The team decided to incorporate payer communication into the authorization tracking dashboard rather than treat it as a standalone solution.

### Consensus Check

The facilitator asked the three verification questions:

*"Does everyone accept this decision?"* Yes -- all seven members.

*"Is there any opposition to this decision?"* Marcus, the billing analyst, raised a concern: "I think we're underestimating how long the scrubber tool will take. The vendor demos always look smoother than the reality." The team acknowledged his point and committed to building it into the contingency plan.

*"Can everyone support the decision?"* Yes. Marcus specifically said, "I'm not convinced the timeline is realistic, but I think the approach is right, and I'll support it."

That's consensus. Not unanimity. Honest, informed commitment.

### The Plan

**Three solutions selected:**
1. Enhanced onboarding with buddy system for new coders
2. Pre-submission claim scrubber tool implementation
3. Authorization tracking dashboard

**The RACI Matrix:**

| Task | Responsible | Accountable | Consulted | Informed |
|------|------------|-------------|-----------|----------|
| Buddy system design | Sarah (Coding Mgr) | Linda (Team Lead) | HR, Training Dept | All coders |
| Buddy system training materials | Training Dept | Sarah | New coders | Management |
| Scrubber tool vendor selection | James (IT) | Linda | Finance, Compliance | AR team |
| Scrubber tool configuration | James + Vendor | James | Sarah, billing analysts | Management |
| Dashboard requirements | Marcus (Billing) | Linda | Payer relations, IT | AR team |
| Dashboard build & test | IT Development | James | Marcus, Sarah | Management |

**Gantt Chart Timeline:**

```
Month 1 ---|--- Month 2 ---|--- Month 3 ---|--- Month 4 ---|--- Month 5
[====== Phase 1: Buddy System ======]
        [============ Phase 2: Scrubber Tool ============]
                [============ Phase 3: Dashboard ============]
```

- **Phase 1 (Months 1-2):** Design buddy system, match pairs, create training materials, launch. Quick win -- builds momentum.
- **Phase 2 (Months 2-4):** Select scrubber tool vendor, configure, test, deploy. Overlaps with Phase 1 because James can start vendor evaluation while Sarah builds the buddy system.
- **Phase 3 (Months 3-5):** Gather dashboard requirements, build, test, deploy. Overlaps with Phase 2 because requirements can start before the scrubber tool is fully deployed.

**Control System:** Friday status meetings. Three metrics reviewed weekly: new coder error rate (Phase 1), scrubber catch rate (Phase 2), authorization denial rate (Phase 3). Red/yellow/green status for each phase.

**Data Collection:** Denial rate tracked weekly (already available in the claims system). New coder error rates tracked by supervisor review. Scrubber tool hit rates tracked automatically. Authorization denial breakdown by payer tracked monthly.

**Contingency Plans:**

The team used the seven contingency questions. The biggest risk they identified: what if the scrubber tool vendor misses the implementation deadline?

- **Probability:** Medium-high. Marcus's concern was valid. Vendor implementations routinely slip.
- **Impact:** High. The scrubber tool was the highest-impact solution.
- **Prevention:** Negotiate contractual milestones with penalties. James to manage the vendor relationship closely with weekly check-ins.
- **Detection:** Weekly progress reports from vendor. If two consecutive milestones are missed, the contingency triggers.
- **Contingency action:** Manual audit checklist process. Sarah's team would create a paper-based pre-submission review that covers the top 15 denial reasons. It wouldn't catch everything the software would, but it would catch the most common issues.
- **Unanticipated problems:** Emergency team meeting within 48 hours. Linda has authority to reallocate resources.

The team also planned for an opportunity: what if the buddy system works faster than expected? If new coder error rates drop significantly in the first month, they'd reallocate some of Sarah's time from ongoing buddy system support to helping with scrubber tool testing.

### Management Presentation

Because the scrubber tool required a budget approval beyond Linda's authority, the team prepared a presentation for the VP of Revenue Cycle. They followed the structure: Problem (38% denial rate costing $2.4M annually in rework and delayed revenue), Impact (staff overtime, payer relationship strain, cash flow delays), Solution (three-phase plan), Request ($45,000 for scrubber tool license plus 120 hours of IT development time), Benefit (projected 60% reduction in denials, ROI payback in 4 months).

Sarah presented the problem data. James presented the technical solution. Linda presented the timeline and resource request. They rehearsed twice. The VP approved in the meeting and asked for monthly updates.

Step 4 was complete. They had a scored, consensus-backed, management-approved plan with roles, dates, metrics, and contingencies. Time to execute.


## The "Paralysis by Analysis" Warning

I need to address something I've seen happen too many times: teams that get stuck in Step 4 because they can't stop evaluating. They want one more criteria. They want to re-score with different weights. They want to research one more vendor option.

Here's the truth. Any of your top three solutions will probably work. The difference between the best solution and the third-best solution is usually smaller than the difference between implementing something and implementing nothing. The point of the scoring and evaluation process is to make a defensible, data-informed decision -- not to find the mathematically perfect answer. Perfect is the enemy of done.

If your scores are close and the discussion is going in circles, it's time to decide. Use the consensus verification questions. If everyone can honestly say they understand each other's viewpoints and will support the outcome, you have your answer. Move to planning. Move to action.


## What You Can Do Monday Morning

1. **Learn the Criteria Rating Form.** Before your team's next decision, set up a simple criteria rating with three solutions and three criteria. Practice the mechanics so the tool feels natural when you need it for a real decision.

2. **Memorize the three consensus statements.** Print them on a card if you need to. The next time your team reaches a decision -- any decision, not just a PIPS decision -- ask the three verification questions. You'll be surprised how often "consensus" turns out to be "passive acquiescence."

3. **Write a contingency plan for your current project.** Whatever you're working on right now, take ten minutes and answer the seven contingency questions. Most plans don't have contingencies. Yours will.

4. **Practice the one-breath rule.** In your next meeting where ideas are being discussed, try limiting yourself to one-breath statements. Then suggest the team try it. Watch how much more productive the conversation becomes.

5. **Build a RACI matrix for your next team initiative.** Even if it's small, even if there are only three tasks, put names in the Responsible and Accountable columns. Notice how much clearer the ownership becomes.

---

*Next: Step 5 -- Implement the Solution. If Steps 1 through 4 were done well, this should be the easiest chapter in the book. That's not an accident.*

title: Two Phases, One Step
summary: I want to be explicit about why selection and planning live in the same step. In my experience, organizations that separate these into two events -- "let's pick a solution this week, and plan it next ...
bodyMd:
I want to be explicit about why selection and planning live in the same step. In my experience, organizations that separate these into two events -- "let's pick a solution this week, and plan it next month" -- lose momentum. The energy and context from the selection discussion is still fresh when you move into planning. The people who argued for a particular solution are sitting right there and can explain the implementation nuances. The concerns that came up during evaluation become the foundation of your contingency plans.

Do them together. One step, two phases.

**Phase 1 -- Selection:** Reduce the list. Evaluate. Score. Reach consensus.

**Phase 2 -- Planning:** Build the implementation plan. Assign roles. Define contingencies. Prepare for management presentation if needed.

Both phases follow the expand-then-contract rhythm we've been using, though by this point, you're deep into convergence. In the selection phase, you're expanding by generating lots of criteria and exploring lots of evaluation angles. Then you're contracting by scoring, ranking, and converging on a single solution (or a small set of complementary solutions). In the planning phase, you're expanding by thinking through all the possible implementation actions, obstacles, and contingencies. Then you're contracting by committing to a specific plan with specific dates and specific people.

title: The Selection Process
summary: Here's the step-by-step process for getting from "a big list of ideas" to "a solution we all believe in." I'll give you the overview first, then we'll dig into each piece.
bodyMd:
Here's the step-by-step process for getting from "a big list of ideas" to "a solution we all believe in." I'll give you the overview first, then we'll dig into each piece.

### Step 1: Reduce the List with a Broad Filter

Start by eliminating ideas that clearly don't deserve further discussion. The filter is simple: *"Does this idea deserve further consideration?"* You're not asking whether it's the best idea. You're asking whether it should stay on the table at all.

Common filters include:
- **Feasibility:** Can we actually do this with the resources we have?
- **Time:** Can we implement this within a reasonable timeframe?
- **Control:** Do we have the authority to make this change, or does it require someone else's approval that we're unlikely to get?
- **Cost:** Is this within the range of what the organization would spend on this problem?

Don't agonize over borderline cases at this stage. If there's any real debate about whether an idea should stay, keep it. You'll have more rigorous tools for the next round. This first pass is about clearing the obvious non-starters so you can focus your energy on the real contenders.

### Step 2: Pros and Cons

For each remaining idea, do a quick round of pros and cons. Here's a rule I've found invaluable: **the "one breath" rule.** Each person states a pro or a con in one breath. Not a speech. Not a three-minute argument. One breath.

This keeps the discussion focused and prevents any single voice from dominating. You go around the room: "What's a pro?" One breath. "What's a con?" One breath. Next idea.

Write them down. Use a balance sheet format -- pros on the left, cons on the right. This isn't the same as a force field analysis, by the way. A balance sheet is a quick tool for documenting the arguments for and against a particular choice. It helps the group move toward a decision. Force field analysis is an analytical tool that examines the driving and restraining forces around a situation. Both are useful, but they serve different purposes at different moments.

### Step 3: Combine Like Ideas

This is the step people skip, and it costs them. Look at your remaining list and ask: "Are any of these really the same idea expressed differently?" Or better yet: "Could any of these be combined into a more powerful solution?"

Think of it like a grocery list. If someone wrote "apples" and someone else wrote "fruit," you don't need both items. If one person suggested "weekly training sessions" and another suggested "a mentoring program," those might be different -- or they might be two components of a single onboarding improvement initiative.

Number the first idea on your list. Compare the second idea to it. If they're similar, group them together under the same number. If they're different, give the second idea a new number. Continue through the list. You'll often find that twenty ideas reduce to eight or ten distinct concepts.

### Step 4: Get to Six or Fewer

Here's a practical truth: most groups can meaningfully evaluate about six options. More than that and the discussion becomes unwieldy, the scoring takes too long, and people disengage. If your combined list still has more than six ideas, apply another round of filtering. This time, use tighter criteria -- not just "does it deserve consideration" but "is this realistic given our constraints?"

If you can't get below six through filtering alone, use weighted voting (which I'll explain shortly) to identify the top six.

### Step 5: Apply Criteria Rating

Now you're ready for the most structured tool in the selection process. This is where you move from discussion to disciplined evaluation.

### Step 6: Reach Consensus

And here's where you turn that evaluation into a commitment. Not a vote. A consensus.

Let me take you through the critical tools for Steps 5 and 6 in detail.

title: Scoring Systems: Making the Invisible Visible
summary: The whole point of scoring is to make your decision-making process transparent. When a team picks a solution based on gut feeling, or because the loudest person in the room advocated for it, or becaus...
bodyMd:
The whole point of scoring is to make your decision-making process transparent. When a team picks a solution based on gut feeling, or because the loudest person in the room advocated for it, or because it was the first idea that came up, they've made a decision -- but they can't explain it, defend it, or learn from it. Scoring systems force the team to articulate *why* one solution is better than another, using criteria that everyone agreed to in advance.

I want to walk through four different scoring approaches. They're all legitimate. Which one you use depends on the situation, the team, and the nature of the decision.

### The Criteria Rating Form

This is the workhorse tool of Step 4. If you learn one scoring method, make it this one.

**How it works:**

1. **Select your criteria.** What matters for this decision? Common criteria include: Cost to implement, Time to implement, Expected impact on the problem, Sustainability over time, Resource requirements, Degree of control (can we do this ourselves?), Appropriateness (does this actually address the root cause?), and Acceptability (will the people affected support this change?).

2. **Assign weights.** Not all criteria are equally important. A weight of 1.0 means standard importance. Use 0.5 for criteria that matter but aren't critical. Use 1.5 or 2.0 for criteria that are make-or-break. The rule of thumb is to keep weights between 0.5 and 2.0 -- anything beyond that range tends to overwhelm the other criteria mathematically.

3. **Rate each solution against each criterion on a 1-5 scale.** 1 means poor, 5 means excellent. Important nuance: for criteria where lower is better (like cost or risk), reverse the scale so that 5 means "low cost" or "low risk." This keeps the math consistent -- higher scores always mean better.

4. **Multiply each rating by the criterion's weight.**

5. **Sum the weighted scores for each solution.**

6. **Discuss the results.**

That last step is the one that matters most. Let me say it plainly: **the Criteria Rating Worksheet guides your discussion. It does not make your decision.** If the math says Solution B wins by two points, but the team has a strong feeling that Solution A is the right answer, that's a conversation worth having. Maybe there's a criterion you didn't include. Maybe the weights need adjusting. Maybe someone has information that changes a rating. The worksheet is a mirror -- it reflects the team's thinking back to them in a structured format. It's not a calculator that spits out an answer.

**Worked example:**

Suppose you're evaluating three solutions and you've agreed on four criteria:

| Criterion | Weight | Solution A | Weighted A | Solution B | Weighted B | Solution C | Weighted C |
|-----------|--------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|
| Cost (5=low) | 1.5 | 4 | 6.0 | 2 | 3.0 | 3 | 4.5 |
| Time to Implement | 1.0 | 3 | 3.0 | 4 | 4.0 | 2 | 2.0 |
| Expected Impact | 2.0 | 3 | 6.0 | 5 | 10.0 | 4 | 8.0 |
| Sustainability | 1.0 | 4 | 4.0 | 3 | 3.0 | 5 | 5.0 |
| **Total** | | | **19.0** | | **20.0** | | **19.5** |

Solutions B and C are nearly tied, with A close behind. This doesn't mean B wins automatically. It means B scored highest, and now you have a data-driven starting point for discussion. Why did B score so high on impact? Is that rating justified? Is B's low cost rating a dealbreaker? That's the conversation.

### Weighted Voting

This is faster and less formal than criteria rating. Good for situations where you need a quick read on group preferences.

**How it works:** Each team member gets a number of votes equal to **1.5 times the number of options.** If you have six solutions on the board, each person gets nine votes. They can distribute those votes however they want -- put all nine on one option, spread them evenly, or any combination in between.

**Four procedural tips that make this work:**

1. **Spread your votes.** Encourage people not to dump all votes on one option. The point is to capture degrees of preference, not just first choice.
2. **Decide before recording.** Everyone should decide their vote allocation before anyone records. This prevents anchoring.
3. **Show fingers.** Have everyone hold up their vote count simultaneously for each option. This prevents people from changing their votes based on what they see others doing.
4. **Record by option, not by person.** Write the total votes for Solution A, then Solution B, etc. Don't write "Marc gave 5 votes to A." The results should be collective, not individual.

Key insight: Weighted voting **does not make decisions. It merely gives information.** It tells you where the group's energy is. A solution that gets zero votes from eight people is probably not worth pursuing. A solution that gets at least some votes from everyone might be a unifying choice even if it didn't get the most total votes.

### Paired Comparisons

This method forces the group to make head-to-head choices between every pair of options. It's particularly useful when alternatives seem roughly equal and the group is stuck.

**How it works:** Take every possible pair of solutions and ask: "If we could only do A or B, which would we choose?" Record the winner of each comparison. The solution that wins the most head-to-head matchups is the group's preference.

The number of comparisons grows quickly. For 4 options, you have 6 comparisons. For 6 options, you have 15. For 8 options, you have 28. This is one reason to get your list to six or fewer before applying this tool.

The power of paired comparisons is that it forces the group to choose even when alternatives seem equal. "I like both" isn't an option. You have to pick one. This reveals hidden preferences and often breaks ties that weighted voting couldn't resolve.

### Point-Scoring System

This is a variation on criteria rating where instead of using a fixed 1-5 scale, you allocate an arbitrary number of points (100 or 1000) among your criteria based on their importance, then distribute points within each criterion across your options.

**Worked example with a 1000-point budget:**

Your criteria and their point allocations (totaling 1000):
- Expected Impact: 300 points
- Cost: 200 points
- Implementation Speed: 150 points
- Sustainability: 200 points
- Team Acceptability: 150 points

For each criterion, distribute its points across the solutions based on how well each solution meets that criterion. If Solution A has much higher expected impact than B or C, it might get 150 of the 300 impact points while B gets 100 and C gets 50.

Sum the points for each solution. The one with the most total points is the mathematical favorite -- and, like every other scoring method, it's a starting point for discussion, not a final verdict.

Some teams find the point-allocation approach more intuitive than the weight-times-rating math of criteria rating. Use whichever resonates with your group.

title: Consensus: Not What You Think It Means
summary: Let me clear up a misconception that I've seen trip up more teams than any other single issue in the PIPS process.
bodyMd:
Let me clear up a misconception that I've seen trip up more teams than any other single issue in the PIPS process.

Consensus does not mean unanimity. It does not mean everyone's first choice wins. It does not mean we discuss until every person is enthusiastically on board. That's a recipe for paralysis, and it gives veto power to the most stubborn person in the room.

The definition I use comes from William Ouchi's *Theory Z* (1981), his influential work on how American organizations could adopt Japanese management principles, and it's the most practical definition of consensus I've ever encountered. Consensus is reached when **each member of the group can honestly say three things:**

1. **"I believe that you understand my point of view."**
2. **"I believe that I understand your point of view."**
3. **"Whether or not I prefer this decision, I will support it because it was reached openly and fairly."**

Read that third statement again. "Whether or not I prefer this decision." That's the key. You don't have to love the decision. You don't have to think it's the best possible choice. You have to believe the process was fair, that your perspective was heard and understood, and that you're willing to support the outcome.

This is why consensus is more about **listening** than about **talking.** If I'm trying to convince you that my solution is better, I'm talking. If I'm trying to understand why you favor a different solution so that I can honestly say "I believe that I understand your point of view," I'm listening. PIPS consensus is built on listening.

### The Three Verification Questions

As a facilitator, you need a practical way to check whether the group has actually reached consensus or whether people are just tired and agreeing to end the meeting. At every decision point -- not just the final solution selection, but every significant decision in Steps 1 through 6 -- ask these three questions:

1. **"Does everyone accept this decision?"**
2. **"Is there any opposition to this decision?"**
3. **"Can everyone support the decision?"**

These aren't redundant. Question 1 tests for passive agreement. Question 2 explicitly invites dissent -- it's permission to object. Question 3 tests for active commitment. Someone might accept a decision (question 1) and not actively oppose it (question 2) but still not be willing to support it (question 3). That matters. If someone can't say yes to all three, the consensus isn't real, and you need more discussion.

I've seen teams where one person stayed silent through the entire evaluation, and the facilitator said "Great, we have consensus" and moved on. That person went back to their desk and quietly undermined the solution for six months. Not maliciously -- they genuinely believed it was the wrong approach. But nobody asked, and they didn't feel comfortable volunteering their opposition. The three verification questions prevent this. They create explicit, structured space for dissent.

### Test for Reasonableness

Before you invest time in detailed planning, pause. Step back. Ask the group: "Does this pass the common-sense test?"

You've just been through a rigorous evaluation process with scoring worksheets and criteria weights and mathematical totals. It's possible to get so deep into the mechanics that you lose sight of practical reality. The solution with the highest score might be technically sound but culturally impossible. It might require a technology the organization has already tried and abandoned. It might depend on a vendor relationship that's deteriorating.

This is a quick gut-check, not a formal process. Two minutes, not two hours. "Looking at this with fresh eyes, does this make sense as the thing we're going to commit our time and resources to?" If the answer is yes, move to planning. If something feels off, name it and discuss it.

title: Cost-Benefit Analysis and Balance Sheets
summary: For solutions that involve significant cost, you'll need a more formal analysis than the criteria rating provides. A cost-benefit analysis doesn't have to be an elaborate financial model. At its core,...
bodyMd:
For solutions that involve significant cost, you'll need a more formal analysis than the criteria rating provides. A cost-benefit analysis doesn't have to be an elaborate financial model. At its core, it's a structured comparison of what the solution will cost versus what it will return.

**Costs** include: direct financial expenditure, staff time, opportunity cost (what else could those resources be doing?), disruption during implementation, and training time.

**Benefits** include: direct financial savings, time savings, error reduction, quality improvement, staff satisfaction, and customer satisfaction.

Not everything needs to be dollarized. Some benefits are hard to quantify -- like "reduced frustration" or "better team morale." Include them anyway. A balance sheet with some qualitative entries is more honest and useful than one that pretends everything can be expressed in currency.

The balance sheet is the pros-and-cons format applied specifically to the cost-benefit question. It's a decision support tool, not a decision-making tool. The team still decides. The balance sheet makes the tradeoffs visible.

title: Phase 2: Planning the Implementation
summary: You've selected your solution. The group has reached genuine consensus. Now you need a plan that will actually work in the real world.
bodyMd:
You've selected your solution. The group has reached genuine consensus. Now you need a plan that will actually work in the real world.

I use a six-item checklist for implementation planning. Every plan needs to address all six. Skip one and you'll find out about it later, usually at the worst possible time.

### The 6-Item Implementation Planning Checklist

#### 1. Sequential Steps

Divide the solution into sequential, easily manageable steps. Each step becomes a reference point for tracking progress. If you can't break it into steps, the solution is either too simple to need a plan (just do it) or too vague to implement (go back and define it better).

Think about dependencies. What has to happen before something else can start? What can happen in parallel? This is where Gantt charts and PERT charts earn their keep -- they make the sequence and dependencies visible.

#### 2. Clear Roles

Every task needs a name next to it. Not a department. Not "the team." A specific person who is accountable for that specific deliverable. I'm a fan of the RACI matrix for this: for each task, define who is **Responsible** (doing the work), who is **Accountable** (making sure it gets done), who needs to be **Consulted** (their input is needed before action), and who needs to be **Informed** (they need to know what happened after the fact).

The accountability column is the important one. There should be exactly one name in the Accountable column for each task. If there are two names, nobody's actually accountable.

#### 3. Commitment Strategy

This is where you think explicitly about who needs to support this solution and how you'll get -- and keep -- their support.

There's a distinction worth understanding here: **passive commitment** versus **active commitment.** Passive commitment means "I won't stand in the way." Active commitment means "I'll help make this happen." You need to know which type you need from each stakeholder, because the strategies are different.

For passive commitment, information is usually enough. Keep people in the loop. Let them know what's changing and when. For active commitment, you need involvement. Ask for their input. Give them a role. Make them part of the solution, not a bystander watching it happen.

#### 4. Control System

How will you know if the plan is on track? Define the monitoring mechanism before you start implementing. What metrics will you watch? How often will you check them? Who reviews the data? What constitutes "off track" enough to trigger a response?
