title: Leadership and Strategy
summary: This one's different. I've saved it for last because it's the most important and the most commonly overlooked.
bodyMd:
This one's different. I've saved it for last because it's the most important and the most commonly overlooked.

PIPS isn't just for fixing operational problems. It applies to how organizations make strategic decisions. And if I'm being honest, strategic decision-making is where I've seen some of the most egregious violations of everything PIPS stands for.

### The Problem

A company's strategic planning process produced an average of 12-15 strategic initiatives per year. Their completion rate? Thirty percent. Seven out of ten initiatives launched with executive fanfare died somewhere between kickoff and the finish line. Not because they were bad ideas. Because the organization didn't have the capacity to execute all of them simultaneously.

The symptoms were everywhere: initiative leads competing for the same resources, project timelines slipping because key people were spread across five initiatives, quarterly reviews that became exercises in explaining why things were behind schedule, and a growing cynicism among middle managers who had learned that "strategic initiative" was code for "extra work that will be abandoned in six months."

### How PIPS Changed the Conversation

The PIPS team -- in this case, a small group of senior leaders who agreed to apply the methodology to their own planning process -- started with the data.

They audited all strategic initiatives from the prior three years. For each one, they documented: the stated objective, the resources allocated, the resources actually consumed, the completion status, and the business impact.

The patterns were damning:

- Initiatives launched in Q1 had a 45% completion rate. Initiatives launched in Q3 had a 12% completion rate. Why? By Q3, resources were already committed to Q1 initiatives, but new priorities kept being added.
- The average initiative required 2.3 FTEs of dedicated effort. The organization had approximately 8 FTEs worth of capacity for initiative work per year. That's capacity for 3-4 simultaneous initiatives. They were launching 12-15.
- Of the initiatives that did complete, 80% were ones that had a single executive sponsor who actively protected the resources. Of the ones that failed, 90% had either no clear sponsor or a sponsor who was also sponsoring two or more other initiatives.

The root cause was simple and uncomfortable: leadership was saying yes to everything and committing to nothing. The strategic planning process had no resource constraint built into it. It was a wish list, not a plan.

### The Solution

**1. Portfolio Management Approach.** The leadership team adopted a hard cap: no more than 5 active strategic initiatives at any time. New initiatives could only be launched when a slot opened -- either because an initiative completed or because one was deliberately stopped.

**2. PIPS Criteria Rating for Prioritization.** Each proposed initiative was evaluated against weighted criteria: strategic alignment, resource requirements, expected ROI, risk level, and time to impact. The same multi-criteria evaluation that PIPS uses in Step 4 for solution selection was applied to initiative selection. No more squeaky-wheel prioritization.

**3. Executive Sponsorship Protocol.** Every active initiative had a single executive sponsor who could sponsor no more than two initiatives simultaneously. The sponsor was accountable for removing obstacles, protecting resources, and reporting progress at monthly leadership meetings using data, not narratives.

**4. Quarterly Kill Review.** Every quarter, each active initiative was reviewed against its original metrics. If an initiative was not meeting interim milestones and couldn't demonstrate a credible path to completion, it was stopped. Not paused. Stopped. Resources were freed for higher-priority work.

Over two years, the completion rate went from 30% to 78%. More importantly, the initiatives that completed were the right ones -- the ones that had been rigorously evaluated, properly resourced, and actively sponsored. The total number of initiatives launched per year dropped from 12-15 to 7-8, but the business impact per initiative was dramatically higher.

### Why This Matters for PIPS Adoption

I'm not just telling you this story because it's a good PIPS example. I'm telling you because leadership's willingness to apply PIPS to their own processes is the single biggest predictor of whether PIPS will take root in an organization.

If the leadership team uses PIPS to make better strategic decisions, two things happen. First, the organization gets better outcomes. Second, everyone in the organization sees that PIPS isn't just something management mandates for the rank and file -- it's something leadership believes in enough to use themselves.

That visibility matters more than any kickoff meeting, any training session, or any motivational speech about continuous improvement. People watch what leaders do, not what leaders say.

### Leadership-Specific PIPS Tips

- **Apply PIPS to your own decision-making first.** Before asking your organization to adopt PIPS, use it yourself. Pick one leadership-level problem and work through the six steps. The experience will make you a better sponsor and a more credible champion.
- **The hardest part is saying no.** PIPS forces prioritization based on data and criteria. For leaders who are used to saying yes to keep stakeholders happy, this is uncomfortable. Embrace the discomfort. An organization that's doing three things well is outperforming one that's doing twelve things poorly.
- **Resource planning is not optional.** If you launch an initiative without clear resource allocation, you haven't committed to it. You've just added it to the wish list. PIPS makes you honest about what you can actually do.

title: The Common Thread
summary: If you've read all six of these examples, you've probably noticed a pattern. In every case, the team's initial assumption about the root cause was wrong.
bodyMd:
If you've read all six of these examples, you've probably noticed a pattern. In every case, the team's initial assumption about the root cause was wrong.

- HR assumed it was compensation. It was onboarding.
- IT assumed it was staffing. It was preventable ticket volume.
- Product assumed it was development speed. It was approval cycle time.
- Operations assumed the close just took that long. It was sequential processing of parallelizable tasks.
- Finance assumed errors were inevitable. The input method was the problem.
- Leadership assumed they needed better execution. They needed fewer initiatives.

This isn't a coincidence. It's the human condition. We jump to solutions because solutions feel productive. We skip root cause analysis because it feels slow. And we end up solving the wrong problem, then wondering why nothing changed.

PIPS is the antidote to that tendency. Not because it's brilliant. Because it forces you to do the work you'd rather skip.

Every department can benefit from that discipline. Every department.

title: What You Can Do Monday Morning
summary: 1. **Pick one problem in your department that everyone "knows" the cause of.** Write down what people assume the root cause is. Then spend one hour looking at the actual data. Does the data support th...
bodyMd:
1. **Pick one problem in your department that everyone "knows" the cause of.** Write down what people assume the root cause is. Then spend one hour looking at the actual data. Does the data support the assumption? In my experience, at least half the time, it doesn't.

2. **Identify your department's equivalent of "password reset tickets."** Every department has work that consumes time but shouldn't require a human -- manual data entry that could be automated, questions that could be answered by documentation, approvals that could be streamlined. List the top five. That list is your PIPS starting point.

3. **Map one process as it actually happens, not as it's documented.** Pick a process that's been frustrating your team. Walk through it step by step with the people who actually do the work. Write down every handoff, every wait, every workaround. The gap between the documented process and reality is where your improvement opportunities live.

4. **Calculate the cost of your team's top rework item.** How much time does your team spend redoing work that should have been done right the first time? Put a dollar figure on it. That number will get attention.

5. **Have a 15-minute conversation with a peer in another department about their biggest process pain point.** Not to solve it -- just to understand it. Cross-functional awareness is where the best PIPS projects start, because so many problems span departmental boundaries.

title: Scaling PIPS
summary: There's a misconception about PIPS that I need to clear up right now, before it takes root and limits everything you do with this methodology.
bodyMd:
# Chapter 11: From Big Problems to Everyday Fixes

There's a misconception about PIPS that I need to clear up right now, before it takes root and limits everything you do with this methodology.

The misconception is that PIPS is for big problems. The kind that get a dedicated team, a project charter, a twelve-week timeline, and a management presentation at the end. Those problems exist, and PIPS handles them beautifully. But if you think PIPS is only for those problems, you're using maybe 10% of what it can do.

PIPS is a thinking discipline. And thinking disciplines scale. You can apply the same six-step logic to a question that takes five minutes to work through or a transformation that takes five years. The steps don't change. The depth changes. The formality changes. The documentation changes. But the underlying logic -- define the gap, analyze the cause, generate options, select the best one, implement it, check if it worked -- that logic works at every scale.

Let me show you what I mean.


## The 5-Minute PIPS

You will use this version more than any other. Once the PIPS framework is internalized, your brain will start running through the steps automatically whenever something doesn't work the way it should. That's the goal. Not more meetings. Better thinking.

### The Printer That Jammed Every Tuesday

Here's one that actually happened, though the details have been simplified.

An office had a printer that jammed regularly on Tuesday mornings. Three or four times between 8 AM and 10 AM. Every other day of the week, the printer worked fine. People complained. Someone called the service company. The technician came out, cleaned the rollers, replaced a part, and said it should be fine. Tuesday came. It jammed again.

Now, in most offices, this would continue indefinitely. People would work around it, use a different printer, complain at lunch, and eventually someone would requisition a new machine. Problem "solved" -- except they'd still be dealing with whatever caused the original problem.

Here's the 5-Minute PIPS, the way it ran through someone's head:

**Step 1 -- What's the gap?** The printer jams 3-4 times every Tuesday morning. It doesn't jam any other day. Desired state: zero jams, every day.

**Step 2 -- Why?** What's different about Tuesdays? The jams happen between 8 and 10 AM. The print queue logs show that on Tuesday mornings, the marketing team prints their weekly activity report. It's 180-220 pages. That's more than this printer processes in any other two-hour window during the entire week. The printer isn't broken. It's being overloaded.

**Step 3 -- What could we do?** A few options: buy a higher-capacity printer, split the report into smaller batches, schedule the print job for overnight Monday, route the job to the larger printer in the copy room, or reduce the report length.

**Step 4 -- What's the best option?** Scheduling the print job for overnight Monday costs nothing, disrupts nothing, and solves the problem immediately. The large-format printer in the copy room is also an option but requires marketing to walk to a different floor. Overnight scheduling wins.

**Step 5 -- Do it.** Marketing sets up a scheduled print job for 11 PM Monday.

**Step 6 -- Did it work?** No Tuesday jams for the next month. Done.

Total elapsed time for the analysis: about five minutes of thinking and one conversation with the marketing coordinator. No team charter. No brainstorming session. No presentation. Just disciplined thinking applied to a small, specific problem.

The point is not that this was a hard problem. The point is that without the PIPS framework in someone's head, the most likely outcome was a new printer. That's a thousand-dollar solution to a scheduling problem. Multiply that pattern across an entire organization and the waste is staggering.

### The Everyday Stuff That Shouldn't Have to Be Done Every Day

The 5-Minute PIPS is especially powerful for identifying processes that are consuming time unnecessarily. Things your team does every day that they shouldn't have to do at all, or that should only happen once instead of repeatedly.

A few examples of 5-Minute PIPS thinking in action:

- "Why do we manually format this report every Friday?" Because the data export from the system comes out in the wrong format. Can we change the export template? Yes. Time saved: 45 minutes per week, 39 hours per year.

- "Why does someone walk this form to the third floor every afternoon?" Because the receiving department needs the data for their end-of-day process. Could we share it electronically? Of course. We just never set it up because the walk was "only ten minutes."

- "Why do three people review this approval before it goes out?" Because five years ago there was a big error, and management added two additional reviewers. Has there been an error since? No. Could we go back to one reviewer with a spot-check audit instead? Yes.

Each of these is a small win. But small wins compound. An organization that captures fifty small wins a year through 5-Minute PIPS thinking has freed up hundreds of hours and eliminated dozens of frustration points. More importantly, it's built a culture where people instinctively ask "why do we do it this way?" instead of accepting friction as inevitable.

### What Happens When You Keep Compounding

I want to give you a picture of what this looks like over years, not weeks.

Early in my career, I built what we called "the operator role" -- a single position responsible for all system maintenance work. Data loads, batch processing, file corrections, error handling. When we started, the operator handled about a hundred tasks a month and it took roughly four hours a day.

We had one rule: the role should always take four hours a day, regardless of volume. If it crept past four hours, that was a signal -- something needed to be automated, simplified, or eliminated. Every time the operator encountered a repetitive task, we'd run a quick 5-Minute PIPS: what's the gap? Why is this manual? What could we automate? Do it. Check that it worked.

Over several years, that role went from a hundred tasks a month to over four thousand. Still four hours a day. Same role. The compound effect of hundreds of small improvements -- each one a 5-Minute PIPS -- turned a position that could barely keep up with a hundred tasks into one that effortlessly handled forty times the volume.

There's a detail that made this work: we trained operators the hard way first. Before showing anyone the automated version of a task, we made them do it manually for weeks. Every step, the long way, no shortcuts. We called it the Mr. Miyagi approach -- wax on, wax off. It felt tedious in the moment, but when a dependency failed or something needed to be modified, the operator understood the underlying process well enough to diagnose and fix it. They weren't just button-pushers. They were problem-solvers who happened to have automation handling the routine work.

We also wrote every process so that our lowest-cost employee could execute it. Then that person would document it so an even lower-cost employee could do it -- or so it could be automated entirely. This constant push to simplify and delegate downward was the engine of scaling. It's how a team that started with a handful of people handled the workload of a department ten times its size.

The numbers tell the story. The broader team that operated with this mindset tripled their productivity over four years -- going from roughly 800 completed work items a year to over 2,600, with only 12% attributed to bugs and rework. Individual contributors went from completing about 100 items per quarter to 300. Average completion time per item dropped by a third. System uptime hit 99.999%. And the team wasn't three times larger. They were the same people, working the same hours. The difference was entirely in how they worked -- relentless small improvements, compounded over years.

At one point, all of the eligibility processing, accumulator management, and semi-automated data loads for the entire company were handled by a half-FTE operator. One person, half their day. For work that would have buried a team of four a few years earlier. That's what compounding gets you.

That's the power of 5-Minute PIPS thinking applied consistently over time. No single improvement was dramatic. No single automation saved the day. But the accumulation of hundreds of small, disciplined improvements created a capability that would have been impossible to achieve through any one-time initiative.


## The 1-Hour PIPS

This is the sweet spot for department-level problems that affect a team but don't require a full project mobilization. You gather your team, put the framework on a whiteboard, and work through Steps 1 through 4 in a structured hour. Implementation and evaluation happen afterward, but the thinking work gets done in a single focused session.

### The Inconsistent Onboarding Checklist

A department with eight managers and about forty new hires per year had a problem: onboarding was inconsistent. Every manager did it differently. Some were thorough -- structured first week, clear documentation, scheduled check-ins. Others handed the new hire a laptop and said, "Ask me if you have questions." The result was predictable: new hires who got a good onboarding hit productivity faster and stayed longer. Those who didn't were more likely to struggle and leave.

The department head knew it was a problem but hadn't been able to fix it because, as she put it, "every time I bring it up, it turns into a two-hour argument about whose approach is best."

Here's how the 1-Hour PIPS session ran:

**Minutes 1-5: Frame the problem.**

"Our onboarding experience varies significantly by manager. New hires report inconsistent experiences, and our time-to-productivity ranges from 3 weeks to 12 weeks depending on which team they join. We want every new hire to have a consistent, high-quality first 30 days regardless of manager."

Write the As-Is and Desired State on the whiteboard. No debate yet. Just get it on the board.

**Minutes 5-15: Quick data review.**

The department head had pulled three data points in advance: time-to-productivity by manager (based on when new hires were first assigned independent work), 90-day survey scores by manager, and first-year retention by manager. The numbers told a clear story. Two managers had significantly better outcomes. The differences weren't subtle.

This step is critical. By starting with data, you prevent the conversation from becoming "my way vs. your way." It's not about opinions. It's about what the numbers say.

**Minutes 15-30: Brainstorm the checklist.**

The group brainstormed everything that should happen in a new hire's first 30 days. They used Freewheeling -- anyone could call out an item. The scribe wrote them all on the whiteboard without evaluation. They generated 34 items in 15 minutes.

Items ranged from "set up workstation before day 1" to "meet with each peer 1-on-1 in the first week" to "review department goals and metrics" to "shadow a senior team member for one full day."

**Minutes 30-45: Filter and prioritize.**

The team reviewed each item. Some were universally agreed upon -- those went straight to the checklist. Some were nice-to-have -- those were bracketed for possible inclusion later. A few were contentious -- the team discussed briefly and either included them with modifications or bracketed them.

Key move: the department head asked the two managers with the best outcomes what they were doing that others weren't. Both independently mentioned the same three things: a structured first-week schedule sent to the new hire before day 1, a 1-on-1 with every team member in the first five days, and a weekly 15-minute check-in for the first month. Those three practices went on the checklist.

**Minutes 45-55: Build the implementation plan.**

The checklist was drafted with 22 items, organized by day/week. One manager volunteered to create the final template. Another volunteered to pilot it with the next two hires. The department head committed to reviewing results after 90 days.

**Minutes 55-60: Confirm next steps and close.**

Template draft by end of next week. Pilot starts with the next hire. 90-day review scheduled on the calendar before anyone leaves the room.

Total investment: one hour of eight people's time. The template was in use within two weeks. Within six months, time-to-productivity variation across managers dropped by 60%.


## The Multi-Week PIPS

This is the full formal process. Dedicated team, scheduled meetings, data collection periods, analysis sessions, and a management presentation. This is the level where PIPS looks most like a traditional project, and it's the level most people think of when they hear "PIPS."

I won't walk through a complete multi-week example here because that's essentially what we did in Chapters 4 through 9 with the Meridian denial rate project. But I want to highlight the structural elements that distinguish a multi-week PIPS from a 1-Hour PIPS:

**Dedicated team.** Not people working on this "when they have time." People who are assigned to this project with allocated hours. The team is cross-functional -- it includes people who do the work, people who manage the work, and people affected by the work.

**Scheduled meeting cadence.** Weekly meetings during active phases, typically 60-90 minutes. Biweekly during data collection phases when there's not enough new information to discuss weekly. Every meeting has an agenda, a timekeeper, and a scribe.

**Formal data collection.** Not a quick look at existing reports. Deliberate measurement. If the existing data doesn't tell you what you need to know, you design a data collection effort. Check sheets, sampling plans, observation periods. The data collection phase might last 2-4 weeks on its own.

**Root cause analysis tools.** This is where you pull out the full toolkit: cause-and-effect diagrams, Pareto analysis, force field analysis, the five whys. A multi-week PIPS gives you the time to use these tools properly, not just superficially.

**Solution evaluation matrix.** Formal criteria weighting. Formal scoring. The team doesn't just pick the solution that feels right -- they evaluate each candidate against defined criteria and document the rationale.

**Management presentation.** The team presents their findings, recommendation, and implementation plan to the sponsoring leadership. This serves two purposes: it gets approval and resources for implementation, and it builds the team's credibility and visibility.

**Implementation plan with milestones.** Not "we'll roll it out." A specific plan with dates, owners, milestones, and contingencies.

**Formal evaluation period.** The team reconvenes after implementation to measure results against the original metrics. Did the gap close? By how much? Are there unintended consequences? Should the solution be adjusted?

A multi-week PIPS typically runs 8-16 weeks from problem selection to evaluation. Some run longer for particularly complex problems. The key is that every week has a purpose, and the team is progressing through the steps in order, not skipping ahead.


## The Strategic PIPS

This is the largest scale: company-wide transformations that involve multiple workstreams, executive sponsorship, and quarterly reviews. A strategic PIPS isn't one PIPS project -- it's a portfolio of PIPS projects organized around a strategic objective.

### Digital Transformation as a Strategic PIPS

A company decided it needed to "digitally transform." That phrase is so overused it's almost meaningless, but the underlying problem was real: their core business processes still ran on paper, spreadsheets, and institutional knowledge in people's heads. They were losing competitive ground to companies that had automated, integrated, and data-driven operations.

Here's how PIPS structured a transformation that might otherwise have been a vague, expensive mess:

**The Strategic As-Is:** Manual, paper-based processes in operations, finance, and customer service. Average process cycle times 3-5x industry benchmarks. Employee satisfaction surveys consistently cite "outdated tools" as a top-3 frustration.

**The Strategic Desired State:** Core processes digitized and integrated within 24 months. Cycle times within 1.5x of industry benchmarks. Employee tool satisfaction score above 70% (current: 31%).

**The Portfolio of PIPS Projects:** Instead of one massive transformation project, the leadership team identified seven specific process areas and launched a separate PIPS project for each:

1. Invoice processing (AP automation -- the Finance example from Chapter 10)
2. Customer onboarding workflow
3. Internal helpdesk and IT service delivery
4. Employee onboarding (HR)
5. Inventory management
6. Sales pipeline tracking
7. Management reporting and dashboards

Each project had its own team, its own timeline, and its own metrics. But they were coordinated at the portfolio level by an executive steering committee that met monthly to review progress, resolve resource conflicts, and adjust priorities.

Not all seven launched simultaneously. The first three started in Q1. Two more launched in Q2 as the first wave completed their analysis phases and required less intensive team participation. The final two launched in Q3.

This is PIPS at strategic scale: breaking a complex transformation into discrete, manageable problems, each tackled with the full PIPS methodology, and coordinating the portfolio so that resources are used effectively and lessons from early projects inform later ones.

The 24-month goal was largely achieved -- six of seven projects completed within the timeframe, the seventh finished three months late but delivered strong results. More importantly, the organization now had over forty people who had been through a complete PIPS cycle, understood the methodology, and could apply it to future problems. That capability was the real strategic asset.


## The Key: Proportionality

I've given you four levels, but the real message is simpler than any categorization.

Match the rigor to the problem.

I used to call the simplest level a "Mostly Fine Decision." Most decisions in a day don't need analysis. They need someone to make a call and move on. The same principle applies to how you build solutions: build what you need, only when you need it, and only as much as you need. Don't over-engineer. Don't gold-plate. It's never going to be perfect, so get it done and improve it later. Make the mostly fine decision and save your rigor for the problems that deserve it.

Don't bring a full PIPS team to a printer jam. You'll waste everyone's time, and people will associate PIPS with bureaucratic overkill. A 5-Minute PIPS solves the printer jam. Done.

Don't try to fix company culture in a lunch meeting. Culture is a multi-year, multi-workstream challenge that requires sustained leadership commitment, formal measurement, and deliberate investment. Give it the multi-week or strategic treatment it deserves.

The most common mistake I see is organizations that only do PIPS formally. They set up the whole apparatus -- team charter, meeting cadence, presentation schedule -- for every problem. Within six months, people are exhausted. PIPS becomes associated with overhead, and the informal, everyday thinking discipline never develops.

The second most common mistake is the opposite: organizations that only do PIPS informally. People "think in PIPS" but never document, never measure, never present findings. There's no organizational learning. Good analysis happens in someone's head and stays in someone's head. When that person leaves, the insight walks out with them.

You need both. You need the 5-Minute PIPS in everyone's head for daily problem solving. And you need the formal multi-week process for the problems that deserve it. The informal builds the culture. The formal builds the capability and the institutional memory.


## Teaching People to Think in PIPS

The best thing you can do for your organization is not to run more PIPS projects. It's to get more people thinking in PIPS terms even when they're not running a formal project.

How do you do that? You model it. You ask the PIPS questions in everyday conversations:

- Someone brings you a problem: "What does the current state look like? What would better look like?" (Step 1)
- Someone proposes a solution: "What data led us to this solution? Do we know the root cause, or are we treating a symptom?" (Step 2)
- Someone says there's only one option: "What else could we try? Let's generate at least two more alternatives before we decide." (Step 3)
- Someone picks an option: "What criteria are we using to evaluate these? How do they rank against each other?" (Step 4)
- Someone implements a change: "What's the plan? Who owns each piece? What does the timeline look like?" (Step 5)
- Something gets implemented: "How will we know if it worked? When will we check?" (Step 6)

You don't have to call it PIPS. You don't have to announce that you're using a methodology. You just ask the questions. Over time, people start asking them themselves. That's when the culture shifts.

I had a manager once tell me, "My team has started finishing each other's PIPS sentences. Someone will say 'we have a problem with...' and before they finish, someone else says 'what's the data?'" That's exactly what you want. When the methodology disappears into the way people naturally think, it's won.


## A Word About Judgment

I want to add something that might seem obvious but needs to be said: knowing which level of PIPS to apply is itself a skill. It's a judgment call, and you'll get better at it with practice.

Some guidelines:

- If the problem affects one person or one task, start with a 5-Minute PIPS.
- If the problem affects a team or a department, a 1-Hour PIPS is usually right.
- If the problem is cross-functional, involves multiple root causes, or has been resistant to previous solutions, go multi-week.
- If the problem is strategic in nature and requires sustained investment, treat it as a strategic PIPS.

But these are guidelines, not rules. Some problems that seem small turn out to be symptoms of something systemic. You start with a 5-Minute PIPS and realize the root cause goes deeper than you expected. Great -- escalate it to a 1-Hour or multi-week. That's not failure. That's the process working.

And some problems that seem enormous turn out to have simple root causes. You charter a multi-week project, collect data, and discover that one configuration change fixes everything. Great -- make the change, document it, evaluate the results, and close the project. Don't keep it alive just because you allocated twelve weeks for it. That's waste, and waste is what we're here to eliminate.

The point is to be proportional, not to be rigid. Use the right amount of rigor for the problem in front of you, and be willing to adjust when the evidence tells you to.


## What You Can Do Monday Morning

1. **Practice one 5-Minute PIPS this week.** Pick something small that's been annoying you or your team. Run through the six steps in your head. Don't write anything down. Don't call a meeting. Just think through it systematically and see if the root cause is what you assumed.

2. **Schedule a 1-Hour PIPS session for one department-level problem.** Pick a real issue. Invite the right people. Write the As-Is and Desired State on a whiteboard before anyone walks in. Work through Steps 1-4 in the hour. Assign implementation and evaluation as follow-ups.

3. **Start asking PIPS questions in your regular conversations.** When someone brings you a problem, ask "What does the data say?" When someone proposes a solution, ask "What other options did we consider?" You don't need to label it. Just ask the questions.

4. **Identify one process your team does regularly that shouldn't require as much time as it takes.** Not a broken process -- a functional one that's just unnecessarily manual, slow, or redundant. That's your next 5-Minute or 1-Hour PIPS target.

---

Knowing which level of PIPS to apply -- and building the judgment to scale up or down as needed -- is a skill that grows with practice. But individual skill isn't enough. For PIPS to truly take hold, the organization itself needs to change. In the next chapter, we'll look at what it takes to build a culture where PIPS thinking becomes the default, not the exception.

title: The 5-Minute PIPS
summary: You will use this version more than any other. Once the PIPS framework is internalized, your brain will start running through the steps automatically whenever something doesn't work the way it should....

### The Everyday Stuff That Shouldn't Have to Be Done Every Day

The 5-Minute PIPS is especially powerful for identifying processes that are consuming time unnecessarily. Things your team does every day that they shouldn't have to do at all, or that should only happen once instead of repeatedly.

A few examples of 5-Minute PIPS thinking in action:

- "Why do we manually format this report every Friday?" Because the data export from the system comes out in the wrong format. Can we change the export template? Yes. Time saved: 45 minutes per week, 39 hours per year.

- "Why does someone walk this form to the third floor every afternoon?" Because the receiving department needs the data for their end-of-day process. Could we share it electronically? Of course. We just never set it up because the walk was "only ten minutes."

- "Why do three people review this approval before it goes out?" Because five years ago there was a big error, and management added two additional reviewers. Has there been an error since? No. Could we go back to one reviewer with a spot-check audit instead? Yes.

Each of these is a small win. But small wins compound. An organization that captures fifty small wins a year through 5-Minute PIPS thinking has freed up hundreds of hours and eliminated dozens of frustration points. More importantly, it's built a culture where people instinctively ask "why do we do it this way?" instead of accepting friction as inevitable.

### What Happens When You Keep Compounding

I want to give you a picture of what this looks like over years, not weeks.

Early in my career, I built what we called "the operator role" -- a single position responsible for all system maintenance work. Data loads, batch processing, file corrections, error handling. When we started, the operator handled about a hundred tasks a month and it took roughly four hours a day.

We had one rule: the role should always take four hours a day, regardless of volume. If it crept past four hours, that was a signal -- something needed to be automated, simplified, or eliminated. Every time the operator encountered a repetitive task, we'd run a quick 5-Minute PIPS: what's the gap? Why is this manual? What could we automate? Do it. Check that it worked.

Over several years, that role went from a hundred tasks a month to over four thousand. Still four hours a day. Same role. The compound effect of hundreds of small improvements -- each one a 5-Minute PIPS -- turned a position that could barely keep up with a hundred tasks into one that effortlessly handled forty times the volume.

There's a detail that made this work: we trained operators the hard way first. Before showing anyone the automated version of a task, we made them do it manually for weeks. Every step, the long way, no shortcuts. We called it the Mr. Miyagi approach -- wax on, wax off. It felt tedious in the moment, but when a dependency failed or something needed to be modified, the operator understood the underlying process well enough to diagnose and fix it. They weren't just button-pushers. They were problem-solvers who happened to have automation handling the routine work.

We also wrote every process so that our lowest-cost employee could execute it. Then that person would document it so an even lower-cost employee could do it -- or so it could be automated entirely. This constant push to simplify and delegate downward was the engine of scaling. It's how a team that started with a handful of people handled the workload of a department ten times its size.

The numbers tell the story. The broader team that operated with this mindset tripled their productivity over four years -- going from roughly 800 completed work items a year to over 2,600, with only 12% attributed to bugs and rework. Individual contributors went from completing about 100 items per quarter to 300. Average completion time per item dropped by a third. System uptime hit 99.999%. And the team wasn't three times larger. They were the same people, working the same hours. The difference was entirely in how they worked -- relentless small improvements, compounded over years.

At one point, all of the eligibility processing, accumulator management, and semi-automated data loads for the entire company were handled by a half-FTE operator. One person, half their day. For work that would have buried a team of four a few years earlier. That's what compounding gets you.

That's the power of 5-Minute PIPS thinking applied consistently over time. No single improvement was dramatic. No single automation saved the day. But the accumulation of hundreds of small, disciplined improvements created a capability that would have been impossible to achieve through any one-time initiative.

title: The 1-Hour PIPS
summary: This is the sweet spot for department-level problems that affect a team but don't require a full project mobilization. You gather your team, put the framework on a whiteboard, and work through Steps 1...
bodyMd:
This is the sweet spot for department-level problems that affect a team but don't require a full project mobilization. You gather your team, put the framework on a whiteboard, and work through Steps 1 through 4 in a structured hour. Implementation and evaluation happen afterward, but the thinking work gets done in a single focused session.

### The Inconsistent Onboarding Checklist

A department with eight managers and about forty new hires per year had a problem: onboarding was inconsistent. Every manager did it differently. Some were thorough -- structured first week, clear documentation, scheduled check-ins. Others handed the new hire a laptop and said, "Ask me if you have questions." The result was predictable: new hires who got a good onboarding hit productivity faster and stayed longer. Those who didn't were more likely to struggle and leave.

The department head knew it was a problem but hadn't been able to fix it because, as she put it, "every time I bring it up, it turns into a two-hour argument about whose approach is best."

Here's how the 1-Hour PIPS session ran:

**Minutes 1-5: Frame the problem.**

"Our onboarding experience varies significantly by manager. New hires report inconsistent experiences, and our time-to-productivity ranges from 3 weeks to 12 weeks depending on which team they join. We want every new hire to have a consistent, high-quality first 30 days regardless of manager."

Write the As-Is and Desired State on the whiteboard. No debate yet. Just get it on the board.

**Minutes 5-15: Quick data review.**

The department head had pulled three data points in advance: time-to-productivity by manager (based on when new hires were first assigned independent work), 90-day survey scores by manager, and first-year retention by manager. The numbers told a clear story. Two managers had significantly better outcomes. The differences weren't subtle.

This step is critical. By starting with data, you prevent the conversation from becoming "my way vs. your way." It's not about opinions. It's about what the numbers say.

**Minutes 15-30: Brainstorm the checklist.**

The group brainstormed everything that should happen in a new hire's first 30 days. They used Freewheeling -- anyone could call out an item. The scribe wrote them all on the whiteboard without evaluation. They generated 34 items in 15 minutes.

Items ranged from "set up workstation before day 1" to "meet with each peer 1-on-1 in the first week" to "review department goals and metrics" to "shadow a senior team member for one full day."

**Minutes 30-45: Filter and prioritize.**

The team reviewed each item. Some were universally agreed upon -- those went straight to the checklist. Some were nice-to-have -- those were bracketed for possible inclusion later. A few were contentious -- the team discussed briefly and either included them with modifications or bracketed them.

Key move: the department head asked the two managers with the best outcomes what they were doing that others weren't. Both independently mentioned the same three things: a structured first-week schedule sent to the new hire before day 1, a 1-on-1 with every team member in the first five days, and a weekly 15-minute check-in for the first month. Those three practices went on the checklist.

**Minutes 45-55: Build the implementation plan.**

The checklist was drafted with 22 items, organized by day/week. One manager volunteered to create the final template. Another volunteered to pilot it with the next two hires. The department head committed to reviewing results after 90 days.

**Minutes 55-60: Confirm next steps and close.**

Template draft by end of next week. Pilot starts with the next hire. 90-day review scheduled on the calendar before anyone leaves the room.

Total investment: one hour of eight people's time. The template was in use within two weeks. Within six months, time-to-productivity variation across managers dropped by 60%.

title: The Multi-Week PIPS
summary: This is the full formal process. Dedicated team, scheduled meetings, data collection periods, analysis sessions, and a management presentation. This is the level where PIPS looks most like a tradition...
bodyMd:
This is the full formal process. Dedicated team, scheduled meetings, data collection periods, analysis sessions, and a management presentation. This is the level where PIPS looks most like a traditional project, and it's the level most people think of when they hear "PIPS."

I won't walk through a complete multi-week example here because that's essentially what we did in Chapters 4 through 9 with the Meridian denial rate project. But I want to highlight the structural elements that distinguish a multi-week PIPS from a 1-Hour PIPS:

**Dedicated team.** Not people working on this "when they have time." People who are assigned to this project with allocated hours. The team is cross-functional -- it includes people who do the work, people who manage the work, and people affected by the work.

**Scheduled meeting cadence.** Weekly meetings during active phases, typically 60-90 minutes. Biweekly during data collection phases when there's not enough new information to discuss weekly. Every meeting has an agenda, a timekeeper, and a scribe.

**Formal data collection.** Not a quick look at existing reports. Deliberate measurement. If the existing data doesn't tell you what you need to know, you design a data collection effort. Check sheets, sampling plans, observation periods. The data collection phase might last 2-4 weeks on its own.

**Root cause analysis tools.** This is where you pull out the full toolkit: cause-and-effect diagrams, Pareto analysis, force field analysis, the five whys. A multi-week PIPS gives you the time to use these tools properly, not just superficially.

**Solution evaluation matrix.** Formal criteria weighting. Formal scoring. The team doesn't just pick the solution that feels right -- they evaluate each candidate against defined criteria and document the rationale.

**Management presentation.** The team presents their findings, recommendation, and implementation plan to the sponsoring leadership. This serves two purposes: it gets approval and resources for implementation, and it builds the team's credibility and visibility.

**Implementation plan with milestones.** Not "we'll roll it out." A specific plan with dates, owners, milestones, and contingencies.

**Formal evaluation period.** The team reconvenes after implementation to measure results against the original metrics. Did the gap close? By how much? Are there unintended consequences? Should the solution be adjusted?

A multi-week PIPS typically runs 8-16 weeks from problem selection to evaluation. Some run longer for particularly complex problems. The key is that every week has a purpose, and the team is progressing through the steps in order, not skipping ahead.

title: The Strategic PIPS
summary: This is the largest scale: company-wide transformations that involve multiple workstreams, executive sponsorship, and quarterly reviews. A strategic PIPS isn't one PIPS project -- it's a portfolio of ...
bodyMd:
This is the largest scale: company-wide transformations that involve multiple workstreams, executive sponsorship, and quarterly reviews. A strategic PIPS isn't one PIPS project -- it's a portfolio of PIPS projects organized around a strategic objective.

### Digital Transformation as a Strategic PIPS

A company decided it needed to "digitally transform." That phrase is so overused it's almost meaningless, but the underlying problem was real: their core business processes still ran on paper, spreadsheets, and institutional knowledge in people's heads. They were losing competitive ground to companies that had automated, integrated, and data-driven operations.

Here's how PIPS structured a transformation that might otherwise have been a vague, expensive mess:

**The Strategic As-Is:** Manual, paper-based processes in operations, finance, and customer service. Average process cycle times 3-5x industry benchmarks. Employee satisfaction surveys consistently cite "outdated tools" as a top-3 frustration.

**The Strategic Desired State:** Core processes digitized and integrated within 24 months. Cycle times within 1.5x of industry benchmarks. Employee tool satisfaction score above 70% (current: 31%).

**The Portfolio of PIPS Projects:** Instead of one massive transformation project, the leadership team identified seven specific process areas and launched a separate PIPS project for each:

1. Invoice processing (AP automation -- the Finance example from Chapter 10)
2. Customer onboarding workflow
3. Internal helpdesk and IT service delivery
4. Employee onboarding (HR)
5. Inventory management
6. Sales pipeline tracking
7. Management reporting and dashboards

Each project had its own team, its own timeline, and its own metrics. But they were coordinated at the portfolio level by an executive steering committee that met monthly to review progress, resolve resource conflicts, and adjust priorities.

Not all seven launched simultaneously. The first three started in Q1. Two more launched in Q2 as the first wave completed their analysis phases and required less intensive team participation. The final two launched in Q3.

This is PIPS at strategic scale: breaking a complex transformation into discrete, manageable problems, each tackled with the full PIPS methodology, and coordinating the portfolio so that resources are used effectively and lessons from early projects inform later ones.

The 24-month goal was largely achieved -- six of seven projects completed within the timeframe, the seventh finished three months late but delivered strong results. More importantly, the organization now had over forty people who had been through a complete PIPS cycle, understood the methodology, and could apply it to future problems. That capability was the real strategic asset.

title: The Key: Proportionality
summary: I've given you four levels, but the real message is simpler than any categorization.
bodyMd:
I've given you four levels, but the real message is simpler than any categorization.

Match the rigor to the problem.

I used to call the simplest level a "Mostly Fine Decision." Most decisions in a day don't need analysis. They need someone to make a call and move on. The same principle applies to how you build solutions: build what you need, only when you need it, and only as much as you need. Don't over-engineer. Don't gold-plate. It's never going to be perfect, so get it done and improve it later. Make the mostly fine decision and save your rigor for the problems that deserve it.

Don't bring a full PIPS team to a printer jam. You'll waste everyone's time, and people will associate PIPS with bureaucratic overkill. A 5-Minute PIPS solves the printer jam. Done.

Don't try to fix company culture in a lunch meeting. Culture is a multi-year, multi-workstream challenge that requires sustained leadership commitment, formal measurement, and deliberate investment. Give it the multi-week or strategic treatment it deserves.

The most common mistake I see is organizations that only do PIPS formally. They set up the whole apparatus -- team charter, meeting cadence, presentation schedule -- for every problem. Within six months, people are exhausted. PIPS becomes associated with overhead, and the informal, everyday thinking discipline never develops.

The second most common mistake is the opposite: organizations that only do PIPS informally. People "think in PIPS" but never document, never measure, never present findings. There's no organizational learning. Good analysis happens in someone's head and stays in someone's head. When that person leaves, the insight walks out with them.

You need both. You need the 5-Minute PIPS in everyone's head for daily problem solving. And you need the formal multi-week process for the problems that deserve it. The informal builds the culture. The formal builds the capability and the institutional memory.

title: Teaching People to Think in PIPS
summary: The best thing you can do for your organization is not to run more PIPS projects. It's to get more people thinking in PIPS terms even when they're not running a formal project.
bodyMd:
The best thing you can do for your organization is not to run more PIPS projects. It's to get more people thinking in PIPS terms even when they're not running a formal project.

How do you do that? You model it. You ask the PIPS questions in everyday conversations:

- Someone brings you a problem: "What does the current state look like? What would better look like?" (Step 1)
- Someone proposes a solution: "What data led us to this solution? Do we know the root cause, or are we treating a symptom?" (Step 2)
- Someone says there's only one option: "What else could we try? Let's generate at least two more alternatives before we decide." (Step 3)
- Someone picks an option: "What criteria are we using to evaluate these? How do they rank against each other?" (Step 4)
- Someone implements a change: "What's the plan? Who owns each piece? What does the timeline look like?" (Step 5)
- Something gets implemented: "How will we know if it worked? When will we check?" (Step 6)

You don't have to call it PIPS. You don't have to announce that you're using a methodology. You just ask the questions. Over time, people start asking them themselves. That's when the culture shifts.

I had a manager once tell me, "My team has started finishing each other's PIPS sentences. Someone will say 'we have a problem with...' and before they finish, someone else says 'what's the data?'" That's exactly what you want. When the methodology disappears into the way people naturally think, it's won.

title: A Word About Judgment
summary: I want to add something that might seem obvious but needs to be said: knowing which level of PIPS to apply is itself a skill. It's a judgment call, and you'll get better at it with practice.
bodyMd:
I want to add something that might seem obvious but needs to be said: knowing which level of PIPS to apply is itself a skill. It's a judgment call, and you'll get better at it with practice.

Some guidelines:

- If the problem affects one person or one task, start with a 5-Minute PIPS.
- If the problem affects a team or a department, a 1-Hour PIPS is usually right.
- If the problem is cross-functional, involves multiple root causes, or has been resistant to previous solutions, go multi-week.
- If the problem is strategic in nature and requires sustained investment, treat it as a strategic PIPS.

But these are guidelines, not rules. Some problems that seem small turn out to be symptoms of something systemic. You start with a 5-Minute PIPS and realize the root cause goes deeper than you expected. Great -- escalate it to a 1-Hour or multi-week. That's not failure. That's the process working.

And some problems that seem enormous turn out to have simple root causes. You charter a multi-week project, collect data, and discover that one configuration change fixes everything. Great -- make the change, document it, evaluate the results, and close the project. Don't keep it alive just because you allocated twelve weeks for it. That's waste, and waste is what we're here to eliminate.

The point is to be proportional, not to be rigid. Use the right amount of rigor for the problem in front of you, and be willing to adjust when the evidence tells you to.

title: What You Can Do Monday Morning
summary: 1. **Practice one 5-Minute PIPS this week.** Pick something small that's been annoying you or your team. Run through the six steps in your head. Don't write anything down. Don't call a meeting. Just t...
bodyMd:
1. **Practice one 5-Minute PIPS this week.** Pick something small that's been annoying you or your team. Run through the six steps in your head. Don't write anything down. Don't call a meeting. Just think through it systematically and see if the root cause is what you assumed.

2. **Schedule a 1-Hour PIPS session for one department-level problem.** Pick a real issue. Invite the right people. Write the As-Is and Desired State on a whiteboard before anyone walks in. Work through Steps 1-4 in the hour. Assign implementation and evaluation as follow-ups.

3. **Start asking PIPS questions in your regular conversations.** When someone brings you a problem, ask "What does the data say?" When someone proposes a solution, ask "What other options did we consider?" You don't need to label it. Just ask the questions.

4. **Identify one process your team does regularly that shouldn't require as much time as it takes.** Not a broken process -- a functional one that's just unnecessarily manual, slow, or redundant. That's your next 5-Minute or 1-Hour PIPS target.

---

Knowing which level of PIPS to apply -- and building the judgment to scale up or down as needed -- is a skill that grows with practice. But individual skill isn't enough. For PIPS to truly take hold, the organization itself needs to change. In the next chapter, we'll look at what it takes to build a culture where PIPS thinking becomes the default, not the exception.

title: Building Culture
summary: This might be the most important chapter in the book. PIPS will fail in your organization if you treat it as a methodology. It will succeed if you treat it as a culture.
bodyMd:
# Chapter 12: Building the Culture

This might be the most important chapter in the book. PIPS will fail in your organization if you treat it as a methodology. It will succeed if you treat it as a culture.

I've seen organizations with perfect PIPS training programs produce zero lasting improvement. I've seen organizations with sloppy technique but strong cultural commitment produce extraordinary results year after year. The methodology matters -- it provides structure and rigor. But culture is the soil. Bad soil, dead seed.

PIPS is more than a problem-solving method. It's a way of thinking about work, problems, and what it means to get better. When it's truly embedded, people don't "do PIPS" -- they just do their jobs with PIPS thinking woven into how they operate.

So how do you build that culture? Not by mandating it. You can't mandate a culture any more than you can mandate friendship. What you can do is create the conditions where the culture grows naturally, and then protect those conditions long enough for roots to take hold.

There are five conditions. Every one of them is necessary. Skip one, and the whole thing is at risk.


## Condition 1: Psychological Safety

This is the foundation. Everything else rests on it. If people don't feel safe identifying problems, PIPS is dead on arrival.

Think about what PIPS asks people to do. It asks them to look at their work and say, "This isn't working well enough." It asks them to gather data that quantifies how far current performance is from where it should be. It asks them to stand in a room and present evidence that something is broken.

Now think about what happens in many organizations when someone does that. They get blamed. Not always overtly. Sometimes it's subtle: "Well, who's been managing that process?" or "I thought you were supposed to have fixed that." Sometimes it's just a vibe -- the meeting gets tense, body language changes, and the messenger learns to keep their mouth shut next time.

The person who reports the problem is a hero, not a troublemaker. That sentence needs to be more than a platitude. It needs to be true, visibly and consistently, in every meeting, at every level of the organization.

Here's what psychological safety looks like in practice:

**When someone surfaces a problem, the first response is "Thank you."** Not "Why didn't you catch this sooner?" Not "Whose department is that in?" Just: "Thank you for bringing this to our attention. Let's look at the data."

**When data reveals poor performance, the conversation focuses on the process, not the person.** "This process is producing an 8% error rate" is productive. "Your team is producing an 8% error rate" puts someone on the defensive. The difference seems small. It's enormous.

**When a PIPS project doesn't produce the expected results, the team is recognized for the rigor of their process, not punished for the outcome.** I'll say more about this under Condition 4, but it starts here. If teams learn that a "failed" PIPS project gets them criticized, they'll stop tackling hard problems and cherry-pick easy ones where success is guaranteed. That's the opposite of what you want.

**Leaders model vulnerability.** When a senior leader says, in front of the team, "I thought the root cause was X, but the data shows it's Y, and I was wrong" -- that single moment does more for psychological safety than a hundred policy statements.

I want to be direct about something. If your organization has a blame culture -- if people get punished, formally or informally, for bringing bad news -- do not launch PIPS. Fix the blame culture first. I know that's hard to hear. I know it's not the advice you want. But launching PIPS into a blame culture is worse than not launching it at all, because people will participate reluctantly, surface only safe problems, and the resulting mediocre outcomes will be used as evidence that "process improvement doesn't work here." You'll have inoculated the organization against the very thing you're trying to do.

Start with safety. Everything follows from there.


## Condition 2: Common Language

Every community has a shared vocabulary. Doctors have theirs. Lawyers have theirs. Software engineers have theirs. These vocabularies aren't just jargon -- they're tools for thinking. When everyone shares the same terms, communication becomes faster, more precise, and less prone to misunderstanding.

PIPS gives your organization a common language for talking about problems and solutions. And that language is one of the most underappreciated benefits of the methodology.

When everyone in the organization can say "What's the As-Is state?" and "What does the data say?" without it sounding strange, you've achieved something powerful. You've given people a way to engage with problems that is structured without being bureaucratic, rigorous without being academic, and shared without being imposed.

Here's what common language looks like in practice:

**In meetings, people use PIPS terms naturally.** "Before we brainstorm solutions, let's make sure we've defined the gap clearly." "Are we in Step 2 or Step 3 right now?" "What criteria are we using to evaluate these options?" These aren't stilted references to a methodology. They're efficient shorthand for thinking clearly.

**New employees learn the vocabulary early.** Not in a two-day PIPS training course (though that has its place), but through exposure. They hear the terms used in meetings. They see them on whiteboard notes. They're introduced to the six steps in their first month, casually, as "this is just how we think about problems here."

**The vocabulary prevents some common dysfunction.** When someone says "I think we should do X" and a colleague says "What data supports that?", it's not a personal challenge. It's a PIPS-culture norm. The question is expected. It's welcome. It pushes the conversation from opinion to evidence without anyone feeling attacked.

**The vocabulary bridges departments.** One of the chronic problems in organizations is that different departments speak different languages. Finance talks about variance analysis. IT talks about incident management. HR talks about engagement scores. But when every department shares the PIPS vocabulary, they can work together on cross-functional problems without spending the first three meetings just agreeing on terms.

A note on how not to do this: don't create a glossary of 50 PIPS terms and require people to memorize them. The vocabulary should be small, intuitive, and learned through use. The core terms -- As-Is state, Desired State, root cause, criteria rating, brainstorming vs. evaluation -- are all you need. People will naturally expand their vocabulary as they participate in PIPS projects. Let it happen organically.


## Condition 3: Visible Leadership Commitment

I mentioned this in Chapter 10 when we talked about strategic PIPS, but it deserves its own section here because it's that important.

People watch what leaders do, not what leaders say. If leadership says "we believe in PIPS" but continues to make gut-feeling decisions without data, skip root cause analysis, and jump straight to solutions in their own meetings, the organization will learn the real lesson: PIPS is for the rank and file.

Visible leadership commitment means three things:

**Leaders use PIPS themselves.** When the CEO sits in a strategy meeting and says "What does the data say?" instead of "What do you think?", the culture shifts. When the VP of Operations runs a Step 2 analysis on her own department's performance instead of just demanding better numbers, the culture shifts. When the CFO applies criteria rating to evaluate budget proposals instead of funding whoever lobbied hardest, the culture shifts.

These moments don't need to be dramatic. They don't need to be announced. In fact, the most powerful moments are the quiet ones -- when a leader asks a PIPS question in a small meeting and nobody even comments on it because it's become normal. That's when you know the culture has taken hold at the top.

**Leaders allocate real resources.** Time, money, and attention. Saying "PIPS is important" while expecting teams to do it on top of their existing workload is not commitment. It's lip service. Commitment means dedicated meeting time. It means a PIPS facilitator who is trained and available. It means allowing teams to collect data during work hours, not after hours. It means funding solutions that PIPS projects recommend, even when they require investment.

One approach that worked for me: the 75/25 rule. Seventy-five percent of the team's time goes to their core work. Twenty-five percent goes to improvement -- learning new skills, building better tools, automating repetitive tasks, fixing the processes that waste everyone's time. That's not a suggestion. It's budgeted. It's on the schedule. And it sends an unmistakable signal: getting better at how we work is part of the work, not extra credit.

I've seen organizations where leadership enthusiastically launched PIPS, praised the teams that participated, and then declined to fund the solutions those teams recommended. That happened exactly twice before people stopped volunteering for PIPS teams. Why would you spend twelve weeks analyzing a problem and developing a solution if you know the solution is going to die in budget review?

**Leaders protect PIPS from being overridden.** This is the hardest one. At some point, a senior person will want to skip the process. They'll want to jump straight to a solution because they "already know what the problem is." They'll want to kill a PIPS project because it's surfacing uncomfortable truths. They'll want to implement their preferred solution without going through criteria rating because "we don't have time for all that."

When that happens, and it will, the leadership team has to hold the line. Not dogmatically -- there are genuine emergencies where speed matters more than process. But those emergencies are rare. The moment PIPS becomes optional when inconvenient, it's no longer a culture. It's a hobby.


## Condition 4: Celebrate the Process, Not Just Results

This is where PIPS gets philosophically interesting, and where it diverges most sharply from how most organizations think about improvement.

The PIPS grading system -- and I'll detail this in the appendix -- weights process quality at 67% and business impact at 33%. Two-thirds of the grade is about how well the team followed the methodology. Only one-third is about what they achieved.

This ratio is intentional. And when I explain it to executives, I usually get pushback. "We're a results-oriented organization," they'll say. "Why would we grade process over outcomes?"

Here's why.

A team that follows rigorous PIPS methodology but gets modest business results has built a capability. They've internalized the discipline. They've trained their brains to think systematically. They've created documentation that the next team can learn from. And they've demonstrated that they can be trusted with harder problems next time. The modest results from this project will be dwarfed by the cumulative results of the next five projects this team takes on, because they'll get better and better at the methodology.

A team that gets spectacular business results without following rigorous methodology got lucky. Maybe they had good intuition. Maybe they happened to pick the right solution. But they didn't build a repeatable capability. They can't teach anyone else how to do what they did, because what they did was guess correctly. And the next time they face a problem, they'll guess again, and luck runs out.

I have watched organizations celebrate the lucky guessers and ignore the rigorous thinkers. It happens all the time. The team that delivered 40% improvement by skipping root cause analysis and implementing the boss's pet solution gets the award. The team that delivered 15% improvement through meticulous analysis, data-driven decision making, and careful implementation gets a polite golf clap. Within six months, nobody is bothering with root cause analysis anymore, because the incentive structure rewards shortcuts.

You have to invert that incentive. You have to look a team in the eye and say: "Your results were modest, but your process was outstanding. You defined the problem precisely. Your data collection was rigorous. Your root cause analysis surfaced something nobody expected. Your solution evaluation was thorough. This is exactly the kind of work that will make this organization better over time. Thank you."

And you have to mean it.

This doesn't mean results don't matter. Of course they matter. The 33% weighting on business impact ensures that teams are working on real problems with real stakes, not academic exercises. But the emphasis on process quality sends a critical cultural message: how you got there matters as much as where you ended up.


## Condition 5: Make It Easy

The last condition is the most practical: remove friction. If doing PIPS feels like extra work on top of "real work," it will die. It has to feel like part of the work -- the part that makes everything else easier.

Here's what "make it easy" looks like:

**Provide templates and forms.** Don't make teams create their own problem statements, data collection sheets, cause-and-effect diagrams, or criteria rating matrices from scratch. Give them templates. Pre-built, ready to fill in. This seems trivial. It isn't. The difference between "let's start our PIPS project" and "where do we even begin?" is often a blank template that provides structure.

**Train facilitators.** Every PIPS team needs a facilitator -- someone who understands the methodology well enough to guide the team through it. This doesn't need to be an external consultant. It should be an internal person who has been through PIPS training and has facilitated at least one project. Build a bench of facilitators so that any team that wants to start a project has access to guidance.

**Allocate meeting time.** Don't make PIPS teams fight for conference room space or beg managers for permission to meet. If a PIPS project is approved, the meeting time is approved with it. Put it on the calendar. Protect it. Cancel it only for genuine emergencies, not for "something came up."

**Create a simple project intake process.** When a team wants to start a PIPS project, what do they do? If the answer involves a three-page proposal, a committee review, and a six-week approval process, you've killed PIPS before it started. Keep the intake simple. A brief problem statement, a sponsor name, and a proposed team. Someone reviews it within a week and says yes or suggests modifications. That's it.

**Share tools and examples.** Maintain a library of completed PIPS projects that new teams can reference. Not as templates to copy, but as examples of what good looks like. When a team is struggling with their cause-and-effect diagram, being able to look at three completed ones from other projects is enormously helpful.

**Celebrate publicly.** I'll say more about recognition below, but public celebration is part of making it easy. When people see their colleagues being recognized for PIPS work, participation stops feeling like an extra obligation and starts feeling like an opportunity.


## How to Start

Do not launch a "PIPS Initiative."

I know that sounds counterintuitive. You've just read about five conditions that need to be in place. You need leadership commitment, training, facilitators, templates. Surely that requires a formal launch?

No. It requires preparation. But the launch itself should be quiet.

Here's the approach I've seen work:

**Start with one team and one real problem.** Not the biggest problem in the organization. Not the safest problem either. Pick a real, meaningful problem in a department that has a manager willing to try something new. Facilitate it yourself if you need to, or bring in someone who knows the methodology.

**Get results.** Work through the six steps. Collect data. Do the analysis. Implement the solution. Measure the outcome. This might take 8-12 weeks.

**Let the story spread.** When the team presents their results -- and they should present their results, at least to their own leadership -- the story does the work. "We had this problem. We thought the cause was X. The data showed it was Y. We implemented Z. Here are the before-and-after numbers." That story is more compelling than any PowerPoint about continuous improvement philosophy.

**Do it again.** Pick another team, another problem. Maybe in a different department. Get results again. Now you have two stories.

**By the third or fourth project, people start asking how they can do it too.** That's when you're ready for a broader rollout. Not because you've mandated it from the top, but because demand has built from the bottom. The early adopters are your proof of concept. Their results are your marketing material. Their team members are your future facilitators.

This approach is slower than a big-bang launch. It takes 6-12 months to build enough organic demand for a broader rollout. But the adoption rate is dramatically higher, because people aren't being told to do something -- they're asking to do something. That's a fundamentally different starting position.

I've seen big-bang PIPS launches. They generate excitement for about six weeks. Then the reality of actually changing how people work sets in, the enthusiasm fades, and within a year, PIPS is one more entry in the graveyard of good intentions I described in the Foreword. Don't do that. Be patient. Build momentum. Let results speak.


## The PIPS Team: A Community of Practice

Once you have multiple PIPS projects running across the organization, you need a structure to coordinate them, share learning, and maintain quality. This is the PIPS team -- and I want to be careful about how I describe it, because it's not what you think.

The PIPS team is not a bureaucracy. It's not a governance committee. It's not a group of process police who audit everyone's work and enforce compliance. If it becomes any of those things, disband it immediately. Those structures kill PIPS cultures.

The PIPS team is a community of practice. It's a group of people from across the organization who share a commitment to the methodology and meet regularly to:

- **Review active PIPS projects.** Not to audit them, but to support them. Are any teams stuck? Do any teams need help with a particular tool or technique? Can one team learn from another team's experience with a similar problem?

- **Share completed projects.** When a PIPS project finishes, the team presents their story to the PIPS team. What was the problem? What did they learn? What would they do differently? These presentations are not evaluations. They're knowledge sharing.

- **Maintain the toolkit.** Are the templates working? Do they need updating? Has someone found a new tool or technique that should be added? The toolkit is a living document, and the PIPS team is its custodian.

- **Identify facilitator development needs.** Are there enough trained facilitators? Do existing facilitators need coaching on specific skills? Is there a department that wants to start a project but doesn't have facilitator access?

- **Connect the dots across the organization.** Sometimes two teams in different departments are working on related problems without knowing it. The PIPS team can spot those connections and suggest collaboration.

The PIPS team should meet monthly. Membership should rotate -- people cycle in as they complete PIPS projects and bring fresh perspectives. There should be a core of 3-4 permanent members who provide continuity, but the broader membership should be fluid.

Keep it informal. Keep it focused on learning and support. The moment it starts feeling like a committee, something has gone wrong.


## Recognition

People repeat behaviors that are recognized and rewarded. If you want PIPS to become part of your culture, you have to make PIPS work visible and celebrated.

Here are concrete recognition approaches that work:

**Share PIPS wins in all-hands meetings.** Not every win -- that would get repetitive. But quarterly, have a team present a completed project. Five minutes. The problem, the process, the results. Keep it crisp. The audience sees that PIPS produces real results and that the organization values the work.

**Create a PIPS board or channel.** Physical or digital. A place where active projects are tracked and completed projects are displayed. Some organizations use a physical board in a common area. Others use a Slack channel or Teams space. The format matters less than the consistency. Update it regularly. Celebrate completions. Show the pipeline of active work.

**Have departments present to each other.** One of the most powerful recognition moments in a PIPS culture is when a team from finance presents their project to a mixed audience that includes people from IT, HR, operations, and leadership. The cross-pollination of ideas is valuable on its own, but the recognition that comes from presenting your work to peers outside your department is motivating in a way that an email from your boss isn't.

**Recognize process quality, not just results.** This connects back to Condition 4. When you recognize a team, be specific about what they did well methodologically. "This team's data collection was exceptionally thorough" or "Their root cause analysis surfaced an insight that nobody expected" matters more than "They saved us $50,000." Both are worth mentioning. But lead with the process.

**Never, ever use PIPS recognition as a ranking.** The moment you start comparing teams to each other -- "Department A's PIPS project scored higher than Department B's" -- you've created competition where you needed collaboration. Teams will start cherry-picking easy problems to ensure good scores. They'll hide struggles instead of asking for help. Recognize each project on its own merits.


## The Living, Breathing System

I've used the phrase "living system" throughout this book. Here's what it actually looks like when PIPS is alive in an organization.

You know it's working when:

- A frontline employee identifies a problem, and their first instinct is to gather data about it rather than just complaining about it.
- A meeting stalls because people are debating opinions, and someone says "Let's step back -- what does the data tell us?" and everyone nods like that's the most obvious suggestion in the world.
- A new employee, after their first month, says "I like how this place thinks about problems. It's really structured." They might not even know the word PIPS yet. But they can feel the culture.
- A PIPS project fails to produce results, and the response from leadership is "What did we learn?" not "Who's responsible?"
- Someone from outside the organization -- a consultant, a new hire from a competitor, a board member -- comments that problem-solving here feels different from other places they've worked.
