- Teams naturally form to tackle cross-functional problems without being told to. They use the steps because they've internalized them. They document because they've seen the value of institutional memory. They evaluate results because closing the loop is just what you do.

That's a living system. Not a binder on a shelf -- an instinct. Disciplined thinking applied to every problem, big or small, without anyone having to be told to do it.


## Handling Resistance

It would be dishonest to write about building a PIPS culture without acknowledging that you will face resistance. Not might. Will. And the resistance will come from reasonable people with understandable concerns.

Here are the most common forms of resistance and how to respond:

### "We've always done it this way."

This is the most common and the most human. People have invested years in learning the current processes. Questioning those processes feels like questioning their competence.

**Response:** "You're right that this process has worked for a long time, and the people doing it are skilled. PIPS isn't a criticism of the current work -- it's a recognition that the environment has changed and the process hasn't kept up. Let's look at the data together and see if there are opportunities to make your work easier and more effective."

Reframe improvement as honoring their expertise, not undermining it. The people who do the work are the best source of insight about what's not working. Position them as the experts, because they are.

### "We tried something like this before."

This one hurts, because it's usually true. Most organizations have tried some form of process improvement before, and most of those attempts died. The scars are real.

**Response:** "Tell me what happened. I want to learn from what didn't work." Then actually listen. Understand why the previous effort failed. Was it lack of leadership support? Too much bureaucracy? No follow-through on recommendations? Each of those failures is a lesson about what to do differently this time. Address the specific failure mode, not the general cynicism.

### "I don't have time for process."

This is usually said by people who are drowning in work -- ironically, often because broken processes are creating unnecessary rework and firefighting.

**Response:** "I hear you. You're busy. Here's the thing -- PIPS is designed to give you time back, not take more of it. Let me show you a quick example." Then share a 5-Minute PIPS story where the outcome was reclaimed time. Don't start by asking for a twelve-week commitment. Start with a ten-minute conversation about one process that's wasting their time. When they see the possibility, they'll find the time.

### "This is just common sense."

My favorite. Because it reveals a fundamental misunderstanding about why organizations struggle with problems.

**Response:** "You're right -- the individual steps are common sense. Define the problem. Gather data. Find the root cause. Generate options. Pick the best one. Implement it. Check if it worked. None of that is controversial. But here's the question: if it's common sense, why isn't it common practice? When was the last time your team sat down and systematically analyzed the root cause of a recurring problem before implementing a solution?"

The gap between common sense and common practice is exactly where PIPS lives. The methodology doesn't contain any revolutionary insights. Its power is that it imposes discipline on thinking that most people know they should do but consistently skip under the pressures of daily work.

### "My department is different."

See Chapter 10. Every department says this. None of them are as different as they think. Share the relevant example from that chapter and move on.


## Making It Yours

Here's the part that separates PIPS cultures that live from PIPS cultures that die.

Every organization that successfully adopts PIPS will develop its own version of it. Not a different methodology -- the six steps are the six steps. But a different flavor. A different personality. Your organization will develop its own PIPS vocabulary beyond the standard terms. Your own favorite tools. Your own war stories. Your own examples of problems that were analyzed beautifully and solutions that changed everything.

That's not just okay. That's the goal.

A generic process improvement program dies. It sits in a binder and gathers dust. It feels imposed from outside. It doesn't connect to anything people care about.

But a PIPS culture that has YOUR problems, YOUR stories, YOUR victories, and YOUR failures in it? That LIVES. Because it's not Marc Albers's methodology anymore. It's not something from a book. It's something that happened here. It's our project that saved the quarter-end close. It's our team that figured out why onboarding wasn't working. It's our analysis that changed how leadership prioritizes initiatives.

Those stories become tribal knowledge. They get told to new employees. They get referenced in meetings. "Remember when the AP team did that PIPS project and found out the error rate was coming from manual data entry? Let's do the same kind of analysis for our process."

That's the living system. It's not mine. It's yours. I just gave you the framework. What you build with it -- the culture, the stories, the institutional capability -- that belongs to your organization. No two organizations will do PIPS exactly the same way, and that's exactly how it should be.

Let me give you an example of what this looks like in practice.

At one company I helped build, our IT operations team developed its own internal language for how we handled support tickets. We had three personas that every team member was expected to embody:

**Speedy Gonzalez:** Respond to every ticket within fifteen minutes. Not resolve -- respond. The person on the other end should never wonder if their request disappeared into a void. Acknowledgment is cheap and it builds trust.

**Erin Brockovich:** Your job isn't to close tickets. Your job is to make tickets go away. Find the root cause. Fix the upstream issue. Build self-service tools. If the same ticket comes in ten times, that's not ten tickets -- that's one process failure you haven't fixed yet.

**The Starbucks Model:** When someone submits a ticket in the wrong format or through the wrong channel, don't reject it. Route it, fix it, and then teach them how to "order" properly next time. Constant, gentle reinforcement until they learn the system. Meet people where they are, not where you wish they were.

None of that is in any PIPS manual. It's not a prescribed tool or a template. It's a culture that a team built around the principles -- close the loop on recurring issues, use data to eliminate waste, make the process better every time you touch it. They took the PIPS mindset and made it theirs. And it worked. That team scaled from handling a few hundred issues a month to thousands, without adding headcount, because they kept closing loops.

We also had a philosophy that I still carry with me: "We own the work until it stops sucking, and we own it again when it starts sucking again." The operations team would take ownership of any broken process -- fix it, stabilize it, improve it -- and then hand it back to the business unit. But the moment that process started degrading, operations pulled it back in, improved it again, and re-released it. No blame. No politics. Just a relentless cycle of ownership, improvement, and release.

That's PIPS culture. Not a poster on the wall. A way of operating.

The team I built around that culture had six traits that I hired for, developed, and reinforced constantly: **passionate curiosity** -- people who genuinely wonder why things work the way they do and can't leave a broken process alone; **entrepreneurial spirit** -- people who see gaps and fill them without waiting to be told; **a simple mindset** -- people who instinctively cut through complexity to find the straightforward path; **fearlessness** -- people willing to challenge the status quo even when it's uncomfortable; **team smarts** -- people who know how to work with others and make the whole greater than the parts; and **battle-hardened confidence** -- and that last one was earned, not hired. It came from repeatedly facing hard problems, working through them with discipline, and coming out the other side knowing they could handle whatever came next.

We tried to hire "whole-brained" people -- analytical enough to dig into data and find root causes, but creative enough to imagine solutions nobody else would think of and galvanize a team around a direction the organization didn't know it could go. That combination is what makes a PIPS culture hum. You need people who can do the rigorous analysis AND make the creative leap. If you only have the analysts, you'll define problems beautifully and never solve them. If you only have the creatives, you'll generate brilliant ideas that don't address the actual root cause. If you want people who work from a spec sheet and nothing else, they can't work in this kind of environment. You need people who own the work -- who don't just execute tasks but understand the business well enough to question whether the task should exist in the first place.

My job, through this book, is to give you enough structure to get started and enough principles to keep going. Your job is to take that structure, apply it to your real problems, and let it evolve into something that fits your organization like a glove. Not like a straitjacket. Like a glove.


## The Long Game

Building a PIPS culture is a long game. It's not a quarter. It's not a year. It's an ongoing investment in how your organization thinks, learns, and improves.

Some milestones to watch for:

**Months 1-6:** You have 1-3 completed projects. The early adopters are enthusiastic. Most of the organization is unaware or mildly curious. This is normal. Don't overhype it.

**Months 6-12:** You have 5-10 completed projects across multiple departments. People are starting to ask about PIPS without being prompted. You have 3-5 trained facilitators. The PIPS team is meeting monthly. You're ready to talk about it more broadly.

**Year 1-2:** PIPS is part of how the organization talks about problems. The common language is established. Multiple departments have their own PIPS stories. New employees are introduced to the methodology during onboarding. Leadership uses PIPS thinking in strategic discussions. Resistance has not disappeared but has diminished to isolated pockets.

**Year 2-3:** PIPS is in the organizational identity. "We're a PIPS organization" doesn't feel like a slogan -- it feels like a description. The methodology has been adapted and customized. The PIPS team functions as a mature community of practice. Results are compounding -- the problems being tackled now are more sophisticated because the organization's problem-solving muscles are stronger.

**Year 3+:** PIPS is in the water supply. New employees who come from other organizations remark on how differently problems are handled here. The phrase "What does the data say?" is unremarkable. Projects that skip root cause analysis feel incomplete, not efficient. The continuous improvement mindset has become a genuine competitive advantage.

(For a more detailed maturity model that maps these cultural milestones to specific organizational capabilities -- from Level 1: Awareness through Level 5: Evolution -- see Chapter 15.)

This timeline is not a guarantee. It's based on what I've seen in organizations that stayed committed. Some move faster. Some hit plateaus. The key variable is leadership consistency. Organizations where leadership stays committed through the inevitable dips and doubts get there. Organizations where leadership loses interest after the first wave of results plateau don't.

There is no finish line for organizational improvement. The best organizations in the world -- the Toyotas, the Virginia Masons -- have been doing continuous improvement for decades and they're still at it. The problems just get more interesting over time.

That means your organization will always have the opportunity to improve. And with PIPS in your culture, you'll have the means to do it.


## What You Can Do Monday Morning

1. **Assess your organization's psychological safety honestly.** Ask yourself: when was the last time someone surfaced a problem and was thanked for it? When was the last time someone was blamed? If blame is more recent than gratitude, start there. Have a conversation with your leadership team about how problems are received.

2. **Start using PIPS language in your own meetings this week.** Not formally. Just naturally. Ask "What's the current state?" and "What does the data say?" See how people react. If the terms feel foreign, that's useful information -- it tells you how far you have to go.

3. **Identify one leader who would make a good early PIPS champion.** Not necessarily the most senior person. The most curious person. The person who already asks good questions and cares about getting things right. Buy them a coffee and have a conversation about a problem they're wrestling with. Walk them through PIPS thinking informally.

4. **Pick your first PIPS project.** One team, one real problem, one willing manager. Don't overthink it. The goal of the first project is not to save the company. It's to prove the methodology works in your environment and create a story worth telling.

5. **Write down three objections you expect to hear about PIPS in your organization.** For each one, draft a response that acknowledges the concern and addresses it specifically. You'll use these sooner than you think.

title: Condition 1: Psychological Safety
summary: This is the foundation. Everything else rests on it. If people don't feel safe identifying problems, PIPS is dead on arrival.
bodyMd:
This is the foundation. Everything else rests on it. If people don't feel safe identifying problems, PIPS is dead on arrival.

Think about what PIPS asks people to do. It asks them to look at their work and say, "This isn't working well enough." It asks them to gather data that quantifies how far current performance is from where it should be. It asks them to stand in a room and present evidence that something is broken.

Now think about what happens in many organizations when someone does that. They get blamed. Not always overtly. Sometimes it's subtle: "Well, who's been managing that process?" or "I thought you were supposed to have fixed that." Sometimes it's just a vibe -- the meeting gets tense, body language changes, and the messenger learns to keep their mouth shut next time.

The person who reports the problem is a hero, not a troublemaker. That sentence needs to be more than a platitude. It needs to be true, visibly and consistently, in every meeting, at every level of the organization.

Here's what psychological safety looks like in practice:

**When someone surfaces a problem, the first response is "Thank you."** Not "Why didn't you catch this sooner?" Not "Whose department is that in?" Just: "Thank you for bringing this to our attention. Let's look at the data."

**When data reveals poor performance, the conversation focuses on the process, not the person.** "This process is producing an 8% error rate" is productive. "Your team is producing an 8% error rate" puts someone on the defensive. The difference seems small. It's enormous.

**When a PIPS project doesn't produce the expected results, the team is recognized for the rigor of their process, not punished for the outcome.** I'll say more about this under Condition 4, but it starts here. If teams learn that a "failed" PIPS project gets them criticized, they'll stop tackling hard problems and cherry-pick easy ones where success is guaranteed. That's the opposite of what you want.

**Leaders model vulnerability.** When a senior leader says, in front of the team, "I thought the root cause was X, but the data shows it's Y, and I was wrong" -- that single moment does more for psychological safety than a hundred policy statements.

I want to be direct about something. If your organization has a blame culture -- if people get punished, formally or informally, for bringing bad news -- do not launch PIPS. Fix the blame culture first. I know that's hard to hear. I know it's not the advice you want. But launching PIPS into a blame culture is worse than not launching it at all, because people will participate reluctantly, surface only safe problems, and the resulting mediocre outcomes will be used as evidence that "process improvement doesn't work here." You'll have inoculated the organization against the very thing you're trying to do.

Start with safety. Everything follows from there.

title: Condition 2: Common Language
summary: Every community has a shared vocabulary. Doctors have theirs. Lawyers have theirs. Software engineers have theirs. These vocabularies aren't just jargon -- they're tools for thinking. When everyone sh...
bodyMd:
Every community has a shared vocabulary. Doctors have theirs. Lawyers have theirs. Software engineers have theirs. These vocabularies aren't just jargon -- they're tools for thinking. When everyone shares the same terms, communication becomes faster, more precise, and less prone to misunderstanding.

PIPS gives your organization a common language for talking about problems and solutions. And that language is one of the most underappreciated benefits of the methodology.

When everyone in the organization can say "What's the As-Is state?" and "What does the data say?" without it sounding strange, you've achieved something powerful. You've given people a way to engage with problems that is structured without being bureaucratic, rigorous without being academic, and shared without being imposed.

Here's what common language looks like in practice:

**In meetings, people use PIPS terms naturally.** "Before we brainstorm solutions, let's make sure we've defined the gap clearly." "Are we in Step 2 or Step 3 right now?" "What criteria are we using to evaluate these options?" These aren't stilted references to a methodology. They're efficient shorthand for thinking clearly.

**New employees learn the vocabulary early.** Not in a two-day PIPS training course (though that has its place), but through exposure. They hear the terms used in meetings. They see them on whiteboard notes. They're introduced to the six steps in their first month, casually, as "this is just how we think about problems here."

**The vocabulary prevents some common dysfunction.** When someone says "I think we should do X" and a colleague says "What data supports that?", it's not a personal challenge. It's a PIPS-culture norm. The question is expected. It's welcome. It pushes the conversation from opinion to evidence without anyone feeling attacked.

**The vocabulary bridges departments.** One of the chronic problems in organizations is that different departments speak different languages. Finance talks about variance analysis. IT talks about incident management. HR talks about engagement scores. But when every department shares the PIPS vocabulary, they can work together on cross-functional problems without spending the first three meetings just agreeing on terms.

A note on how not to do this: don't create a glossary of 50 PIPS terms and require people to memorize them. The vocabulary should be small, intuitive, and learned through use. The core terms -- As-Is state, Desired State, root cause, criteria rating, brainstorming vs. evaluation -- are all you need. People will naturally expand their vocabulary as they participate in PIPS projects. Let it happen organically.

title: Condition 3: Visible Leadership Commitment
summary: I mentioned this in Chapter 10 when we talked about strategic PIPS, but it deserves its own section here because it's that important.
bodyMd:
I mentioned this in Chapter 10 when we talked about strategic PIPS, but it deserves its own section here because it's that important.

People watch what leaders do, not what leaders say. If leadership says "we believe in PIPS" but continues to make gut-feeling decisions without data, skip root cause analysis, and jump straight to solutions in their own meetings, the organization will learn the real lesson: PIPS is for the rank and file.

Visible leadership commitment means three things:

**Leaders use PIPS themselves.** When the CEO sits in a strategy meeting and says "What does the data say?" instead of "What do you think?", the culture shifts. When the VP of Operations runs a Step 2 analysis on her own department's performance instead of just demanding better numbers, the culture shifts. When the CFO applies criteria rating to evaluate budget proposals instead of funding whoever lobbied hardest, the culture shifts.

These moments don't need to be dramatic. They don't need to be announced. In fact, the most powerful moments are the quiet ones -- when a leader asks a PIPS question in a small meeting and nobody even comments on it because it's become normal. That's when you know the culture has taken hold at the top.

**Leaders allocate real resources.** Time, money, and attention. Saying "PIPS is important" while expecting teams to do it on top of their existing workload is not commitment. It's lip service. Commitment means dedicated meeting time. It means a PIPS facilitator who is trained and available. It means allowing teams to collect data during work hours, not after hours. It means funding solutions that PIPS projects recommend, even when they require investment.

One approach that worked for me: the 75/25 rule. Seventy-five percent of the team's time goes to their core work. Twenty-five percent goes to improvement -- learning new skills, building better tools, automating repetitive tasks, fixing the processes that waste everyone's time. That's not a suggestion. It's budgeted. It's on the schedule. And it sends an unmistakable signal: getting better at how we work is part of the work, not extra credit.

I've seen organizations where leadership enthusiastically launched PIPS, praised the teams that participated, and then declined to fund the solutions those teams recommended. That happened exactly twice before people stopped volunteering for PIPS teams. Why would you spend twelve weeks analyzing a problem and developing a solution if you know the solution is going to die in budget review?

**Leaders protect PIPS from being overridden.** This is the hardest one. At some point, a senior person will want to skip the process. They'll want to jump straight to a solution because they "already know what the problem is." They'll want to kill a PIPS project because it's surfacing uncomfortable truths. They'll want to implement their preferred solution without going through criteria rating because "we don't have time for all that."

When that happens, and it will, the leadership team has to hold the line. Not dogmatically -- there are genuine emergencies where speed matters more than process. But those emergencies are rare. The moment PIPS becomes optional when inconvenient, it's no longer a culture. It's a hobby.

title: Condition 4: Celebrate the Process, Not Just Results
summary: This is where PIPS gets philosophically interesting, and where it diverges most sharply from how most organizations think about improvement.
bodyMd:
This is where PIPS gets philosophically interesting, and where it diverges most sharply from how most organizations think about improvement.

The PIPS grading system -- and I'll detail this in the appendix -- weights process quality at 67% and business impact at 33%. Two-thirds of the grade is about how well the team followed the methodology. Only one-third is about what they achieved.

This ratio is intentional. And when I explain it to executives, I usually get pushback. "We're a results-oriented organization," they'll say. "Why would we grade process over outcomes?"

Here's why.

A team that follows rigorous PIPS methodology but gets modest business results has built a capability. They've internalized the discipline. They've trained their brains to think systematically. They've created documentation that the next team can learn from. And they've demonstrated that they can be trusted with harder problems next time. The modest results from this project will be dwarfed by the cumulative results of the next five projects this team takes on, because they'll get better and better at the methodology.

A team that gets spectacular business results without following rigorous methodology got lucky. Maybe they had good intuition. Maybe they happened to pick the right solution. But they didn't build a repeatable capability. They can't teach anyone else how to do what they did, because what they did was guess correctly. And the next time they face a problem, they'll guess again, and luck runs out.

I have watched organizations celebrate the lucky guessers and ignore the rigorous thinkers. It happens all the time. The team that delivered 40% improvement by skipping root cause analysis and implementing the boss's pet solution gets the award. The team that delivered 15% improvement through meticulous analysis, data-driven decision making, and careful implementation gets a polite golf clap. Within six months, nobody is bothering with root cause analysis anymore, because the incentive structure rewards shortcuts.

You have to invert that incentive. You have to look a team in the eye and say: "Your results were modest, but your process was outstanding. You defined the problem precisely. Your data collection was rigorous. Your root cause analysis surfaced something nobody expected. Your solution evaluation was thorough. This is exactly the kind of work that will make this organization better over time. Thank you."

And you have to mean it.

This doesn't mean results don't matter. Of course they matter. The 33% weighting on business impact ensures that teams are working on real problems with real stakes, not academic exercises. But the emphasis on process quality sends a critical cultural message: how you got there matters as much as where you ended up.

title: Condition 5: Make It Easy
summary: The last condition is the most practical: remove friction. If doing PIPS feels like extra work on top of "real work," it will die. It has to feel like part of the work -- the part that makes everythin...
bodyMd:
The last condition is the most practical: remove friction. If doing PIPS feels like extra work on top of "real work," it will die. It has to feel like part of the work -- the part that makes everything else easier.

Here's what "make it easy" looks like:

**Provide templates and forms.** Don't make teams create their own problem statements, data collection sheets, cause-and-effect diagrams, or criteria rating matrices from scratch. Give them templates. Pre-built, ready to fill in. This seems trivial. It isn't. The difference between "let's start our PIPS project" and "where do we even begin?" is often a blank template that provides structure.

**Train facilitators.** Every PIPS team needs a facilitator -- someone who understands the methodology well enough to guide the team through it. This doesn't need to be an external consultant. It should be an internal person who has been through PIPS training and has facilitated at least one project. Build a bench of facilitators so that any team that wants to start a project has access to guidance.

**Allocate meeting time.** Don't make PIPS teams fight for conference room space or beg managers for permission to meet. If a PIPS project is approved, the meeting time is approved with it. Put it on the calendar. Protect it. Cancel it only for genuine emergencies, not for "something came up."

**Create a simple project intake process.** When a team wants to start a PIPS project, what do they do? If the answer involves a three-page proposal, a committee review, and a six-week approval process, you've killed PIPS before it started. Keep the intake simple. A brief problem statement, a sponsor name, and a proposed team. Someone reviews it within a week and says yes or suggests modifications. That's it.

**Share tools and examples.** Maintain a library of completed PIPS projects that new teams can reference. Not as templates to copy, but as examples of what good looks like. When a team is struggling with their cause-and-effect diagram, being able to look at three completed ones from other projects is enormously helpful.

**Celebrate publicly.** I'll say more about recognition below, but public celebration is part of making it easy. When people see their colleagues being recognized for PIPS work, participation stops feeling like an extra obligation and starts feeling like an opportunity.

title: How to Start
summary: Do not launch a "PIPS Initiative."
bodyMd:
Do not launch a "PIPS Initiative."

I know that sounds counterintuitive. You've just read about five conditions that need to be in place. You need leadership commitment, training, facilitators, templates. Surely that requires a formal launch?

No. It requires preparation. But the launch itself should be quiet.

Here's the approach I've seen work:

**Start with one team and one real problem.** Not the biggest problem in the organization. Not the safest problem either. Pick a real, meaningful problem in a department that has a manager willing to try something new. Facilitate it yourself if you need to, or bring in someone who knows the methodology.

**Get results.** Work through the six steps. Collect data. Do the analysis. Implement the solution. Measure the outcome. This might take 8-12 weeks.

**Let the story spread.** When the team presents their results -- and they should present their results, at least to their own leadership -- the story does the work. "We had this problem. We thought the cause was X. The data showed it was Y. We implemented Z. Here are the before-and-after numbers." That story is more compelling than any PowerPoint about continuous improvement philosophy.

**Do it again.** Pick another team, another problem. Maybe in a different department. Get results again. Now you have two stories.

**By the third or fourth project, people start asking how they can do it too.** That's when you're ready for a broader rollout. Not because you've mandated it from the top, but because demand has built from the bottom. The early adopters are your proof of concept. Their results are your marketing material. Their team members are your future facilitators.

This approach is slower than a big-bang launch. It takes 6-12 months to build enough organic demand for a broader rollout. But the adoption rate is dramatically higher, because people aren't being told to do something -- they're asking to do something. That's a fundamentally different starting position.

I've seen big-bang PIPS launches. They generate excitement for about six weeks. Then the reality of actually changing how people work sets in, the enthusiasm fades, and within a year, PIPS is one more entry in the graveyard of good intentions I described in the Foreword. Don't do that. Be patient. Build momentum. Let results speak.

title: The PIPS Team: A Community of Practice
summary: Once you have multiple PIPS projects running across the organization, you need a structure to coordinate them, share learning, and maintain quality. This is the PIPS team -- and I want to be careful a...
bodyMd:
Once you have multiple PIPS projects running across the organization, you need a structure to coordinate them, share learning, and maintain quality. This is the PIPS team -- and I want to be careful about how I describe it, because it's not what you think.

The PIPS team is not a bureaucracy. It's not a governance committee. It's not a group of process police who audit everyone's work and enforce compliance. If it becomes any of those things, disband it immediately. Those structures kill PIPS cultures.

The PIPS team is a community of practice. It's a group of people from across the organization who share a commitment to the methodology and meet regularly to:

- **Review active PIPS projects.** Not to audit them, but to support them. Are any teams stuck? Do any teams need help with a particular tool or technique? Can one team learn from another team's experience with a similar problem?

- **Share completed projects.** When a PIPS project finishes, the team presents their story to the PIPS team. What was the problem? What did they learn? What would they do differently? These presentations are not evaluations. They're knowledge sharing.

- **Maintain the toolkit.** Are the templates working? Do they need updating? Has someone found a new tool or technique that should be added? The toolkit is a living document, and the PIPS team is its custodian.

- **Identify facilitator development needs.** Are there enough trained facilitators? Do existing facilitators need coaching on specific skills? Is there a department that wants to start a project but doesn't have facilitator access?

- **Connect the dots across the organization.** Sometimes two teams in different departments are working on related problems without knowing it. The PIPS team can spot those connections and suggest collaboration.

The PIPS team should meet monthly. Membership should rotate -- people cycle in as they complete PIPS projects and bring fresh perspectives. There should be a core of 3-4 permanent members who provide continuity, but the broader membership should be fluid.

Keep it informal. Keep it focused on learning and support. The moment it starts feeling like a committee, something has gone wrong.

title: Recognition
summary: People repeat behaviors that are recognized and rewarded. If you want PIPS to become part of your culture, you have to make PIPS work visible and celebrated.
bodyMd:
People repeat behaviors that are recognized and rewarded. If you want PIPS to become part of your culture, you have to make PIPS work visible and celebrated.

Here are concrete recognition approaches that work:

**Share PIPS wins in all-hands meetings.** Not every win -- that would get repetitive. But quarterly, have a team present a completed project. Five minutes. The problem, the process, the results. Keep it crisp. The audience sees that PIPS produces real results and that the organization values the work.

**Create a PIPS board or channel.** Physical or digital. A place where active projects are tracked and completed projects are displayed. Some organizations use a physical board in a common area. Others use a Slack channel or Teams space. The format matters less than the consistency. Update it regularly. Celebrate completions. Show the pipeline of active work.

**Have departments present to each other.** One of the most powerful recognition moments in a PIPS culture is when a team from finance presents their project to a mixed audience that includes people from IT, HR, operations, and leadership. The cross-pollination of ideas is valuable on its own, but the recognition that comes from presenting your work to peers outside your department is motivating in a way that an email from your boss isn't.

**Recognize process quality, not just results.** This connects back to Condition 4. When you recognize a team, be specific about what they did well methodologically. "This team's data collection was exceptionally thorough" or "Their root cause analysis surfaced an insight that nobody expected" matters more than "They saved us $50,000." Both are worth mentioning. But lead with the process.

**Never, ever use PIPS recognition as a ranking.** The moment you start comparing teams to each other -- "Department A's PIPS project scored higher than Department B's" -- you've created competition where you needed collaboration. Teams will start cherry-picking easy problems to ensure good scores. They'll hide struggles instead of asking for help. Recognize each project on its own merits.

title: The Living, Breathing System
summary: I've used the phrase "living system" throughout this book. Here's what it actually looks like when PIPS is alive in an organization.
bodyMd:
I've used the phrase "living system" throughout this book. Here's what it actually looks like when PIPS is alive in an organization.

You know it's working when:

- A frontline employee identifies a problem, and their first instinct is to gather data about it rather than just complaining about it.
- A meeting stalls because people are debating opinions, and someone says "Let's step back -- what does the data tell us?" and everyone nods like that's the most obvious suggestion in the world.
- A new employee, after their first month, says "I like how this place thinks about problems. It's really structured." They might not even know the word PIPS yet. But they can feel the culture.
- A PIPS project fails to produce results, and the response from leadership is "What did we learn?" not "Who's responsible?"
- Someone from outside the organization -- a consultant, a new hire from a competitor, a board member -- comments that problem-solving here feels different from other places they've worked.
- Teams naturally form to tackle cross-functional problems without being told to. They use the steps because they've internalized them. They document because they've seen the value of institutional memory. They evaluate results because closing the loop is just what you do.

That's a living system. Not a binder on a shelf -- an instinct. Disciplined thinking applied to every problem, big or small, without anyone having to be told to do it.

title: Handling Resistance
summary: It would be dishonest to write about building a PIPS culture without acknowledging that you will face resistance. Not might. Will. And the resistance will come from reasonable people with understandab...
bodyMd:
It would be dishonest to write about building a PIPS culture without acknowledging that you will face resistance. Not might. Will. And the resistance will come from reasonable people with understandable concerns.

Here are the most common forms of resistance and how to respond:

### "We've always done it this way."

This is the most common and the most human. People have invested years in learning the current processes. Questioning those processes feels like questioning their competence.

**Response:** "You're right that this process has worked for a long time, and the people doing it are skilled. PIPS isn't a criticism of the current work -- it's a recognition that the environment has changed and the process hasn't kept up. Let's look at the data together and see if there are opportunities to make your work easier and more effective."

Reframe improvement as honoring their expertise, not undermining it. The people who do the work are the best source of insight about what's not working. Position them as the experts, because they are.

### "We tried something like this before."

This one hurts, because it's usually true. Most organizations have tried some form of process improvement before, and most of those attempts died. The scars are real.

**Response:** "Tell me what happened. I want to learn from what didn't work." Then actually listen. Understand why the previous effort failed. Was it lack of leadership support? Too much bureaucracy? No follow-through on recommendations? Each of those failures is a lesson about what to do differently this time. Address the specific failure mode, not the general cynicism.

### "I don't have time for process."

This is usually said by people who are drowning in work -- ironically, often because broken processes are creating unnecessary rework and firefighting.

**Response:** "I hear you. You're busy. Here's the thing -- PIPS is designed to give you time back, not take more of it. Let me show you a quick example." Then share a 5-Minute PIPS story where the outcome was reclaimed time. Don't start by asking for a twelve-week commitment. Start with a ten-minute conversation about one process that's wasting their time. When they see the possibility, they'll find the time.

### "This is just common sense."

My favorite. Because it reveals a fundamental misunderstanding about why organizations struggle with problems.

**Response:** "You're right -- the individual steps are common sense. Define the problem. Gather data. Find the root cause. Generate options. Pick the best one. Implement it. Check if it worked. None of that is controversial. But here's the question: if it's common sense, why isn't it common practice? When was the last time your team sat down and systematically analyzed the root cause of a recurring problem before implementing a solution?"

The gap between common sense and common practice is exactly where PIPS lives. The methodology doesn't contain any revolutionary insights. Its power is that it imposes discipline on thinking that most people know they should do but consistently skip under the pressures of daily work.

### "My department is different."

See Chapter 10. Every department says this. None of them are as different as they think. Share the relevant example from that chapter and move on.

title: Making It Yours
summary: Here's the part that separates PIPS cultures that live from PIPS cultures that die.
bodyMd:
Here's the part that separates PIPS cultures that live from PIPS cultures that die.

Every organization that successfully adopts PIPS will develop its own version of it. Not a different methodology -- the six steps are the six steps. But a different flavor. A different personality. Your organization will develop its own PIPS vocabulary beyond the standard terms. Your own favorite tools. Your own war stories. Your own examples of problems that were analyzed beautifully and solutions that changed everything.

That's not just okay. That's the goal.

A generic process improvement program dies. It sits in a binder and gathers dust. It feels imposed from outside. It doesn't connect to anything people care about.

But a PIPS culture that has YOUR problems, YOUR stories, YOUR victories, and YOUR failures in it? That LIVES. Because it's not Marc Albers's methodology anymore. It's not something from a book. It's something that happened here. It's our project that saved the quarter-end close. It's our team that figured out why onboarding wasn't working. It's our analysis that changed how leadership prioritizes initiatives.

Those stories become tribal knowledge. They get told to new employees. They get referenced in meetings. "Remember when the AP team did that PIPS project and found out the error rate was coming from manual data entry? Let's do the same kind of analysis for our process."

That's the living system. It's not mine. It's yours. I just gave you the framework. What you build with it -- the culture, the stories, the institutional capability -- that belongs to your organization. No two organizations will do PIPS exactly the same way, and that's exactly how it should be.

Let me give you an example of what this looks like in practice.

At one company I helped build, our IT operations team developed its own internal language for how we handled support tickets. We had three personas that every team member was expected to embody:

**Speedy Gonzalez:** Respond to every ticket within fifteen minutes. Not resolve -- respond. The person on the other end should never wonder if their request disappeared into a void. Acknowledgment is cheap and it builds trust.

**Erin Brockovich:** Your job isn't to close tickets. Your job is to make tickets go away. Find the root cause. Fix the upstream issue. Build self-service tools. If the same ticket comes in ten times, that's not ten tickets -- that's one process failure you haven't fixed yet.

**The Starbucks Model:** When someone submits a ticket in the wrong format or through the wrong channel, don't reject it. Route it, fix it, and then teach them how to "order" properly next time. Constant, gentle reinforcement until they learn the system. Meet people where they are, not where you wish they were.

None of that is in any PIPS manual. It's not a prescribed tool or a template. It's a culture that a team built around the principles -- close the loop on recurring issues, use data to eliminate waste, make the process better every time you touch it. They took the PIPS mindset and made it theirs. And it worked. That team scaled from handling a few hundred issues a month to thousands, without adding headcount, because they kept closing loops.

We also had a philosophy that I still carry with me: "We own the work until it stops sucking, and we own it again when it starts sucking again." The operations team would take ownership of any broken process -- fix it, stabilize it, improve it -- and then hand it back to the business unit. But the moment that process started degrading, operations pulled it back in, improved it again, and re-released it. No blame. No politics. Just a relentless cycle of ownership, improvement, and release.

That's PIPS culture. Not a poster on the wall. A way of operating.

The team I built around that culture had six traits that I hired for, developed, and reinforced constantly: **passionate curiosity** -- people who genuinely wonder why things work the way they do and can't leave a broken process alone; **entrepreneurial spirit** -- people who see gaps and fill them without waiting to be told; **a simple mindset** -- people who instinctively cut through complexity to find the straightforward path; **fearlessness** -- people willing to challenge the status quo even when it's uncomfortable; **team smarts** -- people who know how to work with others and make the whole greater than the parts; and **battle-hardened confidence** -- and that last one was earned, not hired. It came from repeatedly facing hard problems, working through them with discipline, and coming out the other side knowing they could handle whatever came next.

We tried to hire "whole-brained" people -- analytical enough to dig into data and find root causes, but creative enough to imagine solutions nobody else would think of and galvanize a team around a direction the organization didn't know it could go. That combination is what makes a PIPS culture hum. You need people who can do the rigorous analysis AND make the creative leap. If you only have the analysts, you'll define problems beautifully and never solve them. If you only have the creatives, you'll generate brilliant ideas that don't address the actual root cause. If you want people who work from a spec sheet and nothing else, they can't work in this kind of environment. You need people who own the work -- who don't just execute tasks but understand the business well enough to question whether the task should exist in the first place.

My job, through this book, is to give you enough structure to get started and enough principles to keep going. Your job is to take that structure, apply it to your real problems, and let it evolve into something that fits your organization like a glove. Not like a straitjacket. Like a glove.

title: The Long Game
summary: Building a PIPS culture is a long game. It's not a quarter. It's not a year. It's an ongoing investment in how your organization thinks, learns, and improves.
bodyMd:
Building a PIPS culture is a long game. It's not a quarter. It's not a year. It's an ongoing investment in how your organization thinks, learns, and improves.

Some milestones to watch for:

**Months 1-6:** You have 1-3 completed projects. The early adopters are enthusiastic. Most of the organization is unaware or mildly curious. This is normal. Don't overhype it.

**Months 6-12:** You have 5-10 completed projects across multiple departments. People are starting to ask about PIPS without being prompted. You have 3-5 trained facilitators. The PIPS team is meeting monthly. You're ready to talk about it more broadly.

**Year 1-2:** PIPS is part of how the organization talks about problems. The common language is established. Multiple departments have their own PIPS stories. New employees are introduced to the methodology during onboarding. Leadership uses PIPS thinking in strategic discussions. Resistance has not disappeared but has diminished to isolated pockets.

**Year 2-3:** PIPS is in the organizational identity. "We're a PIPS organization" doesn't feel like a slogan -- it feels like a description. The methodology has been adapted and customized. The PIPS team functions as a mature community of practice. Results are compounding -- the problems being tackled now are more sophisticated because the organization's problem-solving muscles are stronger.

**Year 3+:** PIPS is in the water supply. New employees who come from other organizations remark on how differently problems are handled here. The phrase "What does the data say?" is unremarkable. Projects that skip root cause analysis feel incomplete, not efficient. The continuous improvement mindset has become a genuine competitive advantage.

(For a more detailed maturity model that maps these cultural milestones to specific organizational capabilities -- from Level 1: Awareness through Level 5: Evolution -- see Chapter 15.)

This timeline is not a guarantee. It's based on what I've seen in organizations that stayed committed. Some move faster. Some hit plateaus. The key variable is leadership consistency. Organizations where leadership stays committed through the inevitable dips and doubts get there. Organizations where leadership loses interest after the first wave of results plateau don't.

There is no finish line for organizational improvement. The best organizations in the world -- the Toyotas, the Virginia Masons -- have been doing continuous improvement for decades and they're still at it. The problems just get more interesting over time.

That means your organization will always have the opportunity to improve. And with PIPS in your culture, you'll have the means to do it.

title: What You Can Do Monday Morning
summary: 1. **Assess your organization's psychological safety honestly.** Ask yourself: when was the last time someone surfaced a problem and was thanked for it? When was the last time someone was blamed? If b...
bodyMd:
1. **Assess your organization's psychological safety honestly.** Ask yourself: when was the last time someone surfaced a problem and was thanked for it? When was the last time someone was blamed? If blame is more recent than gratitude, start there. Have a conversation with your leadership team about how problems are received.

2. **Start using PIPS language in your own meetings this week.** Not formally. Just naturally. Ask "What's the current state?" and "What does the data say?" See how people react. If the terms feel foreign, that's useful information -- it tells you how far you have to go.

3. **Identify one leader who would make a good early PIPS champion.** Not necessarily the most senior person. The most curious person. The person who already asks good questions and cares about getting things right. Buy them a coffee and have a conversation about a problem they're wrestling with. Walk them through PIPS thinking informally.

4. **Pick your first PIPS project.** One team, one real problem, one willing manager. Don't overthink it. The goal of the first project is not to save the company. It's to prove the methodology works in your environment and create a story worth telling.

5. **Write down three objections you expect to hear about PIPS in your organization.** For each one, draft a response that acknowledges the concern and addresses it specifically. You'll use these sooner than you think.

title: Facilitator's Playbook
summary: A good PIPS project lives or dies on facilitation. You can have the right problem, the right team, the right data, and the full support of leadership, and still end up with a dysfunctional project if ...
bodyMd:
# Chapter 13: The Facilitator's Playbook

A good PIPS project lives or dies on facilitation. You can have the right problem, the right team, the right data, and the full support of leadership, and still end up with a dysfunctional project if the sessions are poorly run. Conversely, a skilled facilitator can guide a reluctant team through unfamiliar territory and produce outstanding results.

Facilitation is a skill. Like any skill, it can be learned, practiced, and improved. This chapter is your playbook -- the practical guide to running PIPS sessions that people actually want to attend. Not sessions they tolerate. Not sessions they endure while checking their phones under the table. Sessions where people are engaged, productive, and leave feeling like the hour was well spent.

I've facilitated hundreds of PIPS sessions and trained dozens of facilitators. What follows is everything I wish someone had told me before my first one.


## Team Roles

Before your first session, get clear on who's doing what. A PIPS team has six defined roles. Some can be combined in smaller teams, but each role serves a distinct purpose.

### Leader (Sponsor)

The leader is the person with the organizational authority to approve the project, allocate resources, and implement the recommended solution. They're typically a manager or director who owns the process being improved.

The leader's job is to:
- Charter the project and define the scope
- Remove organizational obstacles (getting access to data, getting people released for meetings, securing budget for implementation)
- Attend key sessions, particularly kickoff, root cause analysis review, and the final presentation
- Not dominate the discussion (this is harder than it sounds -- more on that below)

The leader does not need to attend every session. In fact, it's often better if they don't. Teams are more candid when the boss isn't in the room for every brainstorming exercise.

### Process Guide

The process guide is the person who knows the PIPS methodology best. They coach the team on the tools, keep the group oriented to which step they're on, and ensure methodological rigor.

In early PIPS projects, the process guide and the facilitator are often the same person. As your PIPS practice matures, it helps to separate them. The process guide focuses on the "what" (are we using the right tools, are we following the steps, is our data collection rigorous enough) while the facilitator focuses on the "how" (is the discussion productive, is everyone participating, are we staying on track).

### Facilitator

The facilitator guides the discussion. They don't contribute content -- they manage the process of the conversation. They ensure balanced participation, keep time, manage conflict, and protect the ground rules.

This is the hardest role and the most important one. The rest of this chapter is essentially a facilitator training manual. Here's what the facilitator does:

- Opens and closes each session
- Keeps the group focused on the current agenda item
- Ensures every voice is heard, not just the loudest ones
- Manages the pace -- speeds up when the group is spinning, slows down when they're skipping important work
- Remains neutral on content. The facilitator has no opinion on which root cause is most important or which solution is best. The moment the facilitator starts advocating for a specific outcome, they've stopped facilitating.

That last point is non-negotiable. If you are the facilitator, you do not get to have a favorite solution. If you find yourself wanting to steer the group toward a particular conclusion, you need to either set that aside or hand the facilitation role to someone else and join as a regular team member.

### Scribe

The scribe records everything. And I mean everything. The scribe's job is to capture what's said, not to interpret, summarize, or edit.

This is critical: scribes record verbatim. When someone says "I think the delay happens because the approval form goes to three people and none of them know they're supposed to act on it within a specific timeframe," the scribe writes exactly that. They don't write "approval process unclear." That's an interpretation. The full statement contains important nuance -- the number of approvers, the lack of a defined timeframe -- that gets lost in summarization.

Practical tips for scribes:
- Use a laptop or a large whiteboard/flip chart -- whatever allows the fastest capture
- If the group is brainstorming, number every idea sequentially so you can reference them later
- If you miss something, it's okay to say "Can you repeat that? I want to make sure I capture it accurately."
- After the session, clean up the notes for readability but do not change the substance. Send them to the team within 24 hours.

### Timekeeper

The timekeeper tracks time against the agenda and provides warnings. "We have five minutes left on this brainstorm." "We're ten minutes over on the analysis discussion -- facilitator, how do you want to handle that?"

The timekeeper doesn't make decisions about how to adjust -- that's the facilitator's job. The timekeeper provides information so the facilitator can make good decisions.

Simple role, but it prevents the single most common meeting dysfunction: spending 80% of the time on the first agenda item and rushing through everything else.

### Presenter

The presenter is the person who will present the team's findings to leadership at key milestones and at the final presentation. This should be chosen early, not at the last minute, because the presenter needs to understand the full narrative arc of the project.

Ideally, the presenter is a team member who communicates well and who represents the "working level" rather than management. There's something powerful about a frontline analyst presenting data-driven findings to a VP. It signals that PIPS gives everyone a voice, and it builds the presenter's professional development.


## Facilitation Techniques

These are the specific techniques that make PIPS sessions work. Some are unique to PIPS; many are general facilitation best practices applied to the PIPS context.

### Bracketing vs. Crossing Out

When the team is filtering ideas -- narrowing a brainstormed list down to the most promising options -- some ideas won't make the cut. In a typical meeting, those ideas get crossed out. A line through them. Deleted. Gone.

In PIPS, we bracket them instead. A rejected idea gets square brackets around it: [idea]. It stays visible. It stays legible. And there's a reason for that.

Bracketed ideas may come back. As the team learns more, as new data surfaces, as the root cause analysis reveals unexpected findings, an idea that seemed irrelevant in Step 3 might turn out to be exactly right in Step 4. If it was crossed out and forgotten, the team has to regenerate it. If it was bracketed, it's right there, waiting.

Bracketing also sends a psychological message to the person who contributed the idea: your contribution was valued even though it wasn't selected. That matters for participation. People who see their ideas crossed out contribute fewer ideas next time. People who see their ideas bracketed understand that the team considered them and kept them in reserve.

### The One-Breath Rule

During evaluation phases -- when the team is stating pros and cons of each option -- each pro or con must be statable in one breath. Not one sentence (some people can talk for a long time in one sentence). One breath.

This rule prevents filibusters. It forces conciseness. And it ensures that evaluation stays crisp and comparative rather than devolving into mini-speeches about why someone's preferred option is obviously the best.

The facilitator enforces this gently but firmly. "Can you state that in one breath?" If the person can't, the idea probably needs to be broken into multiple distinct pros or cons. That's fine -- list them separately. Each gets its own one-breath statement.

### Weighted Voting

When the team needs to prioritize a list of options, weighted voting gives everyone multiple votes to distribute across the options.

The formula: each person gets votes equal to 1.5 times the number of options. If there are 10 options, each person gets 15 votes. They can distribute these votes however they want -- all 15 on one option, one vote on each of 15 options, or any distribution in between. They can vote for the same item multiple times.

The math works because it forces prioritization without forcing binary yes/no decisions. A person who feels strongly about two options and mildly about three others can vote 5-5-2-2-1. Their priorities are reflected without losing the nuance.

Practical mechanics:
- Write options on a flip chart or whiteboard, numbered
- Give each person a small piece of paper (or use a digital polling tool)
- Everyone writes their vote allocation privately
- Collect and tally
- The ranking is data, not a final decision -- the team discusses the results and reaches consensus

### Clarification vs. Evaluation

This distinction is the single most important ground rule in a brainstorming session.

**Clarification** is asking what someone means. "When you say 'redesign the form,' what specifically would change?" That's a legitimate question that helps the group understand the idea.

**Evaluation** is judging whether an idea is good or bad. "That won't work because we tried it two years ago." That's evaluation, and during brainstorming, it is not allowed.

This is harder to enforce than it sounds, because evaluation often disguises itself as clarification. "How would that work given that we don't have the budget?" is evaluation wearing a clarification costume. The facilitator's job is to catch this and redirect: "Let's capture the idea as stated and address feasibility when we move to the evaluation phase."

Why is this so important? Because evaluation during brainstorming kills creativity. When people know their ideas will be immediately challenged, they self-censor. They only offer ideas they can defend. The bold, creative, unexpected ideas -- the ones that often lead to breakthrough solutions -- never get said because the contributor anticipates pushback and decides it's not worth the hassle.

Separate idea generation from idea evaluation. Always. Without exception.


## Session Design

A well-designed session has a structure that participants can feel, even if they're not consciously aware of it. Here's the template I use for every PIPS session.

### Before the Session

- **Send the agenda 24 hours in advance.** Include the time blocks for each activity and what participants should bring or prepare (data, examples, completed pre-work).
- **Prepare materials.** Flip chart paper, markers, sticky notes, printed copies of any data being reviewed, blank templates for whatever tool the team will use in this session.
- **Set up the room.** U-shaped or circle seating (not classroom rows -- you want eye contact and conversation, not a lecture). Whiteboard accessible. Refreshments if possible (never underestimate the power of good coffee in a working session).

### Session Structure (60-90 Minutes)

**Minutes 1-2: Recap.** The facilitator spends two minutes summarizing where the team is in the overall process. "Last session, we completed our data collection for Step 2. Today, we're going to analyze that data using a cause-and-effect diagram to identify potential root causes." This grounds everyone and prevents the "wait, where are we?" confusion that derails the first fifteen minutes of too many meetings.

**Minutes 3-5: Ground rules reminder.** Especially in early sessions, remind the team of the key ground rules: one conversation at a time, clarification vs. evaluation (if brainstorming), one-breath rule (if evaluating), phones away, respect the timekeeper.

**Minutes 5-50: Core work.** This is the main activity -- the brainstorming, the data analysis, the criteria rating, whatever the step calls for. The time block depends on the activity. Some activities (brainstorming) are better in shorter bursts with breaks. Others (data analysis) benefit from sustained focus.

**Minutes 50-55: Synthesis.** The facilitator summarizes what was accomplished. "Here's what we generated today. Here's what we decided. Here are the open questions." This is not a recap of the discussion -- it's a distillation of the output. The scribe's notes capture the discussion. The synthesis captures the conclusions.

**Minutes 55-58: Next steps.** Specific. Named. Dated. "Jenna is going to pull the Q3 data by Thursday. Michael is going to schedule interviews with the three vendors by next Tuesday. Our next session is Wednesday at 2 PM and we'll be reviewing the Pareto analysis."

**Minutes 58-60: Close.** The facilitator thanks the team, asks if there are any final comments, and closes the session.

### After the Session

- **Scribe sends notes within 24 hours.** Non-negotiable. If notes aren't distributed promptly, the team's memory of the discussion fades, and the next session starts with fifteen minutes of "what did we decide last time?"
- **Facilitator checks in with the process guide.** How did the session go methodologically? Are we on track with the overall timeline? Do we need to adjust the approach for the next session?
- **Follow up on action items.** The facilitator (or a designated person) checks in on between-session assignments a day or two before the next meeting. Not to nag -- to offer help. "Hey Jenna, how's the Q3 data pull going? Do you need anything from me?"

### Session Length

Sixty to ninety minutes. No exceptions.

I know some organizations schedule two-hour or half-day working sessions. And for some activities -- like an initial brainstorming marathon or a data deep-dive -- extended time can work if you build in breaks. But as a standard session length, 60-90 minutes is the sweet spot.

Here's why: attention drops after 90 minutes. Not because people aren't interested. Because human brains have a cognitive limit for sustained focused discussion. After 90 minutes, you're getting diminishing returns. People start checking out. The quality of contributions drops. You're better off stopping at 90 minutes and picking up fresh next time than grinding through a two-hour session where the last 30 minutes produce nothing useful.

If you have more ground to cover than fits in 90 minutes, schedule two sessions with at least a 30-minute break between them. Or schedule the second session for the next day. The team will be fresher and the output will be better.


## Common Facilitation Challenges

Every facilitator faces these situations. How you handle them determines whether the session is productive or painful.

### The Dominator

Every team has one: the person who talks the most, talks the loudest, and talks the longest. They're not necessarily trying to dominate. Often they're just enthusiastic and verbal. But the effect is the same -- other voices get crowded out.

**Solution: Switch from Freewheeling to Round-Robin.**

Freewheeling brainstorming (anyone speaks whenever they have an idea) works well when participation is balanced. When it's not, switch to Round-Robin: go around the room and give each person a turn. If someone doesn't have an idea on their turn, they say "pass" and you come back to them. The dominator gets exactly as much airtime as everyone else.

You can also use time limits. "Each person gets 60 seconds to state their point." The timekeeper enforces it impartially. This works well for evaluation discussions where the dominator tends to give a five-minute speech in support of their preferred option while everyone else gets a sentence.

The key is that these techniques are structural, not personal. You're not singling out the dominator. You're using a different format that naturally balances participation. The dominator may not even realize they're being managed, which is ideal.

### The Silent Member

The opposite problem: the person who never speaks. They're in the room, they're paying attention (maybe), but they don't contribute. This could be introversion, lack of confidence, deference to seniority, or discomfort with the topic.

**Solution: Use Brainwriting or the Slip Method.**

Brainwriting replaces verbal brainstorming with written brainstorming. Everyone writes their ideas on paper or sticky notes silently for 3-5 minutes. Then the ideas are collected, read aloud (by the facilitator, not the author), and posted on the board.

The Slip Method is a variation: everyone writes one idea per slip of paper, passes the slips to the center, and the facilitator reads and groups them. Anonymity removes the participation barrier entirely.

These techniques consistently produce more ideas and more diverse ideas than verbal brainstorming, even with balanced teams. With unbalanced teams, the difference is dramatic. I've seen sessions where the quietest person in the room contributed the breakthrough idea -- on a sticky note -- that they never would have said aloud.

You can also help silent members participate by using targeted check-ins: "Sarah, you've been working with this process for three years. What are you seeing that we might be missing?" This gives them an entry point that leverages their expertise rather than demanding they compete for airtime.

### The Skeptic

The person who thinks PIPS is a waste of time, process improvement is theater, and they could solve this problem in five minutes if everyone would just listen to them.

**Solution: Give them the data analysis role.**

This is counterintuitive, but it works. The skeptic's core trait -- questioning assumptions, doubting claims, demanding evidence -- is exactly what root cause analysis needs. Channel that skepticism into rigor.

"You're right that we should be skeptical about our initial assumptions. That's exactly why Step 2 exists. Would you be willing to lead the data analysis for this project? I want someone who's going to challenge the easy answers and dig for the real root cause."

Most skeptics, when given the data analysis role, become the project's strongest advocates. Because they see the data themselves. They own the analysis. And when the data reveals something unexpected -- which it almost always does -- they can't dismiss it because they're the ones who found it.

I've converted more skeptics this way than through any argument about the value of process improvement.

### The Tangent-Taker

The person who responds to every topic with a related (or semi-related, or barely related) story, concern, or idea that pulls the group away from the agenda.

**Solution: Use a parking lot.**

A parking lot is a designated space -- a flip chart, a section of the whiteboard, a shared document -- where off-topic items are captured. When someone takes a tangent, the facilitator says: "That's an important point, and I want to make sure we don't lose it. Let me add it to the parking lot. Right now, let's stay focused on [current topic]."

The parking lot works because it acknowledges the contribution. The tangent-taker doesn't feel shut down. Their point is literally written on the wall. It just doesn't get discussed right now.

At the end of the session (or at a designated time), the facilitator reviews the parking lot. Some items will have been addressed naturally during the discussion. Some will be assigned as follow-ups. Some will be deferred to a future session. And some, honestly, will be recognized as not relevant to this project and gently set aside.

The facilitator's tone when using the parking lot matters enormously. It should be warm and genuine, not dismissive. "That's a great point -- let me capture that" is very different from "We're getting off track again."

### The Impatient Executive

The sponsor or senior leader who sits in on a session and wants to jump to solutions because they "already know what the problem is" and "we don't have time for all this analysis."

**Solution: Show the cost of skipping steps.**

This is the one situation where I break from pure facilitation and make an argument. I'll say something like:

"I understand the urgency. Let me share a quick example. At [previous organization/project], we had a team that knew the root cause was X. They were experienced, they were smart, and they were confident. They skipped the analysis and went straight to implementation. The solution cost $200,000 and the problem came back within six months. When we finally did the analysis, the root cause wasn't X -- it was Y. The correct solution cost $30,000 and it's been working for three years. The six weeks of analysis saved $170,000 and six months of wasted effort."

If you don't have your own story like this, use one from this book. The point is concrete and financial. Executives respond to dollars and time. "Root cause analysis is important" is abstract. "Skipping root cause analysis cost $170,000 last time" is specific.

You can also offer a compromise: "Let's spend one session -- just this one -- looking at the data before we commit to a solution direction. If the data confirms your hypothesis, we'll move faster. If it doesn't, we'll have saved ourselves from an expensive mistake." Most impatient executives will agree to one session. And most of the time, the data reveals something they didn't expect.


## Meeting Cadence

Not every PIPS phase requires the same meeting frequency. Here's the cadence I recommend:

**During active analysis/brainstorming/evaluation phases:** Weekly meetings. These are the high-engagement periods where the team is actively working through the PIPS steps. Weekly meetings maintain momentum and keep people connected to the project.

**During data collection phases:** Biweekly meetings. When the team is in a data collection period -- waiting for check sheets to fill up, for survey responses to come in, or for measurement periods to complete -- there's not enough new information to justify weekly meetings. Meet biweekly to check on collection progress, troubleshoot any issues, and keep the project alive in people's minds.

**During implementation:** Biweekly or as-needed. Implementation meetings focus on progress against the plan, obstacle removal, and early indicators of results. The frequency depends on the complexity and pace of implementation.

**For the PIPS team (organizational oversight):** Monthly. The community of practice meets monthly to review active projects, share completed ones, and coordinate resources. This meeting should be 60 minutes, tightly run, with a standing agenda.


## Virtual Facilitation

The techniques in this chapter were developed for in-person sessions, but the reality is that many teams work remotely or in hybrid arrangements. Here's how to adapt.

### Brainstorming

**In-person:** Sticky notes, whiteboard, verbal Freewheeling or Round-Robin.

**Virtual:** Use a digital whiteboard like Miro, Mural, or even a shared document. The Brainwriting technique actually works better virtually than in person -- everyone types simultaneously, and the facilitator can group and display ideas in real time. Set a timer for 3-5 minutes of silent typing, then review together.

### Weighted Voting

**In-person:** Paper ballots, dot stickers on flip chart options.

**Virtual:** Use a polling tool. Most video conferencing platforms have built-in polls. For more complex weighted voting, use a shared spreadsheet where each person enters their vote allocation in their own column. The totals calculate automatically. This is actually more accurate and transparent than in-person dot voting.

### Discussion and Debate

**In-person:** Natural back-and-forth, body language cues.

**Virtual:** The facilitator has to work harder. Use the "raise hand" feature to manage speaking order. Call on people by name -- "What do you think about that, David?" Use the chat for quick reactions or clarifications that don't warrant a full speaking turn. And be vigilant about the silent member problem, which is worse virtually because it's easier to hide behind a muted microphone.

### Breakout Rooms

Virtual breakout rooms are your best friend for PIPS sessions. When you need small-group work -- analyzing a subset of data, drafting criteria, discussing a specific root cause -- send groups of 3-4 into breakout rooms for 10-15 minutes, then reconvene. The intimacy of a small virtual room produces better participation than a full-team virtual call, especially for larger teams.

### General Virtual Tips

- **Cameras on.** Not because of surveillance. Because eye contact (even virtual eye contact) creates connection and accountability. It's harder to check email when your face is on screen.
- **Shorter sessions.** Virtual fatigue is real. If your in-person sessions were 90 minutes, consider 60 minutes for virtual. Or do 90 minutes with a formal 10-minute break at the midpoint.
- **Send pre-work.** Virtual sessions are less forgiving of context-setting time. If participants come in prepared, you can spend more time on productive work and less on catching up.
- **Record the session (with consent).** This helps the scribe and provides a reference for anyone who missed the meeting. But get consent first, and be aware that recording can make some people less candid.


## The Facilitator's Mindset

I want to close this chapter with something less tactical and more philosophical, because the best facilitation techniques in the world won't help if the facilitator's mindset is wrong.

The facilitator's job is to make the team successful. Not to look smart. Not to be the center of attention. Not to drive toward a particular outcome. To make the team successful.

That means:

**Your ego stays outside the room.** The team's best session is one where, afterward, they say "We did great work today" -- not "The facilitator was great today." If people remember the content and the outcomes, not the facilitation, you did your job perfectly.

**You serve the process, not your preferences.** If the data points to a root cause you didn't expect, good. If the team selects a solution you wouldn't have chosen, good. Your role is to ensure they got there through rigorous process, not to ensure they got to the "right" answer as you define it.

**You stay curious.** The best facilitators ask genuine questions. Not leading questions designed to drive a conclusion. Genuine questions. "What else might explain this pattern?" "Are there any perspectives we haven't considered?" "What would we need to see in the data to change our minds?" Curiosity is infectious. When the facilitator is genuinely curious, the team becomes genuinely curious, and that's when the best thinking happens.

**You protect the minority voice.** In any group, there's a dominant view that forms quickly. The facilitator's job is to make sure the minority view gets heard -- not because it's necessarily right, but because the best decisions are made when all perspectives are considered. "I notice we're converging quickly on this interpretation. Before we commit to it, does anyone see it differently?" That question, asked at the right moment, has saved more PIPS projects than any tool in the toolkit.

**You're comfortable with silence.** When you ask a question and nobody immediately responds, wait. Count to ten in your head. The silence feels uncomfortable, but it's productive.
